> [!NOTE] **Diagram Note (KTU 2024)**
> All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `procA`, `hseB`, `nodeC`) to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards. Node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold/italic/HTML tags — for unambiguous rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the chemical formula, molecular weight, and percentage nitrogen content of ammonium sulphate.*

**Model Answer:**
The chemical formula of ammonium sulphate is **(NH₄)₂SO₄**. Its molecular weight is **132.14 g/mol**, and it contains approximately **21% nitrogen (N)** by mass. The compound is an important nitrogenous fertilizer widely used in agriculture, particularly for sulphur-deficient soils and paddy cultivation, where it supplies both nitrogen and sulphur nutrients simultaneously. **[3 marks]**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three health hazards associated with the manufacture of ammonium sulphate.*

**Model Answer:**
Three major health hazards in ammonium sulphate manufacture are:
1. **Ammonia (NH₃) inhalation** — affects the respiratory tract, causing irritation, coughing, and pulmonary edema at high concentrations (TLV 25 ppm).
2. **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) burns** — corrosive splashes cause severe skin and eye injuries; mist inhalation damages lung tissue.
3. **Dust exposure** during granulation/drying leads to respiratory ailments such as **occupational asthma** and eye irritation. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Convention):** Module-end questions carry an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **direct neutralization process** for the manufacture of ammonium sulphate, with a neat process flow diagram. Discuss the major **process variables** affecting product quality. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The direct neutralization process reacts pure **synthetic ammonia (NH₃)** gas with **concentrated sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)** in a saturator tower. The reaction is:

$$2NH_3(g) + H_2SO_4(aq) \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4(aq) + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H = -205 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Process Steps:**
1. **Ammonia vaporization** from storage bullets and pre-heating to remove moisture.
2. **Acid preparation** — 98% H₂SO₄ is diluted to ~70–75% and cooled in acid coolers.
3. **Saturator reaction** — NH₃ and H₂SO₄ meet in a packed saturator at 60–80°C. The saturator contains a slurry of (NH₄)₂SO₄ crystals, ensuring controlled crystal growth.
4. **Slurry withdrawal** — crystals are sent to a centrifuge.
5. **Centrifugation and drying** — moisture removed by rotary dryer.
6. **Screening and bagging** — final product is graded and packed.

**Major Process Variables:**
- **Temperature (60–80°C):** Higher temperatures improve crystal size but increase NH₃ escape.
- **pH (4.5–5.5):** Lower pH (acidic) suppresses NH₃ loss but may cause corrosion.
- **Residence time in saturator:** Controls crystal size distribution.
- **Acid concentration:** 70–75% optimal; too concentrated causes localized hot spots.
- **Agitation rate:** Ensures uniform supersaturation and prevents channeling.

**(b)** Discuss the **health, safety, and environmental (HSE)** management strategies in ammonium sulphate plants, with special reference to ammonia release and sulphuric acid handling. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Health Hazards & Controls:**
- NH₃ is a toxic, irritant gas (TLV = 25 ppm, 8-h TWA). Engineering controls include **closed saturators**, **online NH₃ gas detectors** (electrochemical sensors), and **forced ventilation**.
- H₂SO₄ causes severe chemical burns. Controls: **rubber-lined pipes**, **acid-resistant PPE (aprons, face shields, gloves)**, **eyewash stations**, and **emergency showers** within 10 m of handling points.
- Dust exposure: Controlled by **enclosed conveyors**, **baghouse filters**, and **dust suppression water sprays**.

**Safety Strategies:**
- **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** for all critical lines.
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** system for confined space entry (saturator internals).
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** interlocks for over-temperature/over-pressure.
- **Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs)** on saturator and acid dilution tanks.
- **Foam-based fire suppression** for organic contaminants (relevant in caprolactam route).

**Environmental Management:**
- **Ammonia scrubbers** (water/acid absorption) reduce stack NH₃ to < 50 mg/Nm³.
- **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** with ammonia stripping and biological nitrification-denitrification for wastewater.
- **Rainwater harvesting bunds** to prevent run-off contamination.
- **ISO 14001 EMS** and **regular environmental audits**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **gypsum process** for ammonium sulphate manufacture. Derive the **theoretical nitrogen percentage** in the product and compare it with the actual commercial grade. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The gypsum process uses naturally occurring **calcium sulphate dihydrate (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** as the sulphate source, reacting it with **ammonia and carbon dioxide**:

$$CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 \downarrow + H_2O$$

**Process Steps:**
1. Gypsum is pulverized in a ball mill to fine mesh.
2. Pulverized gypsum is mixed with liquid ammonia in a reactor.
3. CO₂ is bubbled through to drive the reaction and precipitate CaCO₃.
4. The slurry is vacuum-filtered to separate CaCO₃ (by-product sent to cement industry).
5. The filtrate is evaporated and crystallized to obtain (NH₄)₂SO₄ crystals.
6. Crystals are centrifuged, dried, and bagged.

**Nitrogen Percentage Derivation:**
Molecular weight of (NH₄)₂SO₄ = 132.14 g/mol.
Mass of N in 1 mole = 2 × 14.01 = 28.02 g.

$$\%N = \frac{28.02}{132.14} \times 100 = 21.21\%$$

**Comparison with Commercial Grade:**
The theoretical nitrogen content is **21.21%**, but the commercial grade typically guarantees **20.5–21.0% N**. The slight shortfall arises from:
- Residual moisture (1–2%) absorbed during handling.
- Free acidity (residual H₂SO₄ from filtrate carryover).
- Anti-caking coating (e.g., kaolin or oil treatment).

**(b)** Discuss the **environmental impacts** of ammonium sulphate production and the **waste minimization strategies** adopted in modern fertilizer complexes. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Major Environmental Impacts:**
1. **Air emissions** — NH₃ slip from saturator vents; particulate matter (PM) from dryers and granulators; SO₂ and NOₓ from combustion systems.
2. **Water pollution** — High Total Nitrogen (TN) effluents, Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), and suspended CaCO₃ (gypsum process). Causes **eutrophication** if discharged untreated.
3. **Solid waste** — Gypsum-process CaCO₃ sludge; spent catalysts; packaging waste.
4. **Soil contamination** — Spillage during transport/storage; accidental release of acid.

**Waste Minimization Strategies:**
- **In-plant recycling** of mother liquor back to the saturator (zero liquid discharge concept).
- **Ammonia recovery units** (e.g., Net Alkali scrubbing) to capture NH₃ slip and convert back to (NH₄)₂SO₄.
- **CaCO₃ by-product utilization** as raw feed in cement manufacturing — converts waste to wealth.
- **Cooling tower blow-down recycling** with Reverse Osmosis (RO).
- **Heat recovery** from saturator exotherm to pre-heat feed streams (pinch analysis).
- **Adoption of green chemistry** — replacing H₂SO₄ with more benign alternatives where feasible.
- **Pollution Prevention (P2)** audits under Responsible Care® and ISO 14001 frameworks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024)**
> 1. **Forgetting the balanced equation** — Many students write NH₃ + H₂SO₄ without balancing, losing 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Confusing nitrogen % with sulphur %** — 21% N vs 24% S — write both clearly.
> 3. **Skipping process flow diagram** — KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** for a labeled flow diagram. A simple box-and-arrow diagram with key streams is sufficient.
> 4. **Ignoring HSE aspects** — A purely chemical answer without HSE discussion scores only 60–70% of marks in PESFT633.
> 5. **No mention of TLV/PEL values** — Always quote **TLV-TWA** for NH₃ (25 ppm) and H₂SO₄ mist (0.2 mg/m³) in HSE answers.
> 6. **Missing CaCO₃ by-product credit** in gypsum process — examiners expect you to mention its **reuse in cement industry**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Chemical formula:** (NH₄)₂SO₄; **MW:** 132.14 g/mol; **N content:** ~21%; **S content:** ~24%
- **Core reaction:** $2NH_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + \text{Heat}$
- **Heat of reaction:** ΔH = −205 kJ/mol (strongly exothermic)
- **Four industrial routes:** Direct Neutralization | Gypsum Process | Caprolactam By-Product | Coke Oven By-Product
- **Saturator conditions:** 60–80°C, pH 4.5–5.5
- **Solubility:** 76.4 g/100 mL water at 20°C
- **TLV-TWA (NH₃):** 25 ppm (ACGIH) — critical HSE number
- **TLV-TWA (H₂SO₄ mist):** 0.2 mg/m³
- **Gypsum reaction:** $CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 + H_2O$
- **CaCO₃ by-product:** Reuse in cement industry (circular economy)
- **HSE controls:** Ammonia scrubbers, acid-resistant PPE, baghouse filters, ETP, ESD systems
- **Environmental concern:** Eutrophication from nitrogenous effluents — requires biological treatment
- **Granulation advantage:** Reduces dust, improves handling, minimizes caking
- **Application:** Suitable for paddy, sugarcane, oilseeds; especially in sulphur-deficient and alkaline soils
- **Storage:** Cool, dry, ventilated area; away from oxidizers and strong alkalis

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organic Fertiliser (Composting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Organic Fertiliser (Composting) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** > *Organic fertilizers are carbon-based compounds derived from natural sources such as plant residues, animal manures, and municipal wastes, used to improve soil fertility by supplying essential nutrients and enhancing microbial activity. Composting is the controlled biological decomposition of organic matter into a stable, humus-rich end product under managed conditions of moisture, aeration, and temperature.* ### 1.1 What is an Organic Fertiliser? An **organic fertiliser** is any fertiliser derived from naturally occurring plant or animal-based materials, containing nutrients in complex organic forms. Unlike synthetic mineral fertilisers (Urea, DAP, MOP), organic fertilisers release nutrients slowly through microbial mineralisation. **Standard Classification as per Fertiliser Control Order (FCO, India):** - **Bulky organic manures** — FYM (Farmyard Manure), compost, vermicompost, green manure - **Concentrated organic manures** — Oil cakes, bone meal, fish meal, blood meal - **Biofertilisers** — Rhizobium, Azotobacter, PSB (Phosphate Solubilising Bacteria) ### 1.2 What is Composting? Composting is a **bio-oxidative process** in which heterogeneous organic substrates are decomposed and stabilised by a mixed microbial population under controlled aerobic, thermophilic conditions, producing CO₂, H₂O, mineralised nutrients, and a stable, sanitised end-product called **Humus** or **Compost**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Garden Magic"** > Imagine a heap of vegetable peels, dried leaves, and kitchen waste left in a corner of your garden. After 60–90 days, with occasional turning and sprinkling of water, the pile turns dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling. What happened? > Nature's recyclers — **bacteria and fungi** — ate up the waste, multiplied, generated heat (the pile became warm!), and converted the smelly trash into black gold for plants. That dark product is **compost**, and the process is **composting**. It is essentially *biological incineration without fire*, where microbes do the burning, and the ash is plant food. ### 1.4 Key Physical / Chemical Constants - **C:N Ratio (Optimum):** 25:1 to 30:1 (initial); < 20:1 (final mature compost) - **Moisture content:** **50–60 % by weight** (critical window) - **pH range:** 6.5 – 8.0 - **Thermophilic temperature:** **55 – 65 °C** (peak); up to **70 °C** in static piles - **Bulk density of finished compost:** 0.5 – 0.8 g/cm³ - **Maturity period:** 60 – 180 days (depends on method) - **Pathogen destruction temperature:** > **55 °C for 3 consecutive days** (USEPA / FCO standard) - **Volumetric reduction:** ~40–60 % of initial substrate ### 1.5 Why Composting is Critical for the Fertiliser Industry - Converts **agricultural waste** (~350 MT/year in India) into wealth. - Reduces dependence on imported urea/DAP. - Improves **soil organic carbon (SOC)**, which has plummeted to < **0.5 %** in Indian soils (ideal: 1.5 – 2 %). - Mitigates **methane emissions** from open waste dumping. - Forms the backbone of the **circular nutrient economy** — N-P-K loops back to soil. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Bio-Chemistry of Composting (The "Why" Behind the Process) Composting proceeds in **four distinct microbial phases**, governed by temperature: **Phase 1 — Mesophilic (Ambient, 0–3 days)** - Temperature: 20 – 40 °C - Dominant microbes: Mesophilic bacteria (*Bacillus*, *Pseudomonas*). - Easily degradable sugars, starches, and amino acids are consumed. - pH drops slightly due to organic acid production. **Phase 2 — Thermophilic (Active, 3–15 days)** - Temperature: **40 – 70 °C** - Dominant microbes: Thermophilic bacteria (*Thermus*, *Bacillus stearothermophilus*), *Actinomycetes*. - Proteins, fats, cellulose, hemicellulose broken down. - **Pathogens, weed seeds, and fly larvae are destroyed** (sanitisation). **Phase 3 — Cooling / Mesophilic Decline (15–30 days)** - Temperature falls back to 30 – 40 °C. - Mesophiles recolonise; **Actinomycetes** dominate — they create the earthy "forest-floor" smell (geosmin & 2-methylisoborneol). - Lignin degradation begins. **Phase 4 — Maturation / Curing (30–180+ days)** - Temperature: ambient. - Humification occurs — formation of stable humic and fulvic acids. - C:N ratio stabilises near 10:1. - Phytotoxins (ammonia, organic acids) dissipate. ### 2.2 Critical Process Parameters | Parameter | Optimum Range | Effect of Deviation | |---|---|---| | C:N Ratio | 25:1 – 30:1 (start) | Low → NH₃ loss; High → slow decomposition | | Moisture | 50 – 60 % | <40 % → microbial dormancy; >65 % → anaerobic, odours | | Aeration / O₂ | **> 5 – 10 %** in pile voids | Anaerobic → CH₄, H₂S, foul odours | | Temperature | 55 – 65 °C (peak) | >70 °C → kills beneficial microbes | | pH | 6.5 – 8.0 | <5.5 → fungal dominance, slow process | | Particle size | 1.25 – 5 cm | Smaller → compaction, less O₂; larger → slow surface attack | ### 2.3 Types / Methods of Composting 1. **Aerobic Composting** — Pile / Windrow / In-vessel (rapid, odourless, preferred). 2. **Anaerobic Composting** — Biogas / Methanogenic digesters. 3. **Vermicomposting** — Use of *Eisenia foetida* / *Eudrilus eugeniae* earthworms. 4. **In-vessel / Mechanical Composting** — Rotating drums, closed reactors. 5. **Static Pile / Passive Composting** — With/without forced aeration. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical Equations) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **KTU high-yield** and must be memorised verbatim. They are tested directly in Part A and Part B numerical questions. **Formula 1 — Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N)** $$C/N = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Carbon in substrate}}{\text{Mass of Nitrogen in substrate}}$$ **Formula 2 — C/N Ratio Adjustment (Mixing Two Substrates)** $$(C/N)_{mix} = \dfrac{M_1 \cdot (C/N)_1 + M_2 \cdot (C/N)_2}{M_1 + M_2}$$ where $M_1, M_2$ are the masses of the two substrates. **Formula 3 — Moisture Content (% wet basis)** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ **Formula 4 — Ammonia Volatilisation Loss** $$\% N_{loss} = k \cdot e^{-\alpha (C/N)} \quad \text{(empirical, pH dependent)}$$ **Formula 5 — Oxygen Consumption Rate (respiration index)** $$R = \dfrac{dO_2}{dt} = K \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$ where $E_a$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature. **Formula 6 — Compost Stability Index (Solvita®-type)** $$SI = \dfrac{CO_2-C}{NH_3-N} \quad (\text{ratio of respiration to ammonification})$$ **Formula 7 — Volume Reduction** $$V_{reduction}\% = \dfrac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{V_{initial}} \times 100$$ ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial scale:** 1000–5000 MT/day plants in India (e.g., Indore, Mumbai BMC plants). - **Emission control:** Aerobic composting reduces CH₄ by **>90 %** vs. landfilling. - **Carbon sequestration:** ~0.3 t CO₂-eq sequestered per ton of compost applied. - **Soil remediation:** Compost immobilises heavy metals (Pb, Cd) in contaminated soils. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Derivations (Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > These derivations are modelled directly on the type of numerical problems asked in **KTU University Exam — Part B (14 marks)** questions of the 2024 Scheme. --- #### **Derivation 1 — C/N Ratio Adjustment Using Mixing Rule** **Given:** - Substrate A (Sawdust): C/N = 400:1, Mass = $M_1$ kg - Substrate B (Sewage sludge): C/N = 8:1, Mass = $M_2$ kg - **Target C/N = 30:1** **Find:** Ratio of $M_1 : M_2$ required. **Step 1 — Write the mass-balance equation for C and N.** Let $M_1$ = mass of substrate A, $M_2$ = mass of substrate B. For carbon: $C_{total} = M_1 \cdot c_1 + M_2 \cdot c_2$ For nitrogen: $N_{total} = M_1 \cdot n_1 + M_2 \cdot n_2$ where $c_i$ and $n_i$ are the C and N fractions. **Step 2 — Express the target C/N ratio.** $$\dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{total}} = 30$$ $$\dfrac{M_1 \cdot c_1 + M_2 \cdot c_2}{M_1 \cdot n_1 + M_2 \cdot n_2} = 30$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical C/N values.** Since $(C/N)_1 = c_1/n_1 = 400$ and $(C/N)_2 = c_2/n_2 = 8$: - Let $n_1 = 1$ unit → $c_1 = 400$ units (sawdust) - Let $n_2 = 1$ unit → $c_2 = 8$ units (sludge) So, total carbon $= 400 M_1 + 8 M_2$ and total nitrogen $= M_1 + M_2$. **Step 4 — Equate to target.** $$\dfrac{400 M_1 + 8 M_2}{M_1 + M_2} = 30$$ **Step 5 — Cross-multiply.** $$400 M_1 + 8 M_2 = 30 M_1 + 30 M_2$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange terms.** $$400 M_1 - 30 M_1 = 30 M_2 - 8 M_2$$ $$370 M_1 = 22 M_2$$ **Step 7 — Simplify the ratio.** $$\dfrac{M_1}{M_2} = \dfrac{22}{370} = \dfrac{11}{185} \approx 0.0595$$ $$\boxed{M_1 : M_2 \approx 1 : 16.82}$$ **Step 8 — Mass Verification (Sanity Check).** If $M_1 = 1$ kg, $M_2 = 16.82$ kg: - C = $400(1) + 8(16.82) = 400 + 134.56 = 534.56$ units - N = $1 + 16.82 = 17.82$ units - C/N = $534.56 / 17.82 = 30.0$ ✓ (target achieved) **Conclusion:** Approximately **17 kg of sewage sludge is needed per 1 kg of sawdust** to obtain an optimum C/N of 30. --- #### **Derivation 2 — Moisture Content Calculation** **Given:** Wet compost mass = $M_{wet} = 250$ kg, oven-dried mass = $M_{dry} = 110$ kg. **Step 1 — Apply the moisture formula.** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{250 - 110}{250} \times 100 = \dfrac{140}{250} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{MC_{wb} = 56 \%}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** The compost lies within the **50 – 60 % optimum** window → biologically active. No re-watering required. --- #### **Derivation 3 — Volume Reduction During Composting** **Given:** Windrow initial volume = $50$ m³; final cured compost volume = $22$ m³. **Step 1 — Apply reduction formula.** $$V_{reduction}\% = \dfrac{V_{i} - V_f}{V_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{50 - 22}{50} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Compute.** $$\boxed{V_{reduction}\% = 56 \%}$$ **Step 3 — Mass loss inference.** Since water loss accounts for ~80 % of volume loss, the actual dry-mass mineralisation is ~10–12 %, consistent with typical aerobic composting literature values. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Microbial Phases vs. Parameters | Phase | Temp (°C) | Dominant Microbe | Substrate Consumed | End-products | |---|---|---|---|---| | Mesophilic | 20 – 40 | *Bacillus*, *Pseudomonas* | Sugars, starches | CO₂, organic acids | | Thermophilic | 40 – 70 | *Thermus*, *Actinomycetes* | Proteins, cellulose | CO₂, NH₃, heat | | Cooling | 30 – 40 | *Actinomycetes*, fungi | Lignin, hemicellulose | Humic precursors | | Curing | 20 – 30 | Mesophiles, fungi | Recalcitrants | Stable humus, fulvic acid | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow — Industrial Windrow Composting ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Organic Waste
Agricultural residue, Food waste, Manure] --> B[Pre-Processing
Shredding and Sieving] B --> C[Substrate Characterisation
CN ratio, Moisture, pH] C --> D{C/N Ratio
25 to 30 target} D -- Ratio less than 20 --> E[Add Bulking Agent
Sawdust, Straw, Dry Leaves] D -- Ratio greater than 40 --> F[Add Nitrogen Source
Urea, Poultry Manure] E --> G[Windrow Formation
Height 1.5 to 2.5 m] F --> G G --> H[Phase 1 Mesophilic
20 to 40 C, 0 to 3 days] H --> I[Phase 2 Thermophilic
55 to 65 C, 3 to 15 days] I --> J[Temperature Monitoring
Daily Probes] J --> K[Turning Schedule
Every 3 to 5 days] K --> L[Phase 3 Cooling
30 to 40 C, 15 to 30 days] L --> M[Phase 4 Curing
30 to 90 days] M --> N[Maturity Tests
CN ratio, Germination Index] N --> O[Stabilised Compost
Humus Rich End Product] O --> P[Bagging and Storage] O --> Q[Field Application] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Composting System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputStream["INPUT STREAM"] A1[Organic Waste Feedstock] A2[Bulking Agents] A3[Water and Amendments] end subgraph ReactorCore["REACTOR CORE - PILE OR VESSEL"] B1[Microbial Community
Bacteria, Fungi, Actinomycetes] B2[Heat Generation
Thermophilic Phase] B3[Gas Exchange
O2 in, CO2 out] end subgraph ControlLoop["CONTROL LOOP"] C1[Temperature Sensor] C2[Moisture Sensor] C3[Oxygen Probe] C4[PLC and SCADA] end subgraph OutputStream["OUTPUT STREAM"] D1[Finished Compost
Humus] D2[Process Water
Leachate] D3[Vent Gas
CO2, Water Vapour] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 C1 --> C4 C2 --> C4 C3 --> C4 C4 --> A3 B2 --> D1 B2 --> D2 B3 --> D3 ``` ### 4.3 Process Block — Vermicomposting Variant ```mermaid flowchart TD V1[Pre-Digested Organic Waste
Partially Composted] --> V2[Addition of Earthworms
Eisenia foetida] V2 --> V3[Mesophilic Bed
20 to 30 C] V3 --> V4[Earthworm Ingestion and Digestion
7 to 30 days] V4 --> V5[Castings Deposition
Vermicast] V5 --> V6[Harvesting and Sieving] V6 --> V7[Final Vermicompost
Nutrient Rich, 1.5 to 2 percent N] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** *Define composting. List the four microbial phases of composting with their characteristic temperature ranges.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Composting is the controlled biological decomposition of heterogeneous organic substrates by a mixed microbial population under aerobic, thermophilic conditions, producing CO₂, H₂O, mineralised nutrients, and a stable humus-like end-product. > > **Phases (2 marks):** > 1. **Mesophilic phase** — 20 – 40 °C, dominated by mesophilic bacteria, utilises simple sugars/starches. > 2. **Thermophilic phase** — 40 – 70 °C, dominated by thermophiles, decomposes proteins/cellulose, destroys pathogens. > 3. **Cooling phase** — 30 – 40 °C, mesophiles recolonise, lignin degradation begins. > 4. **Curing/Maturation phase** — ambient, humification, formation of humic/fulvic acids. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *What is the significance of the C/N ratio in composting? Why is 25:1 – 30:1 considered optimum?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Significance (1.5 marks):** C/N ratio governs microbial growth, decomposition rate, and nitrogen loss. Microbes require a C:N substrate of ~24:1 for metabolism. - **If C/N too high** (>40:1) → N becomes limiting, microbes deplete available N, composting slows. - **If C/N too low** (<20:1) → excess N is released as NH₃, leading to nutrient loss, odour, and alkalinity. - **Why 25:1 – 30:1 is optimum (1.5 marks):** This range provides 25–30 units of C for every 1 unit of N, ensuring that ~2/3 of the C is respired as CO₂ while the remaining is assimilated into microbial biomass, leaving minimal residual N for volatilisation. Microbial C/N is ~8:1, so a starting C/N of 25:1 ends at ~10:1 in mature compost — the ideal humus signature. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > In Part B, students commonly lose marks for: (a) **missing units** in final answers (always write % or °C), (b) **skipping intermediate steps** in C/N mixing problems, (c) **not stating assumptions** (e.g., "assuming dry-mass basis"), and (d) **confusing C/N ratio with N percentage**. Always re-read the question before writing the final answer. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the aerobic windrow method of composting. Discuss the role of turning, aeration, and moisture control with reference to the **C/N ratio** of the substrate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A compost facility receives **1500 kg/day** of food waste (C/N = 15:1) and **800 kg/day** of dry leaves (C/N = 60:1). Determine: **(7 marks)** - (i) The C/N ratio of the blended substrate. - (ii) Whether the blend lies in the optimum range. If not, suggest a corrective measure with calculations. **Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:** 1. **Aerobic windrow method (2 marks):** Long, triangular or trapezoidal piles (height 1.5–2.5 m, width 3–4 m) of organic substrate, periodically turned to supply O₂. Preferred for large-scale municipal/agro waste. 2. **Role of turning (2 marks):** Replenishes O₂ (>5 % in voids), redistributes moisture/heat, breaks crust, exposes fresh substrate to microbes. Frequency: every 3–5 days in active phase. 3. **Role of moisture & aeration vs C/N (2 marks):** Moisture 50–60 % enables microbial transport of nutrients; below 40 % cells dehydrate, above 65 % O₂ diffuses poorly. C/N dictates amendment strategy: low C/N (e.g., food waste 15:1) → add high-C bulking agent (sawdust 400:1) to lift ratio to 25:1. 4. **Concluding statement (1 mark):** Integrated control of C/N, moisture, aeration, and temperature ensures pathogen sanitisation, minimal NH₃ loss, and stable humus. **Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute mixed C/N using the mixing formula.** $$(C/N)_{mix} = \dfrac{M_1 (C/N)_1 + M_2 (C/N)_2}{M_1 + M_2}$$ $$(C/N)_{mix} = \dfrac{1500 \times 15 + 800 \times 60}{1500 + 800}$$ $$= \dfrac{22{,}500 + 48{,}000}{2300} = \dfrac{70{,}500}{2300}$$ $$\boxed{(C/N)_{mix} \approx 30.65 : 1}$$ **Step 2 — Compare with optimum range.** Optimum range = 25:1 – 30:1. The blend at **30.65** is just above the optimum upper limit but still **acceptable for aerobic composting** (acceptable range commonly 25:1 – 35:1). **Step 3 — If strict 30:1 target is mandated, compute corrective mass.** Let $x$ kg of dry leaves to be added to original 1500 kg food waste to achieve C/N = 30:1. $$\dfrac{1500 \times 15 + x \times 60}{1500 + x} = 30$$ $$22{,}500 + 60x = 30(1500 + x) = 45{,}000 + 30x$$ $$60x - 30x = 45{,}000 - 22{,}500$$ $$30x = 22{,}500 \implies x = 750 \text{ kg}$$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Replace 800 kg of dry leaves with **750 kg** of dry leaves and add a small quantity of water to maintain moisture 55 % → final C/N = 30:1 (perfect optimum). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Alternative 2024] **(a)** Compare **aerobic composting, anaerobic composting (biogas), and vermicomposting** with respect to oxygen requirement, end-products, time, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During composting, a **100 kg wet substrate** has a moisture content of **65 %** (wet basis). Calculate: **(7 marks)** - (i) The mass of dry solids and water in the substrate. - (ii) The amount of water (in kg) to be removed to bring moisture to **55 %** (wet basis). - (iii) The final total mass after moisture correction. **Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:** A comparative table is the most efficient way to present this answer. | Criterion | Aerobic | Anaerobic (Biogas) | Vermicomposting | |---|---|---|---| | **Oxygen** | Required (>5 %) | Absent (anaerobic) | Limited O₂, mesophilic | | **End-product** | Humus-rich compost | Biogas (CH₄+CO₂) + digestate | Vermicast (high N) | | **Time** | 60 – 90 days | 20 – 40 days retention | 30 – 60 days | | **Temperature** | Thermophilic (55–65 °C) | Mesophilic/thermophilic | Ambient (20–30 °C) | | **Application** | Soil amendment, FCO-grade compost | Energy + nutrient recovery | Organic kitchen-garden-grade | | **Pathogen kill** | Excellent (thermophilic) | Moderate | None (mesophilic) | (Mark distribution: 1 mark for the table + 3 marks for descriptive analysis + 3 marks for application discussion) **Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Use moisture content formula (wet basis).** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Solve for dry mass.** With $MC_{wb} = 65$ %, $M_{wet} = 100$ kg: $$65 = \dfrac{100 - M_{dry}}{100} \times 100 \implies M_{dry} = 100 - 65 = 35 \text{ kg}$$ Water in substrate = $100 - 35 = 65$ kg. $$\boxed{M_{dry} = 35 \text{ kg}, \quad M_{water} = 65 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate water to be removed for 55 % moisture.** At 55 % MC (wb) with 35 kg dry solids: $$55 = \dfrac{M_{water,new}}{M_{water,new} + 35} \times 100$$ Let $W$ = new water mass. $$0.55 = \dfrac{W}{W + 35} \implies 0.55W + 19.25 = W \implies 0.45W = 19.25$$ $$W = 42.78 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 4 — Water to remove.** $$W_{remove} = 65 - 42.78 = 22.22 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 5 — Final total mass.** $$M_{final} = M_{dry} + W = 35 + 42.78 = 77.78 \text{ kg}$$ $$\boxed{M_{final} \approx 77.78 \text{ kg}, \quad W_{remove} \approx 22.22 \text{ kg}}$$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bullet Revision — Must-Know for KTU Exam** > > - **Definition:** Composting = controlled aerobic bio-oxidation of organic waste into humus. > - **C/N ratio optimum:** 25:1 – 30:1 (start); < 20:1 (mature). > - **Moisture window:** **50 – 60 %** (wb) — narrow but critical. > - **O₂ in voids:** > **5 – 10 %**; below → anaerobic, CH₄, H₂S, odour. > - **Thermophilic peak:** **55 – 65 °C** for **≥ 3 days** kills pathogens (USEPA / FCO). > - **Mixing rule:** $(C/N)_{mix} = (M_1 \cdot (C/N)_1 + M_2 \cdot (C/N)_2)/(M_1 + M_2)$. > - **Four phases:** Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Cooling → Curing. > - **Maturity indicators:** C/N ≤ 20:1, earthy smell, dark brown, no phytotoxicity, germination index > 80 %. > - **Vermicompost N content:** ~1.5 – 2.0 % N (higher than FYM ~0.5 %). > - **FCO India specification:** Compost must have **min. 12 % OC, 0.8 % N, 0.4 % P₂O₅, 0.4 % K₂O**, and **C/N < 20**. > - **Volume reduction:** typically 40 – 60 % of initial. > - **Earthworm species:** *Eisenia foetida* (tiger worm) — most common in Indian vermicomposting. > - **End-product:** stable humus with humic + fulvic acids, slow-release N-P-K + micronutrients. > - **Industrial examples:** Indore composting (first Indian model), Nisargruna biogas plants (IIT-B). ---

topics.title: Bio Fertiliser - Manufacturing Process for Major Intermediates: Ammonia
Occupational Health Problems in Fertiliser Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Occupational Health Problems in Fertiliser Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Occupational Health Problems in Fertiliser Industries** refer to the spectrum of adverse physiological, chemical, biological, and psychological health effects sustained by workers employed in the manufacture, handling, storage, and transportation of chemical fertilisers (nitrogenous, phosphatic, potassic, and complex fertilisers). These problems arise from chronic or acute exposure to hazardous substances (such as **ammonia (NH₃)**, **sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)**, **nitric acid (HNO₃)**, **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)**, **urea dust**, **fluorides**, and **nitrous fumes**) and from physical agents (heat, noise, vibration, ionising radiation in catalyst plants).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Occupational health in fertiliser industries encompasses the promotion and maintenance of the highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers across all occupations, the prevention of ill-health caused by working conditions, and the protection of workers from risks resulting from factors adverse to health, as per the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and **ILO (International Labour Organization)** joint committee definition adapted by the **Factories Act, 1948 (India)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Invisible Battlefield" Analogy**
> Imagine a fertiliser plant worker as a **soldier on a battlefield where the enemy is invisible**. The "bullets" are toxic gases like ammonia or nitrogen dioxide, the "grenades" are acid splashes, the "noise artillery" is the roar of compressors, and the "slow poison" is the cumulative dust of urea or phosphates. Just as a soldier needs body armour, helmets, gas masks, and intelligence reports, an industrial worker needs **engineering controls**, **administrative protocols**, **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)**, and **medical surveillance** to survive the "battle" of an 8-hour shift — and recover for the next one.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Essentials)

| Metric | Full Form | Numerical Standard | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TLV-TWA** | Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average | **25 ppm** for NH₃ (8-hr avg) | Chronic exposure limit |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | **35 ppm** for NH₃ (15-min) | Acute peak exposure |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA) | **50 ppm** for NH₃ | Legal enforcement limit |
| **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | **300 ppm** for NH₃ | Respiratory emergency |
| **OEL** | Occupational Exposure Limit | Country-specific | National standard |
| **dB(A)** | Decibel (A-weighted) | **85 dB(A)** for 8 hrs | Noise-induced hearing loss |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**T**hreshold **S**tops **P**eople **I**nstantly" → **T**WA, **S**TEL, **P**EL, **I**DLH. Remembering this sequence answers 80% of the 3-mark questions in this module.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is a "High-Yield" KTU Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fertiliser industry is classified under **MAH (Major Accident Hazard) installations** under the **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (MSIHC Rules). Hence, occupational health carries **14-mark and 3-mark questions almost every semester** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for this paper. The expected cognitive level is **Apply / Analyse (Bloom Level 3 & 4)** as per NEP 2020.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Occupational Health Hazards in Fertiliser Plants

The theoretical framework of occupational health in fertiliser plants is structured into **five hazard domains**. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate, define, and give **at least one industry-specific example** for each.

#### 2.1.1 Chemical Hazards (Highest Weightage)
- **Toxic Gases:** Ammonia (NH₃), Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ), Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂), Hydrogen Fluoride (HF), Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S from sulphide ores), Chlorine (Cl₂).
- **Corrosive Liquids:** Sulphuric Acid, Nitric Acid, Phosphoric Acid, Ammonium Nitrate (molten), Hydrofluorosilicic Acid.
- **Particulates/Dusts:** Urea dust, Rock phosphate dust, Gypsum dust, Ammonium sulphate dust, Silica dust, Asbestos fibres (legacy plants).
- **Carcinogens:** Hexavalent Chromium [Cr(VI)] in certain catalysts, Crystalline Silica, certain nitro-aromatics.

#### 2.1.2 Physical Hazards
- **Noise:** Centrifugal compressors (NH₃ synthesis), grinding mills, prilling towers, blower fans.
- **Heat Stress:** Reformer furnaces, prilling towers, catalytic converters, hot acid sections.
- **Vibration:** Reciprocating compressors, vibrating screens, conveyor drives.
- **Radiation:** Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) of pressure vessels, X-ray fluorescence analysers in quality labs.
- **Mechanical:** Rotating shafts, conveyors, forklift operations.

#### 2.1.3 Biological Hazards
- Bioaerosols in wastewater treatment, cooling tower legionella, fungal spores in raw material godowns.

#### 2.1.4 Ergonomic Hazards
- Manual handling of 50 kg urea bags (highly prevalent in Indian fertiliser plants), repetitive motions, awkward postures in bagging plants.

#### 2.1.5 Psychosocial Hazards
- Shift work disorders, long-hour fatigue, isolated control rooms, high-pressure production targets.

### 2.2 Disease Inventory (KTU Board-Exam Table Favourite)

| System Affected | Disease / Condition | Causative Agent in Fertiliser Plant | TLV / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Respiratory** | Bronchitis, Asthma, Pneumonitis | NH₃, NO₂, SO₂, HF | 25 ppm (NH₃) |
| **Respiratory** | Pneumoconiosis, Silicosis | Silica, Phosphate rock dust | 0.05 mg/m³ (SiO₂) |
| **Skin / Mucous Membrane** | Dermatitis, Chemical burns | H₂SO₄, HNO₃, NH₃ (anhydrous) | — |
| **Eyes** | Conjunctivitis, Corneal damage | Acid mists, NH₃ vapours | — |
| **Skeletal** | Fluorosis (skeletal/dental) | Hydrogen Fluoride, F⁻ dust | 0.5 ppm (F as HF) |
| **Auditory** | Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL) | Compressors, Prilling fans | 85 dB(A) / 8 h |
| **Thermal** | Heat stroke, Heat exhaustion | Reformer, Prilling tower | WBGT < 27.5 °C |
| **Carcinogenic** | Lung cancer, Mesothelioma | Asbestos, Cr(VI), Silica | ALARP principle |
| **Renal / Systemic** | Methaemoglobinaemia | Aniline derivatives, NO₂ | — |
| **Neurological** | Neuropathy (chronic) | CS₂ (viscose rayon adjacent) | 10 ppm |
| **Reproductive** | Reduced fertility | Lead, certain phthalates | — |
| **Musculoskeletal** | Back injury, Tendinitis | Manual bag handling | NIOSH RWL ≤ 23 kg |

### 2.3 Engineering Health-Physics Formulas (The KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> These equations are tested in **Part B (14-mark) problems** under numerical sub-parts. Memorise the constants and units — that's where 60% of marks are won or lost.

$$
\text{TLV}_{TWA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot T_i}{8 \text{ hours}}
$$

Where $C_i$ is the contaminant concentration during the time period $T_i$ (in hours). This is the **time-weighted average exposure** for variable-concentration work shifts.

$$
Q = \frac{G \cdot K}{C_{TLV}} \quad \left[ \text{m}^3/\text{min} \right]
$$

This is the **dilution ventilation flow rate** for contaminant control, where $G$ is the contaminant generation rate (kg/h), $K$ is the mixing factor (typically **K = 4 to 10**, dimensionless), and $C_{TLV}$ is the threshold limit value in kg/m³. Used in designing local exhaust ventilation in prilling towers.

$$
L_{eq} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left( \frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i / 10} \right)
$$

This is the **equivalent continuous sound level (L_eq)** where $L_i$ is the sound pressure level in dB(A) for duration $t_i$, and $T$ is the reference duration (typically 8 hours). Used for noise dosimetry.

$$
\text{Dose (\%)} = 100 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{TLV_i} \quad \text{where } \frac{C_i}{TLV_i} = 1 \text{ when } C_i < TLV_i
$$

The **OSHA Noise Dose calculation**. A dose of **100%** corresponds to exposure at the PEL of 90 dB(A) for 8 hours.

$$
\text{WBGT} = 0.7 \cdot t_{nw} + 0.2 \cdot t_{g} + 0.1 \cdot t_{db}
$$

The **Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature (ISO 7243)** for heat stress, with $t_{nw}$ = natural wet bulb, $t_g$ = globe thermometer, $t_{db}$ = dry bulb (all in °C). Outdoor workload limit: WBGT ≤ **30 °C** for moderate work.

$$
T_{allowed} = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 90)/5}} \quad \text{[hours]}
$$

The **OSHA permissible exposure time** at noise level $L$ dB(A). At **95 dB(A)**, $T_{allowed} = 4$ h; at **100 dB(A)**, $T_{allowed} = 2$ h. Doubling rule: every 5 dB increase halves the allowed time.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLV-TWA** is the legal foundation for designing **shift rosters** and **respiratory protection programmes** in ammonia and urea plants.
- **Dilution ventilation formula** is used by **HSE engineers** to size exhaust fans in bagging, prilling, and acid handling sections.
- **WBGT** is mandatory in **Indian Fertiliser Plants (Rashtriya Chemicals & Fertilizers, IFFCO, NFL, KRIBHCO)** under the **Factories (Amendment) Rules, 1987** for high-heat zones.
- **L_eq and Dose** drive the **Hearing Conservation Programme (HCP)** required by **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95** and the **Indian Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Exposure Calculation

This derivation appears almost every KTU cycle as a 7-mark numerical. The student is given **multiple time blocks with varying contaminant concentrations**, and asked to compute the **8-hour TWA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical (Model Question Type):**
> A worker in a urea prilling tower has the following exposure pattern to ammonia during an 8-hour shift:
> - **Block 1:** 0 to 2 hours → 30 ppm
> - **Block 2:** 2 to 4 hours → 50 ppm
> - **Block 3:** 4 to 6 hours → 20 ppm
> - **Block 4:** 6 to 8 hours → 40 ppm
> **Compute the TWA and compare with TLV-TWA of 25 ppm.**

**Step 1 — Write the defining equation:**

$$
TWA = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot T_i}{T_{reference}}
$$

Here, the reference period $T_{reference} = 8$ hours (standard KTU convention).

**Step 2 — Identify each $C_i$ and $T_i$ pair:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 30 \text{ ppm}, \quad T_1 = 2 - 0 = 2 \text{ hours} \\
C_2 &= 50 \text{ ppm}, \quad T_2 = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ hours} \\
C_3 &= 20 \text{ ppm}, \quad T_3 = 6 - 4 = 2 \text{ hours} \\
C_4 &= 40 \text{ ppm}, \quad T_4 = 8 - 6 = 2 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the TWA equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWA &= \frac{(30)(2) + (50)(2) + (20)(2) + (40)(2)}{8} \\
TWA &= \frac{60 + 100 + 40 + 80}{8} \\
TWA &= \frac{280}{8} \\
TWA &= 35 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the TLV-TWA:**

$$
35 \text{ ppm} > 25 \text{ ppm (TLV)}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**

The exposure exceeds the TLV-TWA by **40%**. Required actions:
- Issue **air-supplied respirators (SAR)** or **SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus)** for the worker.
- Reduce the duration of high-exposure blocks (rotate worker with low-exposure task).
- Implement **engineering control** (local exhaust ventilation at prill tower hoods).
- Initiate **biological monitoring** (urinary ammonia, blood pH).

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** Always show the **comparison line** ($C_{TWA} \text{ vs. } TLV$) and the **conclusion**. Examiners specifically award 1 mark for the comparison statement.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — OSHA Noise Dose & Permissible Exposure Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical (Acoustic Exposure in Compressor House):**
> A worker is exposed to the following noise levels in a centrifugal compressor room of an ammonia plant:
> - **2 hours** at 95 dB(A)
> - **2 hours** at 88 dB(A)
> - **3 hours** at 92 dB(A)
> - **1 hour** at 80 dB(A)
> **Compute the OSHA Noise Dose (Criterion = 90 dB(A) for 8 h) and the L_eq.**

**Step 1 — Apply the OSHA Dose Formula:**

$$
\text{Dose (\%)} = 100 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

Where the **reference duration** at each level is given by the **5-dB doubling rule**:

$$
T_{ref}(L) = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 90)/5}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reference durations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ref}(95) &= \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^1} = 4 \text{ hours} \\
T_{ref}(88) &= \frac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 4 \cdot 2^{0.4} = 5.278 \text{ hours} \\
T_{ref}(92) &= \frac{8}{2^{(92-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{0.4}} = 4.757 \text{ hours} \\
T_{ref}(80) &= \frac{8}{2^{(80-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-2}} = 32 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio $C_i / T_i$ for each block:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_1}{T_1} &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.500 \\
\frac{C_2}{T_2} &= \frac{2}{5.278} = 0.379 \\
\frac{C_3}{T_3} &= \frac{3}{4.757} = 0.631 \\
\frac{C_4}{T_4} &= \frac{1}{32} = 0.031
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and convert to percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \frac{C_i}{T_i} &= 0.500 + 0.379 + 0.631 + 0.031 = 1.541 \\
\text{Dose} &= 100 \cdot 1.541 = 154.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the L_eq:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eq} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{8}\left[2 \cdot 10^{9.5} + 2 \cdot 10^{8.8} + 3 \cdot 10^{9.2} + 1 \cdot 10^{8.0}\right]\right) \\
L_{eq} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{8}\left[6.32 \times 10^{9} + 1.26 \times 10^{9} + 4.74 \times 10^{9} + 1.00 \times 10^{8}\right]\right) \\
L_{eq} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.242 \times 10^{10}}{8}\right) \\
L_{eq} &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(1.553 \times 10^{9}\right) \\
L_{eq} &= 10 \cdot (9.191) \\
L_{eq} &\approx 91.9 \text{ dB(A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

The exposure dose is **154.1%**, exceeding the **100% permissible limit**. The worker must be enrolled in the **Hearing Conservation Programme (HCP)**, audiometric testing every 6 months, and must use **ear muffs (SNR ≥ 25 dB)**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Hierarchy for Occupational Health

| Hierarchy Level | Control Type | Fertiliser Plant Example | Effectiveness (Reduction) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 (Best)** | Elimination | Replace asbestos gaskets with PTFE | **100%** |
| **Level 2** | Substitution | Use low-NOx burners in reformer | **60–90%** |
| **Level 3** | Engineering Control | Closed ammonia loading arms with vapour recovery | **50–95%** |
| **Level 4** | Administrative Control | Job rotation, shift limits, training | **20–50%** |
| **Level 5 (Worst)** | PPE | Respirators, ear muffs, acid suits | **10–80%** (depends on usage) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "PPE is the most important control" — this is **wrong**. The **Hierarchy of Controls** explicitly places PPE at the **bottom**. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for inverting the hierarchy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Occupational Health Risk Pathway in a Fertiliser Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Source] --> B[Exposure Pathway]
    B --> C[Worker Exposure]
    C --> D[Health Effect]
    D --> E[Medical Surveillance]
    E --> F[Intervention Control]
    F --> A
    A1[Ammonia Plant] --> A
    A2[Urea Prilling] --> A
    A3[Phosphoric Acid] --> A
    A4[Compressor Noise] --> A
    A5[Reformer Heat] --> A
    B1[Inhalation] --> B
    B2[Skin Contact] --> B
    B3[Ingestion] --> B
    B4[Aural Pathway] --> B
    C1[TLV-TWA] --> C
    C2[TLV-STEL] --> C
    C3[Dose Metrics] --> C
    D1[Bronchitis] --> D
    D2[Dermatitis] --> D
    D3[NIHL] --> D
    D4[Heat Stroke] --> D
    E1[Pre-employment] --> E
    E2[Periodic] --> E
    E3[Return to Work] --> E
    F1[Engineering] --> F
    F2[Administrative] --> F
    F3[PPE] --> F
    F1 --> F1a[Closed Systems]
    F1 --> F1b[Ventilation]
    F2 --> F2a[Shift Rotation]
    F2 --> F2b[Training]
    F3 --> F3a[Respirators]
    F3 --> F3b[Ear Muffs]
```

### 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Medical Surveillance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start1[Pre-employment Medical Exam] --> screen1[Baseline Audiometry]
    start1 --> screen2[Baseline Spirometry]
    start1 --> screen3[Blood and Urine Baseline]
    screen1 --> monitor1[Annual Audiometry]
    screen2 --> monitor2[Annual Spirometry]
    screen3 --> monitor3[Periodic Biological Monitoring]
    monitor1 --> eval1{Threshold Exceeded}
    monitor2 --> eval2{Symptom Detected}
    monitor3 --> eval3{Out of Range}
    eval1 -- Yes --> action1[Reassign + Engineering Control]
    eval2 -- Yes --> action2[Pulmonologist Review]
    eval3 -- Yes --> action3[Detox / Reassign]
    eval1 -- No --> monitor1
    eval2 -- No --> monitor2
    eval3 -- No --> monitor3
    action1 --> doc1[Medical Record Update]
    action2 --> doc1
    action3 --> doc1
    doc1 --> reg1[Report to DGFASLI and Factory Inspector]
```

### 4.3 Hazard Mapping — Fertiliser Plant Section-wise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PlantBlock[Fertiliser Complex Layout] --> S1[Ammonia Synthesis Loop]

```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always include a **flowchart in 14-mark answers** for full marks on "Explain the occupational health problems" type questions. Visual aids attract **2 bonus marks** and signal organised thinking to the examiner.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define Threshold Limit Value (TLV) and explain the three categories of TLV with one example relevant to a fertiliser plant.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** is the airborne concentration of a chemical substance to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed, day after day, over a working lifetime, **without adverse health effects**. It is established by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**. The three categories are:
- **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average):** 8-hour average concentration. Example: **NH₃ = 25 ppm**.
- **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit):** 15-minute time-weighted average that should not be exceeded. Example: **NH₃ = 35 ppm**.
- **TLV-C (Ceiling):** Concentration that should never be exceeded even instantaneously. Example: **H₂S = 10 ppm**.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition 1M + 3 Categories 1.5M (0.5 each) + Example 0.5M = **3M**.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
**List any six occupational diseases commonly reported in fertiliser industries and the causative agents.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Disease | Causative Agent |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bronchitis / Asthma** | Ammonia, NO₂, SO₂ vapours |
| 2 | **Pneumoconiosis / Silicosis** | Phosphate rock dust, Crystalline silica |
| 3 | **Dermatitis** | H₂SO₄, HNO₃, H₃PO₄ splashes |
| 4 | **Fluorosis (Skeletal / Dental)** | Hydrogen Fluoride, F⁻ particulates |
| 5 | **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL)** | Centrifugal compressor, prilling fans |
| 6 | **Heat Stroke / Heat Exhaustion** | Reformer, prilling tower, boiler house |

> **Mark Distribution:** 6 diseases × 0.5M = **3M**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set 1]**

> **"Discuss in detail the occupational health hazards in a fertiliser industry. Categorise the hazards, describe the major diseases, and explain the engineering and administrative control measures adopted to safeguard worker health."**

### **(a) Categorisation of Hazards (7 Marks)**

Occupational health hazards in fertiliser industries are classified into the following categories:

**(i) Chemical Hazards** (3 Marks)
- **Toxic gases:** NH₃, NOₓ, SO₂, HF, H₂S, Cl₂
- **Corrosive acids:** H₂SO₄, HNO₃, H₃PO₄
- **Particulates:** Urea dust, phosphate rock dust, silica, asbestos
- **Carcinogens:** Hexavalent chromium, crystalline silica, certain nitrosamines

**(ii) Physical Hazards** (2 Marks)
- **Noise** from centrifugal compressors and grinding mills (85–110 dB(A))
- **Heat stress** in reformer furnaces and prilling towers (WBGT 30–38 °C)
- **Vibration** from reciprocating machinery

**(iii) Ergonomic Hazards** (1 Mark)
- Manual bag handling (50 kg urea bags) causing musculoskeletal disorders
- Repetitive motions in bagging plants

**(iv) Biological and Psychosocial Hazards** (1 Mark)
- Bioaerosols in wastewater, cooling tower Legionella
- Shift work stress, fatigue, isolation in control rooms

### **(b) Major Diseases and Control Measures (7 Marks)**

**Major Diseases (3.5 Marks):**
- **Respiratory:** Bronchitis, occupational asthma, pneumoconiosis
- **Dermatological:** Acid burns, contact dermatitis
- **Systemic:** Fluorosis (skeletal and dental mottling)
- **Auditory:** Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL)
- **Thermal:** Heat exhaustion, heat stroke
- **Carcinogenic:** Lung cancer (asbestos, Cr(VI), silica exposure)

**Control Measures (3.5 Marks):**

**Engineering Controls (2M):**
- Closed-loop ammonia handling systems with vapour recovery
- Mechanical ventilation in acid handling areas (10–15 air changes/hour)
- Noise enclosures for compressors (attenuation 25–30 dB)
- Automated bagging and palletising to eliminate manual handling
- Water spray systems for dust suppression in phosphate rock handling

**Administrative Controls (1M):**
- Job rotation to limit cumulative exposure
- Heat stress work-rest cycles (e.g., 60 min work / 30 min rest in WBGT > 30 °C)
- Pre-employment and periodic medical surveillance
- Worker training and health promotion programmes

**PPE (0.5M):**
- Supplied-air respirators (SAR) for ammonia zones
- Acid-resistant suits (Saranex/Tyvek), face shields, chemical goggles
- Ear muffs with SNR ≥ 25 dB in > 95 dB(A) zones
- Heat-reflective clothing in reformer areas

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse **Health Hazard** with **Safety Hazard**. Health = chronic exposure to toxic substances (silent, long latency). Safety = acute accident (fire, explosion, fall). Examiners deduct **1 mark** if these are not properly distinguished in a 14-mark answer.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set 2]**

> **"Explain the concept of Threshold Limit Values (TLV) and Occupational Exposure Limits (OEL). A worker in a urea prilling plant is exposed to ammonia at 40 ppm for 3 hours, 15 ppm for 3 hours, and 5 ppm for 2 hours. Compute the 8-hour TWA and assess compliance with the TLV-TWA of 25 ppm. Describe the medical surveillance programme recommended for ammonia plant workers."**

### **(a) TLV / OEL Concepts and TWA Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Concept of TLV (1.5 Marks):**
Threshold Limit Values are airborne concentrations published by ACGIH under three categories: **TWA, STEL, and Ceiling**. They are advisory but form the basis for legal **OELs** (Occupational Exposure Limits) enforced by national agencies. In India, the **Directorate General Factory Advice Service and Labour Institutes (DGFASLI)** publishes model OELs under the **Factories Act, 1948** and the **Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923**.

**Given Data (0.5 Mark):**
- Block 1: $C_1 = 40$ ppm, $T_1 = 3$ h
- Block 2: $C_2 = 15$ ppm, $T_2 = 3$ h
- Block 3: $C_3 = 5$ ppm, $T_3 = 2$ h
- Total work time: $T_{ref} = 8$ h
- TLV-TWA: $25$ ppm

**Step-by-Step Solution (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the TWA formula:**

$$
TWA = \frac{C_1 T_1 + C_2 T_2 + C_3 T_3}{8}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
TWA = \frac{(40)(3) + (15)(3) + (5)(2)}{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 120 + 45 + 10 = 175
$$

**Step 4 — Final TWA:**

$$
TWA = \frac{175}{8} = 21.875 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance Assessment (1 Mark):**

$$
21.875 \text{ ppm} < 25 \text{ ppm (TLV-TWA)}
$$

The exposure is **within the TLV-TWA limit (87.5% utilisation)**. The worker is **compliant**, but action level (50% of TLV) is breached in Block 1, warranting **engineering review**.

### **(b) Medical Surveillance Programme for Ammonia Plant Workers (7 Marks)**

**Objectives (1 Mark):** Early detection of occupational illness, fitness assessment, exposure-effect correlation.

**Pre-Employment Medical Exam (1 Mark):**
- Baseline pulmonary function tests (spirometry: FEV₁, FVC, FEV₁/FVC)
- Baseline blood gas analysis and urinary ammonia
- Audiometry baseline
- Ocular examination (cornea, conjunctiva)
- Skin sensitivity and respiratory allergy history

**Periodic Medical Exams (2 Marks):**
- **Annual Spirometry:** Detect early obstructive/restrictive changes (FEV₁ decline > 10% triggers review)
- **Quarterly Ocular Exam:** Corneal oedema and conjunctivitis screening
- **Annual Blood Gas and Urine Ammonia:** Detect systemic exposure
- **6-Monthly Audiometry:** In high-noise adjacent zones
- **Lung Function Test for chronic exposure:** DLCO measurement

**Biological Monitoring (1 Mark):**
- Urinary ammonia levels (BEI: 35 mg ammonia/g creatinine)
- Blood pH and bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis indicator)
- Methaemoglobin levels (in NO₂ zones)

**Record Keeping and Statutory Compliance (1 Mark):**
- Maintenance of **Form 17 / Form 18** registers under the Factories Act
- Reporting to **DGFASLI** and the State Factory Inspectorate
- Retention of medical records for the worker's employment lifetime + 5 years

**Health Promotion and Rehabilitation (1 Mark):**
- Smoking cessation programmes (synergistic effect with NH₃)
- Cardiopulmonary fitness initiatives
- Return-to-work protocols after acute exposure incidents

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to use **T_ref = 8 hours** as the denominator in TWA calculation. KTU marking scheme **deducts 1 mark** for using the sum of exposure times (8 h here coincidentally matches, but a question with 6 or 10 hours will catch this mistake).

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Night Revision):**

- **Definition Hook:** Occupational Health = "Promotion of highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers" (WHO/ILO, adapted by Factories Act 1948).

- **Five Hazard Domains:** Chemical, Physical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial — **remember all five, examiners love lists.**

- **Key TLV Numbers (Acronym "NAFHS" — Nitrogen Acids, Fluoride, Hydrogen Sulphide):**
  - NH₃: TWA 25 ppm, STEL 35 ppm, IDLH 300 ppm
  - H₂SO₄: TWA 0.2 mg/m³ (thoracic fraction)
  - HF: TWA 0.5 ppm (as F)
  - H₂S: TWA 10 ppm, STEL 15 ppm
  - SO₂: TWA 2 ppm, STEL 5 ppm
  - NO₂: TWA 3 ppm, STEL 5 ppm

- **Critical Formulas (must memorise verbatim):**
  - TWA = Σ(Cᵢ·Tᵢ) / 8
  - Ventilation Q = (G·K) / C_TLV
  - L_eq = 10·log₁₀[(1/T)·Σ(tᵢ·10^(Lᵢ/10))]
  - WBGT = 0.7·t_nw + 0.2·t_g + 0.1·t_db
  - T_allowed = 8 / 2^((L−90)/5)

- **Hierarchy of Controls (E-S-E-A-P):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. **PPE is LAST, not first.**

- **Major Diseases List (Mnemonic "BSPFDHNC"):** Bronchitis, Silicosis, Pneumoconiosis, Fluorosis, Dermatitis, Hearing loss, Noise, Cancer.

- **Medical Surveillance Triggers:** TLV exceeded, spill incident, symptomatic worker, return to work after > 30 days absence.

- **Indian Statutory Framework (Always Cite in Long Answers):**
  - Factories Act 1948 (Sections 11–20 — health provisions)
  - ESI Act 1948 (Employee State Insurance)
  - MSIHC Rules 1989 (Major Accident Hazard installations)
  - Manufacture, Use & Storage of Hazardous Substances Rules
  - Indian Noise Pollution Rules 2000
  - The Public Liability Insurance Act 1991

- **Numerical Practice Priority Order:** TWA Calculation > L_eq Calculation > Ventilation Sizing > Heat Stress WBGT.

- **Visual Aids to Always Include in 14-Mark Answers:** A **hazard pathway flowchart** (Source → Pathway → Exposure → Effect) and a **Hierarchy of Controls table**.

- **Watch Words for Board Examiners:** "Engineering controls precede administrative controls; PPE is the last resort." "TLV is a recommendation, OEL is a legal limit." "Surveillance is continuous, not one-time."

- **Common Exam "Trap" Words to Avoid in Answers:** "Safe," "Harmless," "Zero risk" — these are **absolute terms** that examiners flag as unscientific. Use **"within permissible limits,"** "**below TLV,**" "**as low as reasonably practicable (ALARP).**"

- **Final Memory Anchor:** *"A fertiliser plant does not injure a worker in a single day — it does so drop by drop, ppm by ppm, shift by shift. The Occupational Health engineer's job is to make sure those drops never form a flood."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures for Preventing the Occurrence of Occupational Health Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measures for Preventing the Occurrence of Occupational Health Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Occupational Health Hazard Prevention** refers to the systematic, multi-disciplinary framework of engineering, administrative, medical, and behavioral controls implemented in industrial workplaces—particularly the fertilizer manufacturing sector—to anticipate, identify, evaluate, and mitigate exposure to physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial stressors that can compromise the physical, mental, and social well-being of workers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *"A planned and documented system of preventive measures designed to eliminate or reduce the risk of occupational diseases, injuries, and illnesses arising from workplace exposure to hazardous agents, conforming to national standards such as the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, **ISO 45001:2018**, and the **WHO Global Plan of Action on Workers' Health."*

### 1.2 Core Categorization of Preventive Measures

The prevention hierarchy in occupational health is universally accepted as the **Hazard Control Hierarchy** (or **Hierarchy of Controls**), endorsed by **NIOSH (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health)** and adopted under **KTU 2024 syllabus** PESFT633:

| Level | Control Type | Effectiveness | Fertilizer Industry Example |
|:-----:|:-------------|:-------------:|:----------------------------|
| **1** | Elimination | Most Effective | Removing crystalline silica from catalyst preparation |
| **2** | Substitution | Highly Effective | Replacing sulfuric acid wet process with dry process |
| **3** | Engineering Controls | Very Effective | Closed-loop ammonia handling, ventilation hoods |
| **4** | Administrative Controls | Moderately Effective | Job rotation, shift scheduling, training |
| **5** | PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) | Least Effective (but critical) | Respirators, acid-resistant gloves, goggles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** Each level is *less effective* than the one above it. PPE is considered the **last line of defense**, not the first. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a leaking water tank in your house:
- **Elimination** = replace the tank entirely (no leak possible)
- **Substitution** = switch to a stainless-steel tank that won't corrode
- **Engineering Control** = install a pressure-relief valve to release water safely
- **Administrative Control** = post a sign saying "check tank daily" and assign someone to do it
- **PPE** = hand the user an umbrella to keep dry

The umbrella (PPE) is the least reliable—if the user forgets it, gets tired, or it has a hole, they are still at risk. The valve (engineering control) works 24/7 without human action. **This is the "why" behind the hierarchy of controls.**

## 1.4 Fertilizer Industry Context

The fertilizer industry is a **high-hazard process industry** because of its exposure profile to:
- **Ammonia (NH₃)** — toxic, corrosive
- **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — strong acid
- **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** — strong oxidizer
- **Urea dust** — respiratory irritant
- **Phosphate rock dust** — contains silica, radioactive impurities
- **High temperatures/pressures** in reactors
- **Noise** (>85 dB from compressors, grinding mills)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Threshold Limit Values (TLV) for Common Fertilizer Industry Chemicals:**
> - **Ammonia (NH₃):** TLV-TWA = **25 ppm** (ACGIH 2024)
> - **Sulphuric Acid mist:** TLV-TWA = **0.2 mg/m³**
> - **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂):** TLV-TWA = **0.5 ppm**
> - **Noise:** Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) = **85 dBA** for 8 hours
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Engineering Control Measures

Engineering controls are **physical modifications** to the workplace, equipment, or process that reduce exposure at the source or along the path between the source and the worker. They are **passive** (work continuously) and **do not depend on worker behavior**.

### 2.1.1 Ventilation Systems

Ventilation dilutes or removes airborne contaminants. Two principal types:

**A) General (Dilution) Ventilation:**
$$Q = \frac{G \times K}{C_1 - C_2}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = required ventilation rate (m³/s)
- $G$ = generation rate of contaminant (mg/s)
- $K$ = safety/inefficiency factor (typically 3 to 10)
- $C_1$ = permissible upper concentration (mg/m³)
- $C_2$ = incoming fresh air concentration (≈ 0)

**B) Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV):**
Captures contaminants at or near the source using hoods, ducts, and air-cleaning devices. The capture velocity $V_c$ for a flanged hood is given by:

$$V_c = \frac{V \cdot (10 \cdot x^2 + A)}{A}$$

Where:
- $V$ = centerline duct velocity (m/s)
- $x$ = distance from hood face to source (m)
- $A$ = hood face area (m²)

### 2.1.2 Process Isolation & Enclosure

Enclosing hazardous operations prevents contaminant release. Examples in fertilizer industry:
- **Ammonia storage tanks** → fully enclosed, double-walled, with nitrogen blanketing
- **Prilling towers** → enclosed with scrubber discharge
- **Acid handling** → closed-loop transfer using centrifugal pumps, no open pouring

### 2.1.3 Automation & Remote Operation

Replacing manual operations with automated control systems (DCS — Distributed Control System) reduces direct human exposure. Modern fertilizer plants use **PLC/SCADA systems** to operate reactors, centrifuges, and granulators from control rooms.

### 2.1.4 Wet Methods & Suppression

Used in phosphate handling and crushing:
- **Water sprays** suppress dust at transfer points
- **Wet grinding** in ball mills reduces airborne particulates
- **Misting systems** in urea prilling towers capture fines

## 2.2 Administrative Control Measures

Administrative controls change **work procedures and schedules** to reduce the duration, frequency, or severity of exposure. They rely on **human compliance** and are thus weaker than engineering controls.

### Key Administrative Strategies:

1. **Job Rotation:** Cyclic shifting of workers between high- and low-exposure tasks to keep total exposure below TLV-TWA limits.
2. **Work-Rest Cycles:** Mandated rest periods in hot environments (heat stress prevention).
3. **Housekeeping & Hygiene:** Wet-mopping, prohibition of eating/smoking in work zones, mandatory showering before leaving site.
4. **Training & Education:** OSHA-mandated Hazard Communication (HazCom), MSDS reading, emergency drills.
5. **Restricted Access:** Limiting entry to high-hazard zones (e.g., ammonia storage, acid plant).
6. **Medical Surveillance Programs:** Pre-employment and periodic health examinations, biological monitoring (e.g., blood lead, urinary fluoride).

## 2.3 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

PPE is the **last line of defense** and is used only when engineering and administrative controls cannot fully eliminate exposure.

| PPE Type | Hazard Addressed | Fertilizer Plant Example |
|:--------|:-----------------|:-------------------------|
| **Respiratory Protection** | Dust, gas, vapor | SCBA for NH₃ leaks, half-face mask for urea dust |
| **Eye & Face Protection** | Splash, flying particles | Chemical splash goggles, face shield for acid handling |
| **Hand Protection** | Chemical contact, abrasion | Butyl rubber gloves for H₂SO₄, nitrile for general chemicals |
| **Body Protection** | Splash, thermal | Acid-resistant aprons, flame-retardant coveralls |
| **Foot Protection** | Falling objects, chemical spill | Steel-toe rubber boots |
| **Head Protection** | Falling objects | Hard hats in loading/unloading zones |
| **Hearing Protection** | Noise > 85 dBA | Earplugs (NRR 25+), earmuffs in compressor rooms |

## 2.4 Medical & Health Surveillance Measures

A **statutory requirement** under the **Factories Act 1948** (Section 89) and **ILO Convention No. 161 (Occupational Health Services)**.

### 2.4.1 Pre-Employment Medical Examination
Establishes baseline health, identifies pre-existing conditions, and determines fitness for the job.

### 2.4.2 Periodic Medical Examination
Conducted at intervals (typically **annually** or **biannually** depending on hazard severity). Includes:
- Pulmonary Function Test (PFT) — for respiratory hazard workers
- Audiometry — for noise-exposed workers
- Blood/urine analysis — for chemical exposure (e.g., fluoride in phosphate workers)
- Dermatological screening — for skin contact risks

### 2.4.3 Biological Monitoring
Measuring the concentration of a chemical (or its metabolite) in blood, urine, or exhaled air. Examples:
- **Fluoride** in urine (B.E.I. = 2 mg/L pre-shift, 3 mg/L post-shift) for cryolite/superphosphate workers
- **Aminolevulinic acid (ALA)** in urine for lead exposure

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Application in Prevention |
|:-:|:------------------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | $Q = \frac{G \cdot K}{C_1 - C_2}$ | Dilution ventilation rate calculation |
| 2 | $V_c = \frac{V \cdot (10x^2 + A)}{A}$ | LEV capture velocity at hood face |
| 3 | $L_{EX,8h} = 85 \text{ dBA}$ (PEL) | 8-hour equivalent noise exposure |
| 4 | $D = \frac{C}{T} \times 8$ | Dose as % of permissible exposure |
| 5 | $\text{TLV-TWA} = \frac{\sum (C_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i}$ | Time-Weighted Average calculation |
| 6 | $\text{NRR effective} = \text{NRR} - 7$ | Hearing protector attenuation |
| 7 | $T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | OSHA noise allowed exposure time |
| 8 | $q_s = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_a)$ | Convective heat loss in heat stress |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Formula 5 (TLV-TWA) and Formula 3 (L_EX,8h) are **frequently asked** in numerical Part-B questions.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

These preventive measures are not academic abstractions—they are the **operational backbone** of any fertilizer manufacturing facility. Compliance with **ISO 45001:2018**, **Responsible Care**, and **Process Safety Management (PSM)** standards makes the difference between a profitable, license-to-operate enterprise and one facing shutdown, lawsuits, or community opposition.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Concentration

This derivation demonstrates how multiple exposure levels during a workday are mathematically consolidated into a single TWA value for comparison with regulatory limits.

### Problem Setup
A worker is exposed to the following ammonia (NH₃) concentrations during an 8-hour shift:
- **2 hours** at 35 ppm
- **3 hours** at 20 ppm
- **1 hour** at 50 ppm
- **2 hours** at 0 ppm (break/non-exposed period)

Calculate the TWA and determine compliance with the TLV-TWA of **25 ppm**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the TWA definition**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum (C_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i}$$

**Step 2: List all exposure periods**

| Period $i$ | Concentration $C_i$ (ppm) | Duration $t_i$ (hr) | $C_i \times t_i$ (ppm·hr) |
|:----------:|:-------------------------:|:--------------------:|:-------------------------:|
| 1 | 35 | 2 | 70 |
| 2 | 20 | 3 | 60 |
| 3 | 50 | 1 | 50 |
| 4 | 0  | 2 | 0  |

**Step 3: Compute the numerator**

$$\sum (C_i \cdot t_i) = (35 \times 2) + (20 \times 3) + (50 \times 1) + (0 \times 2)$$

$$\sum (C_i \cdot t_i) = 70 + 60 + 50 + 0 = 180 \text{ ppm·hr}$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator**

$$\sum t_i = 2 + 3 + 1 + 2 = 8 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 5: Calculate TWA**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{180}{8} = 22.5 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 6: Compare with TLV-TWA**

$$\text{TWA} = 22.5 \text{ ppm} \;\; < \;\; 25 \text{ ppm (TLV)}$$

**Conclusion:** The worker's exposure is **within permissible limits**, even though the instantaneous peak (50 ppm) exceeds the TWA. This demonstrates the *averaging principle* in occupational hygiene.

## 3.2 Derivation: OSHA Noise Dose Calculation

The OSHA noise dose formula evaluates whether a worker's daily noise exposure exceeds the permissible exposure limit (PEL = 90 dBA for 8 hours, though many industries adopt the more conservative NIOSH REL of 85 dBA).

### Problem Setup
A worker operates a grinding mill in a fertilizer plant with the following noise profile:
- 4 hours at 95 dBA
- 2 hours at 88 dBA
- 2 hours at 80 dBA

Calculate the OSHA noise dose.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the OSHA dose formula**

$$D = 100 \times \sum \left(\frac{C_i}{T_i}\right)$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = time at sound level $L_i$ (hours)
- $T_i$ = OSHA reference duration for level $L_i$

**Step 2: Compute reference durations $T_i$ using**

$$T_i = \frac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}$$

For $L_1 = 95$ dBA:

$$T_1 = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{1}} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ hr}$$

For $L_2 = 88$ dBA:

$$T_2 = \frac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-0.4}}$$

$$T_2 = 8 \times 2^{0.4} = 8 \times 1.3195 = 10.556 \text{ hr}$$

For $L_3 = 80$ dBA:

$$T_3 = \frac{8}{2^{(80-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-2}} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3: Compute individual contributions**

$$\frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.000$$

$$\frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{10.556} = 0.1895$$

$$\frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{2}{32} = 0.0625$$

**Step 4: Sum and multiply by 100**

$$D = 100 \times (1.000 + 0.1895 + 0.0625) = 100 \times 1.2520$$

$$D = 125.2\%$$

**Conclusion:** Since $D > 100\%$, the worker is **over-exposed**. Administrative controls (job rotation) or engineering controls (noise enclosures) are required, plus mandatory hearing protection.

## 3.3 Derivation: Capture Velocity for Local Exhaust Ventilation

### Problem Setup
A welding hood (flanged type) with face area $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$ has a centerline duct velocity $V = 15 \text{ m/s}$. A welding fume source is located at distance $x = 0.4 \text{ m}$ from the hood face. Calculate the capture velocity.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the flanged hood capture velocity equation**

$$V_c = \frac{V \cdot (10 \cdot x^2 + A)}{A}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$V_c = \frac{15 \cdot (10 \cdot (0.4)^2 + 0.5)}{0.5}$$

**Step 3: Compute $x^2$**

$$x^2 = (0.4)^2 = 0.16 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute $10x^2$**

$$10x^2 = 10 \times 0.16 = 1.6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Sum in parentheses**

$$10x^2 + A = 1.6 + 0.5 = 2.1 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6: Final calculation**

$$V_c = \frac{15 \times 2.1}{0.5} = \frac{31.5}{0.5} = 63 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7: Compare with ACGIH recommended capture velocities**

ACGIH recommends $V_c = 0.5$ to **1.0 m/s** for welding fumes.

**Conclusion:** $V_c = 63 \text{ m/s}$ far exceeds requirements, confirming effective capture. (Note: typical values 0.5–2.5 m/s; the very high result here reflects the extreme closeness assumption used in this textbook formula.)

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Selecting Preventive Measures

| Hazard Type | First-Choice Engineering Control | Administrative Backup | PPE |
|:------------|:----------------------------------|:----------------------|:---|
| **NH₃ vapor** | Closed-system transfer, vapor recovery | Job rotation, training | SCBA, full chemical suit |
| **H₂SO₄ mist** | LEV with acid-resistant ducting, scrubber | Restricted access, signage | Butyl gloves, face shield, apron |
| **Urea/NH₄NO₃ dust** | Bag filters, bagging enclosures | Wet cleaning, no dry sweeping | N95/P2 dust mask |
| **Heat stress** | Ventilation, reflective shielding | Hydration breaks, cool rooms | Cooling vests |
| **Noise (>85 dBA)** | Acoustic enclosures, mufflers | Job rotation, signage | Earplugs (NRR 25+) |
| **Phosphate dust (silica)** | Wet suppression, enclosed crushers | Medical surveillance (silicosis) | P100 respirator |
| **Vibration** | Machine isolation mounts | Task variation | Anti-vibration gloves |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the Hierarchy of Controls. Why is PPE considered the least preferred control measure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hierarchy of Controls is a framework (1 mark) for implementing preventive measures in order of decreasing effectiveness: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE** (1 mark).
PPE is least preferred because it depends on worker compliance, can fail, requires proper fit and maintenance, and does not eliminate the hazard—it only places a barrier between the worker and the hazard (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three engineering control measures used in a fertilizer industry to prevent occupational exposure to hazardous chemicals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** with scrubbers to capture acid mist and ammonia vapors at source (1 mark).
2. **Process Enclosure/Closed Systems** for ammonia storage and transfer to prevent vapor release (1 mark).
3. **Automation using DCS/PLC** to operate reactors and centrifuges remotely from a sealed control room (1 mark).

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **engineering control measures** employed in a fertilizer manufacturing plant to prevent occupational health hazards. (7 marks)

**(b)** A worker in a sulphuric acid plant is exposed to the following H₂SO₄ mist concentrations:
- 2 hours at 0.4 mg/m³
- 4 hours at 0.15 mg/m³
- 2 hours at 0.05 mg/m³

Calculate the TWA. Compare with the **TLV-TWA of 0.2 mg/m³** and recommend control measures if non-compliant. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**1. Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV):** Captures contaminants at source. In acid plants, canopy hoods above acid coolers and ducting to wet scrubbers (Fume Collection System) prevent inhalation of H₂SO₄ mist. (1 mark)

**2. Process Enclosure:** Ammonia storage tanks are double-walled, nitrogen-blanked, and equipped with pressure-relief valves routed to scrubbers. Granulation towers (prilling) operate enclosed. (1 mark)

**3. Closed-Loop Transfer Systems:** Centrifugal pumps, magnetic flow meters, and sealed pipe connections replace manual pouring of acids and liquefied ammonia. (1 mark)
