**4. Automation & Remote Operation:** DCS-based control rooms enable operators to start, stop, and monitor reactors, evaporators, and packaging lines without entering hazardous zones. (1 mark)

**5. Ventilation Systems:** General dilution ventilation using roof extractors and supply fans in control rooms and laboratories. Air changes per hour (ACH) of 10–15 are typical for chemical-handling areas. (1 mark)

**6. Wet Suppression & Dust Control:** Water spray systems at transfer points, transfer towers, and bagging stations suppress phosphate and urea dust. (1 mark)

**7. Isolation & Segregation:** Physical separation of incompatible chemicals (e.g., ammonia plant from acid plant); bund walls around storage tanks. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1: Apply the TWA formula**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum (C_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i}$$

**Step 2: Tabulate the values**

| Period $i$ | $C_i$ (mg/m³) | $t_i$ (hr) | $C_i \times t_i$ |
|:----------:|:-------------:|:----------:|:----------------:|
| 1 | 0.40 | 2 | 0.80 |
| 2 | 0.15 | 4 | 0.60 |
| 3 | 0.05 | 2 | 0.10 |

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

$$\sum (C_i \cdot t_i) = 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.10 = 1.50 \text{ mg/m³·hr}$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator**

$$\sum t_i = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 5: Calculate TWA**

$$\text{TWA} = \frac{1.50}{8} = 0.1875 \text{ mg/m³}$$

**Step 6: Compliance check**

$$\text{TWA} = 0.1875 < 0.20 \text{ mg/m³} = \text{TLV-TWA}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The exposure is **just within** permissible limits (margin = 6.25%). This is a **borderline case**.

**Recommended Control Measures (4 marks):**
1. **Engineering:** Install/improve LEV at the acid cooler section; inspect scrubber efficiency monthly.
2. **Administrative:** Reduce exposure duration via job rotation; conduct acid mist monitoring every 6 months.
3. **PPE:** Mandatory use of acid-resistant face shield, butyl-rubber gloves, and chemical apron.
4. **Medical:** Biannual pulmonary function test and dental erosion check for exposed workers.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the components of a **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) program** in a fertilizer industry. Discuss the selection criteria for respiratory protection against ammonia exposure. (7 marks)

**(b)** The compressor room of an ammonia plant produces a steady noise level of **98 dBA** during an 8-hour shift. The plant adopts the **NIOSH Recommended Exposure Limit (REL) of 85 dBA**. Using the OSHA 5-dB exchange rate, determine:
(i) The reference duration $T$ for 98 dBA.
(ii) Whether the worker is overexposed.
(iii) The minimum time the worker may be exposed without exceeding the REL. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Components of a PPE Program (4 marks):**
1. **Hazard Assessment:** Identify workplace hazards, mapping them to specific body parts.
2. **Selection:** Choose PPE certified to **IS / EN / ANSI / NIOSH** standards.
3. **Training:** Educate workers on proper use, limitations, donning/doffing, cleaning, and storage.
4. **Fitting & Maintenance:** Especially for respirators—quantitative or qualitative fit-testing annually.
5. **Inspection & Replacement:** Routine checks for damage, expiry (especially for chemical gloves), and proper storage.

**Respiratory Protection Selection for NH₃ (3 marks):**

| Concentration (ppm) | Recommended Respirator |
|:-------------------:|:-----------------------|
| < 25 (TLV) | Half-mask with NH₃ cartridge (APR) |
| 25–250 | Full-face APR or PAPR with NH₃ cartridges |
| 250–500 | Full-face PAPR with NH₃ cartridges, or SCBA |
| > 500 or IDLH | **SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus)** with positive pressure |

**Selection criteria include:**
- Assigned Protection Factor (APF) — e.g., SCBA = 10,000
- IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) for NH₃ = **300 ppm**
- Cartridge breakthrough time and end-of-service-life indicator (ESLI)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1: Use the OSHA 5-dB exchange rate formula**

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 85)/5}}$$

**(i) Compute T for L = 98 dBA**

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(98-85)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{13/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2.6}}$$

$$2^{2.6} = 2^{2} \times 2^{0.6} = 4 \times 1.5157 = 6.0629$$

$$T = \frac{8}{6.0629} = 1.319 \text{ hr} \approx 1 \text{ hr } 19 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Compliance check**

Since the worker's exposure is **8 hours** at 98 dBA, but the reference duration $T$ is only **1.319 hours**, the worker is **SEVERELY OVEREXPOSED**. (1 mark)

**Step 3: Compute the OSHA noise dose**

$$D = 100 \times \frac{C}{T} = 100 \times \frac{8}{1.319} = 606.5\%$$

This is approximately **6 times** the permissible dose. (1 mark)

**(iii) Minimum exposure time to stay within REL**

Set $C_{max} = T$ and solve for the level at which 8 hours is permissible, or the time at which 98 dBA gives 100% dose:

For 100% dose: $C_{max} = T = 1.319$ hours (≈ 1 hr 19 min). (1 mark)

**Recommended controls (1 mark):**
- **Engineering:** Install acoustic enclosures around compressors; use vibration isolators.
- **Administrative:** Limit shifts to < 1.5 hours; rotate workers.
- **PPE:** Earplugs with NRR ≥ 25 (achieving effective attenuation ≥ 10 dB), reducing 98 dBA to ~ 88 dBA, then to ~83 dBA.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PEL with TLV:** PEL (OSHA) = 90 dBA; TLV/REL (NIOSH/ACGIH) = 85 dBA. Read the question carefully! (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Unit Errors in TWA:** Always express $C_i$ in the same unit as the TLV (ppm or mg/m³). (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** in the dose formula. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Misidentifying the hierarchy order.** Engineering > Administrative > PPE in effectiveness. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Confusing IDLH with TLV.** IDLH for NH₃ = 300 ppm (not 25 ppm). (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting to convert hours to consistent units** (e.g., 1 hr 19 min instead of decimal 1.319 hr). (Lose 0.5 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls Order:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (most → least effective).
- ✅ **PPE is the LAST line of defense**, not the first—remember this for essay questions.
- ✅ **Engineering controls** (LEV, enclosure, automation, wet suppression) are **passive and 24/7**—they do not depend on worker behavior.
- ✅ **Administrative controls** include job rotation, training, medical surveillance, restricted access, and housekeeping.
- ✅ **Medical surveillance** is mandated by the **Factories Act 1948 (Sec 89)** and **ILO Convention 161**.
- ✅ **TLV-TWA for NH₃** = 25 ppm; **PEL for noise** = 85–90 dBA; **IDLH for NH₃** = 300 ppm.
- ✅ **OSHA 5-dB exchange rate** formula: $T = 8 / 2^{(L-85)/5}$ for NIOSH; $T = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/5}$ for OSHA.
- ✅ **TWA formula:** $\text{TWA} = \sum(C_i t_i) / \sum t_i$ — must always sum to 8 hours (or actual shift duration).
- ✅ **LEV capture velocity** for flanged hoods uses the equation $V_c = V(10x^2 + A)/A$.
- ✅ **Fertilizer-specific hazards:** NH₃ (toxic), H₂SO₄/HNO₃ (corrosive), urea dust (irritant), phosphate dust (silicosis risk), heat, noise.
- ✅ **Biological monitoring** examples: urinary fluoride (cryolite exposure), blood lead, urinary ALA.
- ✅ **Hygiene facilities** (showers, locker rooms) are part of administrative controls under the **Workers' Welfare** provisions.
- ✅ **For KTU 14-mark questions**, always structure your answer: **Definition → Categorization → Explanation → Numerical/Example → Conclusion → Control Recommendations.**
- ✅ **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) underpins **ISO 45001:2018** Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements of ISO 9000, 14000 and OHSAS 1800
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements of ISO 9000, 14000 and OHSAS 1800

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Management System Standards

In modern process industries — particularly the **fertilizer industry** which deals with hazardous chemicals (ammonia, urea, sulphuric acid, phosphoric acid, nitric acid) at high pressures and temperatures — operating without a structured **Management System (MS)** is analogous to driving a heavy truck on a highway without brakes, steering, or mirrors. To bring global uniformity in how organizations manage **Quality, Environment, and Occupational Health & Safety**, the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and the **Occupational Health and Safety Assessment Series (OHSAS)** project group introduced a family of voluntary, consensus-based standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO** is a non-governmental, independent international body headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland**, comprising national standards bodies from 167+ countries. It does **not** certify companies — third-party certification bodies (CBs) do.

### Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**ISO 9000 Series**
A family of standards developed by **ISO Technical Committee 176 (TC 176)** that establishes the requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**. The current edition is **ISO 9001:2015**, built on the **Annex SL (High-Level Structure)** and the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle.

**ISO 14000 Series**
A family of standards developed by **ISO Technical Committee 207 (TC 207)** that establishes the requirements for an **Environmental Management System (EMS)**. The current edition is **ISO 14001:2015**, also aligned with Annex SL and PDCA.

**OHSAS 18000 / 18001**
The **Occupational Health and Safety Assessment Series** was developed by a consortium of certification bodies, industrial organizations, and national standards bodies. **OHSAS 18001:2007** specified requirements for an **Occupational Health and Safety Management System (OHSMS)**. *Note: OHSAS 18001 has been officially withdrawn and superseded by **ISO 45001:2018** in October 2021.*

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 syllabus purposes, OHSAS 18000/18001 is still examinable as a foundational concept. Students should additionally be aware of the successor ISO 45001:2018.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Pillars" House

Imagine you are constructing a house for a fertilizer plant. Each pillar represents a management system:

| Pillar | Foundation Represents | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 9000** | Customer Satisfaction & Product Quality | The strong foundation that ensures bricks (products) are uniform and load-tested |
| **ISO 14000** | Environmental Protection & Pollution Control | The rainwater drainage and waste management system of the house |
| **OHSAS 18000** | Worker Safety & Health | The fire extinguishers, exit signs, helmets, and first-aid kits |

Together, these three pillars form an **Integrated Management System (IMS)** — the strong, earthquake-resistant house that protects both occupants (workers) and surroundings (environment) while delivering value (quality products).

### Why These Standards Matter in the Fertilizer Industry

The fertilizer sector is classified as a **Major Hazard Industry (MHI)** under the **Factories Act, 1948** and the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC Rules)**. Key risk drivers include:

- **Toxic releases** (NH₃, HF, Cl₂, NOx)
- **Explosive atmospheres** (dust from urea, ammonium nitrate)
- **High-temperature reactors** (Haber-Bosch at 400–500 °C and 150–300 bar)
- **Corrosive chemicals** (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄)
- **Onsite/Offsite Emergency Plans** mandated under MSIHC Rules

In this context, the three ISO/OHSAS standards act as the **operational nervous system** ensuring risk is systematically identified, controlled, and continuously improved.

---

## 1.3 High-Level Structure of Each Standard

All three standards follow a **common 10-clause structure (Annex SL)** since 2015:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annex SL High-Level Structure (HLS)**
> 1. Scope
> 2. Normative References
> 3. Terms & Definitions
> 4. Context of the Organization
> 5. Leadership
> 6. Planning
> 7. Support
> 8. Operation
> 9. Performance Evaluation
> 10. Improvement

This harmonization allows **seamless integration** of QMS, EMS, and OHSMS into a single **Integrated Management System (IMS)**.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

- **Atmospheric pressure:** **101.325 kPa** (used in fugitive emission calculations)
- **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL):** measured in **ppm** or **mg/m³**
- **Sound pressure level:** measured in **dB(A)**
- **Lethal Concentration 50 (LC₅₀):** measured in **ppm·volume** or **mg/L**
- **Lethal Dose 50 (LD₅₀):** measured in **mg/kg body weight**
- **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average):** typically **8-hour workday**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ISO 9000 Series — Quality Management System (QMS)

### 2.1.1 Evolution & Family of Standards

| Standard | Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 9000:2015** | Quality management systems — Fundamentals and vocabulary | Defines terms and concepts |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | Quality management systems — Requirements | Certifiable standard |
| **ISO 9004:2018** | Quality management — Quality of an organization | Guidance for sustained success |
| **ISO 19011:2018** | Guidelines for auditing management systems | Audit methodology |

### 2.1.2 The Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9000:2015)

1. **Customer focus** — Primary focus is meeting customer requirements
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction
3. **Engagement of people** — Competent, empowered people at all levels
4. **Process approach** — Activities managed as interrelated processes
5. **Improvement** — Ongoing focus on improvement
6. **Evidence-based decision making** — Decisions based on analysis of data
7. **Relationship management** — Manage relationships with interested parties

### 2.1.3 Core Requirements of ISO 9001:2015 (Clause-wise Breakdown)

- **Clause 4 — Context of the Organization:** Identify internal/external issues, needs of interested parties, define QMS scope, determine processes (SIPOC: Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers)
- **Clause 5 — Leadership:** Top management shall demonstrate leadership, establish quality policy, assign responsibilities & authorities
- **Clause 6 — Planning:** Address risks & opportunities, define quality objectives, plan changes
- **Clause 7 — Support:** Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information
- **Clause 8 — Operation:** Operational planning, product/service requirements, design & development, control of externally provided processes, production & service provision, release, nonconforming outputs
- **Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation:** Monitor, measure, analyze, evaluate; conduct internal audit; conduct management review
- **Clause 10 — Improvement:** Determine & select opportunities, treat nonconformities, corrective action, continual improvement

### 2.1.4 PDCA Cycle in ISO 9001

The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle is applied at two levels:
- **Overall QMS framework** (clauses 4–10)
- **Each individual process** within the QMS

---

## 2.2 ISO 14000 Series — Environmental Management System (EMS)

### 2.2.1 Evolution & Family of Standards

| Standard | Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 14001:2015** | Environmental management systems — Requirements with guidance for use | Certifiable EMS standard |
| **ISO 14004:2016** | General guidelines on implementation | Implementation guidance |
| **ISO 14005:2019** | Guidelines for a flexible approach to phased implementation | Phased EMS |
| **ISO 14006:2020** | Eco-design | Integrating eco-design |
| **ISO 14040 / 14044** | Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) | LCA methodology |
| **ISO 14064 / 14065** | Greenhouse gases (GHG) | Carbon footprinting |
| **ISO 14046** | Water footprint | Water Footprint Assessment |
| **ISO 19011** | Auditing guidelines | Audit EMS |

### 2.2.2 Core Requirements of ISO 14001:2015

- **Environmental Policy** — Commitment to protection of environment, prevention of pollution, legal compliance, continual improvement
- **Environmental Aspects & Impacts Identification** — All activities, products, services evaluated for aspects (causes) and impacts (effects on environment)
- **Legal & Other Requirements** — Identify, access, determine how they apply
- **Objectives, Targets & Programmes** — Measurable environmental objectives at relevant functions/levels
- **Operational Control** — Procedures for significant aspects, emergency preparedness & response
- **Monitoring & Measurement** — Track performance, calibrate equipment
- **Compliance Evaluation** — Periodically evaluate compliance with legal obligations
- **Internal Audit & Management Review** — Same as QMS

> [!NOTE]
> The fertilizer industry is governed by **Environmental Protection Act, 1986**, **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**, **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**, **Hazardous Waste Management Rules**, and **Consent to Operate** from **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCB)** under **Consent Order** system.

### 2.2.3 Aspect–Impact Relationship

$$\text{Environmental Aspect} \longrightarrow \text{Environmental Impact}$$

Examples in a fertilizer plant:
- Aspect: Discharge of cooling tower blowdown → Impact: Thermal pollution in receiving water body
- Aspect: Urea dust emission from prilling tower → Impact: Air pollution, eutrophication risk
- Aspect: HF fumes from phosphoric acid plant → Impact: Acid rain, vegetation damage
- Aspect: Spent catalyst disposal → Impact: Soil & groundwater contamination

---

## 2.3 OHSAS 18000 / OHSAS 18001 — Occupational Health & Safety Management System

### 2.3.1 Origin & Status

- Developed by **OHSAS Project Group** (1996, revised 2007)
- Standards: **OHSAS 18001:2007** (Requirements), **OHSAS 18002:2008** (Implementation guidance)
- **Withdrawn in March 2021** and replaced by **ISO 45001:2018**
- Under KTU 2024 syllabus, OHSAS 18001 is treated as the baseline OHSMS framework

### 2.3.2 Core Elements of OHSAS 18001:2007

- **OH&S Policy** — Top management commitment
- **Hazard Identification, Risk Assessment & Determination of Controls (HIRA)**
- **Legal & Other Requirements**
- **Objectives & Programme(s)**
- **Resources, Roles, Responsibility, Accountability & Authority**
- **Competence, Training & Awareness**
- **Communication, Participation & Consultation**
- **Documentation & Document Control**
- **Operational Control**
- **Emergency Preparedness & Response**
- **Performance Monitoring & Measurement**
- **Incident Investigation, Nonconformity, Corrective & Preventive Action**
- **Records & Records Management**
- **Audit & Management Review**

### 2.3.3 HIRA — The Heart of OHSAS 18001

Hazard identification followed by Risk Assessment using methods like:
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**
- **What-If Analysis**
- **Checklist Analysis**
- **Bow-tie Analysis**

### 2.3.4 Risk Rating Formula (Used in HIRA)

$$\text{Risk Rating (RR)} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Likelihood (L)} \times \text{Exposure (E)}$$

Or in simpler **S × L** matrix:

$$\text{Risk Level} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Likelihood}$$

Severity scale (typical 1–5):
- 1 = Negligible (no injury)
- 2 = Minor (first aid only)
- 3 = Moderate (medical treatment)
- 4 = Major (serious injury, lost time)
- 5 = Catastrophic (fatality, permanent disability)

Likelihood scale (1–5):
- 1 = Rare
- 2 = Unlikely
- 3 = Possible
- 4 = Likely
- 5 = Almost certain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Examiner Focus: Marks are often awarded for explaining the **Plan-Do-Check-Act** linkage and the **documented information** requirements across all three standards.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Statement | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan → Do → Check → Act | All three management systems |
| **Risk Score** | $RR = S \times L$ | OHS risk assessment |
| **Noise Dose (OSHA)** | $D = \frac{100}{T_c} \int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt$ | Hearing conservation |
| **TLV-TWA** | $C_{TWA} = \frac{\sum C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | Chemical exposure assessment |
| **LC₅₀ Conversion** | $1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{MW}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3$ at 25 °C, 1 atm | Toxic gas evaluation |
| **Aspect–Impact Matrix** | Significance = Magnitude × Frequency | Environmental prioritization |
| **Compliance Index** | $CI = \frac{\text{No. of compliances}}{\text{Total requirements}} \times 100$ | Legal audit |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \frac{\text{Good units from first attempt}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100$ | Quality performance |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Quality economics |

> **Note on units:** The conversion constant **24.45** comes from the **molar volume of an ideal gas at 25 °C and 1 atm (≈ 24.45 L/mol)**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In a fertilizer complex (e.g., a 1,750 MTPD ammonia + 3,000 MTPD urea plant), the integrated application of ISO 9000/14000/OHSAS 18000 directly contributes to:
- Reduced product rejection rate (lower COPQ)
- Statutory compliance with **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** consent limits
- Lower reportable accident frequency rate (LTIFR)
- Eligibility for **PESO** (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation) licenses
- Better access to **international markets** requiring ISO certifications
- Lower insurance premiums through demonstrable risk control

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step: Risk Rating Calculation (Worked Example)

This is the most commonly asked derivation-type question in KTU exams for OHSAS topics.

### Problem Statement
In the **Ammonia Storage & Handling area** of a fertilizer plant, an inspector identifies a hazard: **possible ammonia leak from a flange joint** during maintenance activity. The estimated values are:
- Severity (S) = 4 (Major — toxic exposure, possible fatality)
- Likelihood (L) = 3 (Possible — flange leaks observed 3 times in past 2 years)
- Exposure (E) = 5 (Continuous — workers present in the area throughout 8-hour shift)

Calculate the **Risk Rating (RR)** and classify the risk level.

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

$$RR = S \times L \times E$$

This is the classic **Fine-Kinney method** (or its modified version) commonly used in chemical industry risk matrices.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$\begin{aligned}
RR &= 4 \times 3 \times 5 \\
RR &= 12 \times 5 \\
RR &= 60
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Classify the Risk Level

Using the standard risk classification table:

| Risk Score Range | Risk Level | Required Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** (Acceptable) | Routine controls; monitor |
| 5 – 12 | **Moderate** (Caution) | Reduce risk; planned controls |
| 13 – 25 | **High** (Undesirable) | Corrective action immediately |
| 26 – 75 | **Very High** | Immediate corrective action, stop operation if necessary |
| 76 – 125 | **Extreme** | Stop operation, redesign |

Since RR = 60, the risk is classified as **VERY HIGH**. Immediate corrective action is required — operations must be suspended or temporarily halted.

### Step 4 — Propose Control Measures (Hierarchy of Controls)

Following the **Hierarchy of Controls** (most effective at top):

1. **Elimination** — Remove the flange joint (use welded connections)
2. **Substitution** — Use lower-pressure ammonia service where possible
3. **Engineering Controls** — Double-block-and-bleed valves, leak detectors, containment dike
4. **Administrative Controls** — Permit-to-Work system, gas testing, trained workforce
5. **PPE** — Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA), chemical-resistant suits, gas mask with NH₃ cartridge

### Step 5 — Recalculate After Control

Suppose engineering controls reduce Likelihood from 3 to 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
RR_{after} &= 4 \times 1 \times 5 \\
RR_{after} &= 20 \quad (\text{High})
\end{aligned}$$

Adding PPE reduces Exposure from 5 to 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
RR_{after\text{-}PPE} &= 4 \times 1 \times 1 \\
RR_{after\text{-}PPE} &= 4 \quad (\text{Low})
\end{aligned}$$

This demonstrates the **principle of risk reduction through multiple layers of control** (the *Swiss Cheese Model*).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: TLV-TWA Calculation for Exposure Assessment

### Problem Statement
A worker is exposed to **NO₂ (nitrogen dioxide)** at the following concentrations during an 8-hour shift:
- 2 hours at 5 ppm
- 2 hours at 3 ppm
- 3 hours at 2 ppm
- 1 hour at 1 ppm

The OSHA PEL for NO₂ is **5 ppm (8-hour TWA)**. Is the worker's exposure within permissible limits?

### Step 1 — Recall the TWA Formula

$$C_{TWA} = \frac{\sum (C_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Numerator

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum (C_i \cdot t_i) &= (5 \times 2) + (3 \times 2) + (2 \times 3) + (1 \times 1) \\
&= 10 + 6 + 6 + 1 \\
&= 23 \text{ ppm·hours}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Denominator (Total Time)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum t_i &= 2 + 2 + 3 + 1 \\
&= 8 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute TWA

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TWA} &= \frac{23}{8} \\
C_{TWA} &= 2.875 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compare with the Standard

Since $C_{TWA} = 2.875 \text{ ppm} < 5 \text{ ppm (PEL)}$, the exposure is **within permissible limits**.

> **Conclusion:** The worker is safe, but the calculation should be repeated whenever work patterns change. ISO 14001/OHSAS 18001 require this evaluation to be documented.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ISO 9000 vs ISO 14000 vs OHSAS 18000

| Parameter | ISO 9001:2015 | ISO 14001:2015 | OHSAS 18001:2007 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Title** | Quality Management Systems | Environmental Management Systems | Occupational H&S Management Systems |
| **Developing Body** | ISO/TC 176 | ISO/TC 207 | OHSAS Project Group |
| **Year of Latest Version** | 2015 | 2015 | 2007 |
| **Certifiable** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **HLS Structure** | Yes (Annex SL) | Yes (Annex SL) | Partial (legacy structure) |
| **PDCA Base** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Core Focus** | Customer satisfaction | Environmental protection | Worker safety & health |
| **Policy Name** | Quality Policy | Environmental Policy | OH&S Policy |
| **Risk Concept** | Risks & Opportunities | Risks & Opportunities | Hazards & Risks |
| **Legal Compliance** | Implicit (Clause 8.1) | Explicit (Clause 6.1.3, 9.1.2) | Explicit (Clause 4.3.1, 4.5.2) |
| **Aspect/Hazard ID** | Process mapping | Environmental aspects | Hazard identification |
| **Objectives** | Quality objectives | Environmental objectives | OH&S objectives |
| **Documentation** | Documented information | Documented information | Documents & records |
| **Continual Improvement** | Mandatory | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| **Successor Standard** | — | — | **ISO 45001:2018** (effective 2018, OHSAS withdrawn 2021) |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — A Quality Metric

COPQ is a critical performance metric in QMS and forms the basis for management review.

$$\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$$

Where:
- **Prevention Costs** — Training, quality planning, supplier capability assessment, design reviews
- **Appraisal Costs** — Inspection, testing, calibration, audits
- **Internal Failure Costs** — Scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure analysis
- **External Failure Costs** — Warranty claims, returns, recalls, customer complaints, legal liabilities

### Worked Example — Fertilizer Plant

A urea prilling plant records the following annual costs:

| Category | Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Operator training & quality planning | 8 |
| Laboratory testing & inspection | 6 |
| Off-spec product re-melting (internal) | 12 |
| Customer compensation for off-spec shipment | 15 |

Compute COPQ.

### Solution

$$\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= P + A + IF + EF \\
COPQ &= 8 + 6 + 12 + 15 \\
COPQ &= 41 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

### Interpretation
The **external failure cost (₹15L) is 36.6% of COPQ**, indicating a poor quality image in the market. Increasing **prevention costs** (training, FMEA, supplier audits) typically reduces internal and external failure costs — a classic **Pareto optimization** demonstrating the economic value of QMS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PDCA Cycle — Universal Management System Engine

This diagram shows how PDCA loops apply at the **process level** and **system level** for all three standards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[PLAN<br/>Establish objectives<br/>Identify risks and opportunities<br/>Plan processes and resources]
    nodeD[DO<br/>Implement the planned processes<br/>Provide resources<br/>Train and operate]
    nodeC[CHECK<br/>Monitor and measure<br/>Audit the system<br/>Review performance]
    nodeA[ACT<br/>Take corrective action<br/>Improve processes<br/>Update objectives]
    nodeP --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeP
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls (Most Effective → Least Effective)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeElim[ELIMINATION<br/>Physically remove the hazard]
    nodeSub[SUBSTITUTION<br/>Replace with less hazardous material or process]
    nodeEng[ENGINEERING CONTROLS<br/>Isolation, ventilation, automation, guards]
    nodeAdmin[ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS<br/>Procedures, training, signage, supervision]
    nodePPE[PPE<br/>Personal Protective Equipment]
    nodeElim --> nodeSub
    nodeSub --> nodeEng
    nodeEng --> nodeAdmin
    nodeAdmin --> nodePPE
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Integrated Management System (IMS) for Fertilizer Industry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOP[TOP MANAGEMENT LEADERSHIP]
        nodeTM[Top Management<br/>Policy Commitment and Direction]
    end

    subgraph CORE[IMS CORE PROCESSES]
        nodeQMS[QMS ISO 9001<br/>Product Quality and Customer Satisfaction]
        nodeEMS[EMS ISO 14001<br/>Environmental Aspects and Impacts]
        nodeOHS[OHSAS 18001<br/>Hazards and Risks]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT[SUPPORT FUNCTIONS]
        nodeHR[Human Resources<br/>Training and Competence]
        nodeProc[Procurement<br/>Supplier Evaluation]
        nodeMaint[Maintenance<br/>Asset Integrity]
        nodeLab[Laboratory<br/>Testing and Calibration]
    end

    subgraph CHECK[CHECK AND REVIEW]
        nodeAudit[Internal Audit]
        nodeMR[Management Review]
        nodeCom[Compliance Evaluation]
    end

    subgraph IMP[IMPROVEMENT]
        nodeCAPA[Corrective and Preventive Action]
        nodeCI[Continual Improvement]
    end

    nodeTM --> nodeQMS
    nodeTM --> nodeEMS
    nodeTM --> nodeOHS

    nodeHR --> nodeQMS
    nodeHR --> nodeEMS
    nodeHR --> nodeOHS

    nodeProc --> nodeQMS
    nodeMaint --> nodeOHS
    nodeLab --> nodeEMS
    nodeLab --> nodeQMS

    nodeQMS --> nodeAudit
    nodeEMS --> nodeAudit
    nodeOHS --> nodeAudit

    nodeAudit --> nodeMR
    nodeCom --> nodeMR
    nodeMR --> nodeCAPA
    nodeCAPA --> nodeCI

    nodeCI -. Feedback .- nodeTM
```

---

## 4.4 Risk Rating Decision Flow (Fine-Kinney / S × L Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Start: Identify Hazard]
    nodeAssess[Assess Severity S 1 to 5]
    nodeLike[Assess Likelihood L 1 to 5]
    nodeExp[Assess Exposure E 1 to 5 if used]
    nodeCalc[Calculate Risk Rating RR]
    nodeClassify{Risk Level?}
    nodeLow[Low 1 to 4<br/>Accept and Monitor]
    nodeMod[Moderate 5 to 12<br/>Plan Control Measures]
    nodeHigh[High 13 to 25<br/>Implement Controls Soon]
    nodeVery[Very High 26 to 75<br/>Immediate Action]
    nodeExt[Extreme 76 to 125<br/>Stop Operations]

    nodeStart --> nodeAssess
    nodeAssess --> nodeLike
    nodeLike --> nodeExp
    nodeExp --> nodeCalc
    nodeCalc --> nodeClassify
    nodeClassify -->|Score 1-4| nodeLow
    nodeClassify -->|Score 5-12| nodeMod
    nodeClassify -->|Score 13-25| nodeHigh
    nodeClassify -->|Score 26-75| nodeVery
    nodeClassify -->|Score 76-125| nodeExt
```

---

## 4.5 ISO 9001 / 14001 / OHSAS 18001 — Clause Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLAUSE[ANNEX SL CLAUSE 4 TO 10]
        nodeC4[Clause 4 Context of Organization]
        nodeC5[Clause 5 Leadership]
        nodeC6[Clause 6 Planning]
        nodeC7[Clause 7 Support]
        nodeC8[Clause 8 Operation]
        nodeC9[Clause 9 Performance Evaluation]
        nodeC10[Clause 10 Improvement]
    end

    subgraph APPL[STANDARD SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS]
        nodeQ[ISO 9001 Customer and Product Quality Focus]
        nodeE[ISO 14001 Environmental Aspects and Impacts]
        nodeO[OHSAS 18001 Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment]
    end

    nodeC4 --> nodeQ
    nodeC4 --> nodeE
    nodeC4 --> nodeO

    nodeC5 --> nodeQ
    nodeC5 --> nodeE
    nodeC5 --> nodeO

    nodeC6 --> nodeQ
    nodeC6 --> nodeE
    nodeC6 --> nodeO

    nodeC7 --> nodeQ
    nodeC7 --> nodeE
    nodeC7 --> nodeO

    nodeC8 --> nodeQ
    nodeC8 --> nodeE
    nodeC8 --> nodeO

    nodeC9 --> nodeQ
    nodeC9 --> nodeE
    nodeC9 --> nodeO

    nodeC10 --> nodeQ
    nodeC10 --> nodeE
    nodeC10 --> nodeO
```

---

## 4.6 Certification / Audit Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeGap[Stage 1: Gap Analysis<br/>Compare current state with standard]
    nodeDocs[Stage 2: Documentation<br/>Manual procedures and records]
    nodeImp[Stage 3: Implementation<br/>At least 3 months of operating evidence]
    nodeInt[Stage 4: Internal Audit and Management Review]
    nodePre[Stage 5: Pre-assessment Audit optional]
    nodeCert[Stage 6: Stage 1 Certification Audit<br/>Documentation Review]
    nodeCert2[Stage 7: Stage 2 Certification Audit<br/>On-site Effectiveness]
    nodeAward[Certificate Awarded<br/>Valid 3 years]
    nodeSurv[Surveillance Audit 1 and 2<br/>Year 1 and Year 2]
    nodeRecert[Re-certification Audit<br/>End of Year 3]

    nodeGap --> nodeDocs
    nodeDocs --> nodeImp
    nodeImp --> nodeInt
    nodeInt --> nodePre
    nodePre --> nodeCert
    nodeCert --> nodeCert2
    nodeCert2 --> nodeAward
    nodeAward --> nodeSurv
    nodeSurv --> nodeRecert
    nodeRecert -. New cycle .- nodeGap
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**List any three Quality Management Principles as per ISO 9000:2015. State their significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The seven Quality Management Principles (any three required):
1. **Customer Focus** — The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed expectations. Significance: Drives customer retention and business growth.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction. Significance: Ensures alignment of organizational objectives.
3. **Process Approach** — Activities are managed as interrelated processes forming a coherent system. Significance: Improves efficiency by optimizing each process and their interactions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "Customer satisfaction" instead of "Customer focus" and miss listing — they write only one or two principles. Always write **at least 3** with brief explanation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is an Environmental Aspect? Give two examples from a fertilizer plant.**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** An *environmental aspect* is an element of an organization's activities, products, or services that **can interact with the environment**. The corresponding change to the environment is termed an *environmental impact*.

**Examples from fertilizer plant:**
1. **Emission of ammonia vapour from the urea prilling tower** — Aspect; impacts air quality (Impact).
2. **Discharge of phosphogypsum slurry into the gypsum pond** — Aspect; impacts soil and groundwater (Impact).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students confuse **Aspect (cause)** with **Impact (effect)**. Always remember: *Aspect = the cause, Impact = the consequence.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** as applied to ISO 9001:2015. Explain how each phase is implemented in a fertilizer manufacturing unit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **core requirements of OHSAS 18001:2007** with reference to hazard identification, risk assessment, and emergency preparedness. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The PDCA cycle is the foundational management system framework introduced by **W. Edwards Deming** and adopted by ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and OHSAS 18001 for systematic management of processes.

**PLAN Phase (2 marks):**
- Establish quality objectives aligned with customer requirements
- Identify risks and opportunities
- Plan changes and resource allocation
- *Example:* In a urea plant, planning includes setting N-content ≥ 46%, biuret ≤ 1.0%, moisture ≤ 0.5%

**DO Phase (2 marks):**
- Implement the processes as planned
- Provide trained manpower and resources
- Execute production, document work instructions
- *Example:* Operate the prilling tower with controlled melt temperature (140 °C), tower air flow, and prill size monitoring

**CHECK Phase (1 mark):**
- Monitor and measure product specifications against defined quality parameters
- Conduct internal audits at planned intervals
- *Example:* Lab analysis every 2 hours; lot-wise N, biuret, moisture testing

**ACT Phase (1 mark):**
- Address nonconformities via corrective actions
- Update procedures based on findings
- Pursue continual improvement (e.g., reduce biuret deviation via improved cooling)

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Hazard Identification (3 marks):**
Three categories of hazards:
1. **Physical** — Mechanical (rotating equipment, conveyors), Electrical (HT/LT panels), Noise (compressors)
2. **Chemical** — Ammonia, HF, H₂SO₄, urea dust
3. **Biological/Ergonomic** — Heat stress in reactor areas, repetitive motion disorders

Methods of identification:
- Hazard checklists, HAZOP, FMEA, What-If analysis
- Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
- Worker feedback and incident reports

**Risk Assessment (2 marks):**
- **Risk = Severity × Likelihood** (Fine-Kinney method)
- Prioritize risks; identify high-risk activities (e.g., confined space entry in ammonia storage)
- Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls** to reduce risk

**Emergency Preparedness & Response (2 marks):**
- On-site Emergency Plan (as per MSIHC Rules, 1989)
- Off-site Emergency Plan (for major hazard units)
- Mock drills quarterly for ammonia leak, fire, explosion
- Emergency equipment: SCBAs, water spray systems, fire hydrant network
- Communication system, assembly points, trained Emergency Response Team (ERT)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **environmental management system (EMS) requirements** of ISO 14001:2015. Describe in detail the steps for **identification of environmental aspects and impacts** in a fertilizer plant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is an **Integrated Management System (IMS)**? Discuss the benefits and challenges of integrating ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and OHSAS 18001 in a fertilizer manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**EMS Requirements of ISO 14001:2015 (3.5 marks):**
The 10 clauses under Annex SL require:
1. **Environmental Policy** — Top management commitment, pollution prevention, legal compliance, continual improvement
2. **Environmental Aspects** — Identification of aspects and impacts
3. **Legal & Other Requirements** — Identification, access, communication
4. **Objectives & Targets** — Measurable, at relevant functions
5. **Operational Control** — Procedures for significant aspects
6. **Monitoring & Measurement** — Calibration, performance tracking
7. **Compliance Evaluation** — Periodic evaluation
8. **Audit & Management Review**
9. **Documentation & Records**
10. **Continual Improvement**

**Steps for Aspect–Impact Identification (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define scope (entire plant boundaries)

**Step 2:** Compile activity register (production, utilities, storage, transportation, waste handling)

**Step 3:** Identify aspects for each activity under three conditions:
- Normal operations
- Abnormal/Start-up/Shut-down
- Emergency conditions

**Step 4:** Identify impacts:
- Air, water, land, noise, biodiversity, community
- Acute vs chronic; reversible vs irreversible

**Step 5:** Evaluate significance — typically using a scoring system:
$$\text{Significance Score} = \text{Magnitude} \times \text{Frequency} \times \text{Regulatory Concern}$$

**Step 6:** Identify significant aspects requiring control

**Examples in fertilizer plant (1 mark):**
| Activity | Aspect | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Urea prilling | Urea dust emission | Air pollution, PM2.5 |
| Cooling tower blowdown | Discharge of hot water | Thermal pollution in river |
| H₂SO₄ plant | SO₂ release | Acid rain |
| Catalyst disposal | Heavy metals (Ni, Mo) | Soil & water contamination |
| Ammonia storage | Fugitive NH₃ emission | Toxic exposure, vegetation damage |

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Definition of IMS (2 marks):**
An **Integrated Management System (IMS)** is a unified management system that integrates the requirements of two or more management system standards (e.g., ISO 9001 + ISO 14001 + OHSAS 18001) into a single coherent framework with common policies, processes, documentation, and audits.

**Benefits of IMS in Fertilizer Industry (3 marks):**
1. **Reduced duplication** — Single set of procedures instead of three parallel ones
2. **Cost savings** — Combined audits (single audit team covering all three standards)
3. **Holistic risk management** — Quality, environment, and safety risks addressed in unison
4. **Improved communication** — Single management review
5. **Better stakeholder confidence** — Shows integrated commitment to Q-E-S
6. **Reduced certification cost** — Single certification cycle
7. **Improved compliance** — Easier to track and demonstrate compliance with multiple regulations

**Challenges (2 marks):**
1. **Resource-intensive initial integration** — Significant time investment
2. **Cultural differences** — Quality, environment, and safety teams traditionally work in silos
3. **Conflict in objectives** — e.g., maximizing production vs. environmental compliance
4. **Complexity in documentation** — Need careful version control
5. **Training needs** — Multi-disciplinary competency required

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often write IMS as merely "combining three standards" without emphasizing the **common Annex SL structure**, **common documented information**, and **combined audit approach**. Always stress the **single set of procedures** and **unified management review**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concepts — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [ ] **ISO 9001:2015** = Quality Management — focus on **customer satisfaction**
- [ ] **ISO 14001:2015** = Environmental Management — focus on **environmental aspects & impacts**
- [ ] **OHSAS 18001:2007** = Occupational H&S Management — focus on **hazard identification & risk assessment**
- [ ] All three standards follow **Annex SL High-Level Structure (Clauses 4–10)** and **PDCA cycle**
- [ ] **Aspect vs Impact** — Aspect = cause; Impact = effect on environment
- [ ] **Hazard vs Risk** — Hazard = potential source of harm; Risk = combination of likelihood and severity
- [ ] **Risk Rating Formula**: $RR = S \times L$ (or $S \times L \times E$ in Fine-Kinney)
- [ ] **7 Quality Management Principles** of ISO 9000:2015 (Customer focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process approach, Improvement, Evidence-based, Relationship management)
- [ ] **PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act (Deming Cycle)
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE
- [ ] **TLV-TWA formula**: $C_{TWA} = \frac{\sum C_i t_i}{\sum t_i}$
- [ ] **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure
- [ ] **OHSAS 18001 was withdrawn in March 2021** and replaced by **ISO 45001:2018**
- [ ] **ISO/TC 176** develops ISO 9000 series; **ISO/TC 207** develops ISO 14000 series
- [ ] **IMS** = Integrated Management System combining two or more standards
- [ ] **On-site Emergency Plan** is mandated under **MSIHC Rules, 1989** in India
- [ ] Fertilizer industry must comply with **Factories Act, EP Act, Water Act, Air Act, Hazardous Waste Rules**
- [ ] **PESO** (formerly CCE) governs storage & transport of explosives/flammables
- [ ] **Consent to Operate** from SPCB is mandatory before production startup
- [ ] **Documentation** now called "**Documented Information**" (per Annex SL, replaced "documents & records")
- [ ] **Management Review** is mandatory under all three standards
- [ ] **Internal Audit** must cover all three standards separately or jointly in IMS
- [ ] **Continual Improvement** is the ultimate goal of all three management systems

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference for KTU Students:** This topic appears in PESFT633 Module 2 and is directly tested in **KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)** under Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) sections. Most questions in Part A focus on **definitions** and **basic principles**, while Part B questions test **practical application in fertilizer industry context**, **PDCA linkage**, and **integration aspects**. Always support your answer with **fertilizer-industry examples** (NH₃, urea, H₂SO₄, HF, phosphoric acid plants) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Hazard Analysis and Risk Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Process Hazard Analysis and Risk Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a systematic, structured, and comprehensive evaluation of the potential hazards associated with an industrial process, designed to identify, evaluate, and control the risks of accidental releases of hazardous materials, energy surges, or operational failures that could result in injuries, property damage, or environmental degradation.* In the context of the **fertilizer industry**, PHA is mandated and routinely applied to processes handling materials such as **anhydrous ammonia (NH₃)**, **nitric acid (HNO₃)**, **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)**, **hydrogen (H₂)**, and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — all of which are regulated under standards like **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** and **EPA Risk Management Programs (RMP)**. ### Conceptual Analogy: "The Road Trip Risk Assessment" Imagine you are planning a long road trip from **Kochi to Kanyakumari**. - **Hazard** = A sharp hairpin turn on a hillside road (the inherent dangerous condition that exists) - **Risk** = The probability that you will actually have an accident at that turn, multiplied by how bad the accident could be - **PHA** = Sitting down with a map, identifying every dangerous turn, every blind spot, every fatigued-driver risk, and the weather forecast - **Risk Management** = Deciding to drive slowly at the hairpin, using fog lights, taking a rest break, and installing better brakes You don't eliminate the hairpin (you can't move the mountain), but you *manage* your exposure to it. That is what PHA does for a fertilizer plant. ## 1.2 What is Risk Management? > [!NOTE] > **Risk Management** is the coordinated set of activities used to **direct and control an organization with respect to risk**. It involves identification, assessment, prioritization, and application of resources to minimize, monitor, and control the probability and/or impact of unfortunate events. ## 1.3 Hazard vs. Risk — The Critical Distinction | Term | Definition | Example in Fertilizer Plant | |------|------------|------------------------------| | **Hazard** | An inherent physical or chemical property with the potential to cause harm | 2000 kg of anhydrous ammonia stored at 16 bar pressure | | **Risk** | The combination of the likelihood of an event and its severity | A 1-in-10,000/year chance of a catastrophic release affecting 500 m radius | | **Consequence** | The direct outcome of an event | Toxic vapor cloud, multi-fatality, ₹50 Crore property loss | | **Probability** | The likelihood of occurrence per unit time | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year | > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Pearl:** Examiners LOVE asking the difference between *hazard* and *risk*. A *hazard* is intrinsic; a *risk* is a function of exposure, probability, and consequence. ## 1.4 Why PHA and Risk Management Matter in the Fertilizer Industry The fertilizer industry is categorized as a **Major Accident Hazard (MAH)** industry due to: 1. **Inventory of toxic materials** (NH₃, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HF) 2. **High operating pressures** (up to 1000 bar in urea synthesis) 3. **High operating temperatures** (up to 950 °C in primary reformer) 4. **Corrosive and reactive chemicals** (mixed acid, hot carbonate solutions) 5. **Runaway reaction potential** (nitration, sulfonation) Historical incidents such as **Bhopal (1984)**, **Texas City (2005)**, and **West Fertilizer Co. Explosion (2013)** underscore the catastrophic potential of inadequate PHA. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Standard Reference:** *The threshold quantity (TQ) for regulated substances under OSHA PSM is **2500 lb (1134 kg)** for anhydrous ammonia and **1000 lb (454 kg)** for chlorine — fertilizers plants routinely exceed these quantities.* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Standard PHA Workflow (KTU-Recommended Sequence) The structured approach adopted across KTU 2024 Scheme curricula follows this **7-step logic ladder**: 1. **Define the Scope & Boundaries** — Identify the node, system, or process unit under study 2. **Gather Process Safety Information (PSI)** — P&IDs, PFDs, MSDS, operating procedures 3. **Hazard Identification** — What can go wrong? (using HAZOP, What-If, Checklist) 4. **Consequence Analysis** — How bad could it be? (using dispersion, fire, explosion modeling) 5. **Frequency/Probability Assessment** — How likely? (using historical data, FTA, ETA) 6. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare against company risk tolerance criteria (Risk Matrix) 7. **Risk Treatment & Documentation** — Implement controls, document findings ## 2.2 PHA Methodologies — A Comparative Study > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently tests the *selection* of PHA technique for a given scenario. The choice depends on **project phase**, **process complexity**, and **available data**. | # | Method | Best Used For | Strength | Limitation | |---|--------|---------------|----------|------------| | 1 | **Checklist** | Mature, repetitive operations | Quick, simple | Only finds known problems | | 2 | **What-If Analysis** | Early design / brainstorming | Flexible, low cost | Depends on team expertise | | 3 | **HAZOP** | Continuous chemical processes (urea, ammonia plants) | Highly systematic, thorough | Time-intensive, requires expert team | | 4 | **FMEA** | Mechanical/equipment failure analysis | Equipment-focused | Misses procedural errors | | 5 | **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Complex accident pathways with multiple causes | Quantitative, logical | Requires failure rate data | | 6 | **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Post-initiator consequence modeling | Maps outcome branches | Needs credible initiator frequency | | 7 | **Bow-Tie** | Operational risk visualization | Combines FTA + ETA | Visualization can oversimplify | | 8 | **LOPA** (Layer of Protection Analysis) | Verifying Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) | Semi-quantitative | Requires IPL data | ## 2.3 The Risk Equation (Foundation of All Risk Calculations) The fundamental risk equation used across the chemical process industry (CPI) is: $$R = f \times C$$ Where: - $R$ = **Risk** (a measure of the expected loss per unit time, e.g., ₹/year or fatalities/year) - $f$ = **Frequency** of the hazardous event (events per year, dimensionless per year) - $C$ = **Consequence** (magnitude of harm, in fatalities, ₹ loss, or environmental impact units) A more granular decomposition expands $f$ as a product of initiating event frequency and probability of failure of protective layers: $$R = f_{\text{initiator}} \times P_{\text{IPL}_1} \times P_{\text{IPL}_2} \times P_{\text{IPL}_3} \times \cdots \times P_{\text{IPL}_n} \times C$$ Where $\text{IPL}_i$ represents the **i-th Independent Protection Layer**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Note:** Memorize the formulas below — they appear every KTU cycle in Part A and Part B questions. | # | Formula | Description / Units | Application | |---|---------|---------------------|-------------| | 1 | $R = f \times C$ | Risk = Frequency × Consequence | Baseline risk | | 2 | $R = P \times S$ | Where P = Probability (per year), S = Severity (impact) | Risk matrix construction | | 3 | $f_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | Total system failure frequency (sum of independent events) | Parallel reliability | | 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential distribution) | Equipment failure | | 5 | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (hours) | Reliability engineering | | 6 | $f_{\text{AND}} = f_A \cdot f_B$ | AND gate frequency (both must fail) | Fault Tree AND gate | | 7 | $f_{\text{OR}} = f_A + f_B$ | OR gate frequency (either fails) | Fault Tree OR gate | | 8 | $D = \text{IDLH concentration} \div \text{ERPG}_2$ | Danger index (toxicity metric) | Ammonia release zones | | 9 | $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}})$ | Safety Integrity Level equation | IEC 61511 | | 10 | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{initial}} \times \prod (1 - E_i)$ | Residual risk after controls | Risk reduction verification | ### 2.4.1 SIL (Safety Integrity Level) Reference Table | SIL Level | Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{\text{avg}}$) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | |-----------|---------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------| | SIL 1 | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | | SIL 2 | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | | SIL 3 | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10,000 | | SIL 4 | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 | ## 2.5 The ALARP Principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) > [!TIP] > **ALARP** is the philosophical and legal cornerstone of risk management in UK, EU, and Indian PSM regulations. It dictates that residual risk must be reduced *until the cost of further reduction is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained.* $$\text{Risk is "ALARP"} \iff \frac{\text{Cost of further risk reduction}}{\text{Risk reduction benefit}} \gg 1 \text{ (grossly disproportionate)}$$ ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design Phase:** PHA influences equipment specification, spacing, and layout - **Operating Phase:** Drives preventive maintenance, operating procedures, and emergency response - **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory under OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, and Indian Factories Act (Section 7A, 41B) - **Insurance & Liability:** Insurers demand documented PHA before underwriting major hazard facilities - **Investment Decisions:** Determines whether a greenfield fertilizer plant is sited near populated areas # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: HAZOP Deviation Analysis Procedure The HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) study is the **gold-standard PHA technique** for fertilizer processes. Below is its complete procedural derivation, step-by-step, as required for KTU 14-mark questions. ### Step 1: Define the Study Objective and Scope $$\text{Scope} = \{ \text{Node}_1, \text{Node}_2, \ldots, \text{Node}_n \}$$ Each "node" is a process section (e.g., ammonia synthesis loop, urea reactor, nitric acid absorption tower) where a *design intention* can be stated. ### Step 2: Form the Multidisciplinary Team A HAZOP team typically consists of: $$\text{Team} = \{ \text{Process Engineer}, \text{Operations Lead}, \text{Instrumentation Eng.}, \text{Safety Officer}, \text{Maintenance Rep.} \}$$ > **Logic:** A single engineer cannot catch all deviations — fresh eyes from different disciplines catch different failure modes. ### Step 3: Identify the Process Variable and Apply Guide Words The core HAZOP equation is: $$\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word} + \text{Process Variable}$$ The standard KTU-recommended guide words are: | Guide Word | Meaning | Example for Urea Reactor | |------------|---------|---------------------------| | **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of intention | **NO** flow → blocked line | | **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **MORE** pressure → over-pressurization | | **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **LESS** temperature → incomplete reaction | | **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **REVERSE** flow → backflow into feed tank | | **AS WELL AS** | Additional condition | Flow AS WELL AS water → two-phase flow | | **PART OF** | Less than complete | PART OF composition → NH₃ deficient | | **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | OTHER THAN material → wrong reactant | ### Step 4: For Each Deviation, Identify Causes For **"MORE Pressure"** in ammonia synthesis converter: $$\text{Causes} = \{ \text{Compressor surge}, \text{Blockage in downstream cooler}, \text{Emergency cooling failure}, \text{Exothermic reaction runaway} \}$$ ### Step 5: Determine Consequences $$C(\text{More Pressure}) = \{ \text{Rupture disc lift}, \text{Vessel over-pressure}, \text{Toxic NH}_3 \text{ release}, \text{Explosion if ignited} \}$$ ### Step 6: Identify Existing Safeguards $$\text{Safeguards} = \{ \text{PSV relief valve}, \text{High-pressure interlock at 200 bar}, \text{Rupture disc at 220 bar}, \text{Emergency shutdown system} \}$$ ### Step 7: Estimate Risk Using the Risk Matrix The risk score is computed as: $$\text{Risk Score} = P \times S$$ Where: - $P$ = Likelihood rating (1 to 5) - $S$ = Severity rating (1 to 5) ### Step 8: Recommend Additional Safeguards if Risk is Unacceptable $$\text{If } R > R_{\text{tolerable}} \implies \text{Add IPL} \implies \text{Recompute } R_{\text{residual}}$$ ### Step 9: Document and Follow Up All actions are tracked in a **HAZOP Worksheet** with assigned responsibilities and deadlines. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Risk Matrix Construction (Standard 5×5 KTU Format) A standard 5×5 risk matrix used in KTU practical assessments is constructed as follows: | Severity → / Likelihood ↓ | **S1 (Negligible)** | **S2 (Minor)** | **S3 (Moderate)** | **S4 (Major)** | **S5 (Catastrophic)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **L5 (Frequent)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** | | **L4 (Probable)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | **16** | 20 | | **L3 (Occasional)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | | **L2 (Remote)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | | **L1 (Improbable)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Color Coding Convention (For Exam Diagrams):** > - Green: Score 1–4 → **Acceptable** > - Yellow: Score 5–12 → **ALARP region** (manage with controls) > - Red: Score 15–25 → **Unacceptable** (must reduce) ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Fault Tree OR-Gate Frequency **Problem:** A urea reactor has two independent cooling water pumps. Pump A fails with frequency $f_A = 2 \times 10^{-3}$/year. Pump B fails with frequency $f_B = 3 \times 10^{-3}$/year. Compute total system failure frequency if loss of cooling occurs when *either* pump fails. **Step-by-step Solution:** $$\text{Given: } f_A = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}, \quad f_B = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$\text{Topology: OR gate} \implies f_{\text{total}} = f_A + f_B$$ $$f_{\text{total}} = (2 \times 10^{-3}) + (3 \times 10^{-3}) \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$f_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **Interpretation:** Cooling system fails once every 200 years on average. If consequence = ₹10 Crore per incident: $$R = f \times C = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (\text{₹}10 \times 10^7) = \text{₹}5 \times 10^5 \text{ per year} = \text{₹}5 \text{ Lakh/year}$$ ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Risk Reduction by Adding a Protection Layer **Problem:** Initial risk $R_i = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ fatalities/year. After installing a SIL 2 safety instrumented function (PFD$_{\text{avg}} = 10^{-2}$), what is the residual risk? **Solution:** $$R_{\text{residual}} = R_i \times \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$$ $$R_{\text{residual}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (10^{-2})$$ $$R_{\text{residual}} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ fatalities/year}$$ **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** $$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1}{10^{-2}} = 100$$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** A SIL 2 system reduces risk by a factor of 100, taking risk from the "intolerable" zone to the "ALARP" zone. ## 3.5 SIL Determination from Target Risk Reduction If tolerable risk is $R_{\text{tol}} = 10^{-6}$/year and initiator frequency is $f_i = 10^{-2}$/year, the required PFD is: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{required}} = \frac{R_{\text{tol}}}{f_i} = \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-2}} = 10^{-4}$$ $$\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD}) = -\log_{10}(10^{-4}) = 4$$ **Wait** — but $10^{-4}$ falls in the **SIL 3** band (per IEC 61511). So this SIF must be designed to SIL 3. ## 3.6 Event Tree Branch Probability Computation **Problem:** A release of 1000 kg of ammonia can lead to 3 outcomes: - **Path 1:** Immediate ignition → Pool fire (probability of ignition = 0.05) - **Path 2:** Delayed ignition → Flash fire (probability = 0.20) - **Path 3:** No ignition → Toxic vapor cloud (probability = 0.75) Compute branch frequencies given initiator $f_i = 1 \times 10^{-4}$/year. **Solution:** $$f_1 = f_i \times P_{\text{immediate ignition}} = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$f_2 = f_i \times P_{\text{delayed ignition}} = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.20 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$f_3 = f_i \times P_{\text{no ignition}} = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.75 = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **Verification:** Sum should equal $f_i$: $$f_1 + f_2 + f_3 = (5 + 20 + 75) \times 10^{-6} = 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1} = f_i \quad \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The PHA Workflow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Define Scope] --> B[Gather Process Safety Information] B --> C[Form Multidisciplinary Team] C --> D{Select PHA Method} D --> D1[Checklist] D --> D2[What-If] D --> D3[HAZOP] D --> D4[FMEA] D --> D5[FTA / ETA] D --> D6[Bow-Tie] D1 --> E[Identify Deviations] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E D6 --> E E --> F[Identify Causes] F --> G[Assess Consequences] G --> H[Estimate Frequency] H --> I[Calculate Risk Score] I --> J{Risk Acceptable?} J -->|Yes| K[Document and Close] J -->|No| L[Recommend Safeguards] L --> M[Implement IPLs] M --> I K --> N[Periodic Re-PHA] N --> A ``` ## 4.2 Bow-Tie Diagram: Ammonia Release Scenario ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Left Side: Threats] -->|Causes| B1((TOP EVENT
NH3 Release)) B1 -->|Consequences| C1[Right Side: Outcomes] A1 --> A2[Overpressure] A1 --> A3[Corrosion] A1 --> A4[Gasket Failure] A1 --> A5[Operator Error] C1 --> C2[Vapor Cloud] C1 --> C3[Pool Fire] C1 --> C4[Toxic Exposure] P1[/Preventive Controls/] -.->|Reduces| A1 P1 --> P11[Pressure Relief Valve] P1 --> P12[Corrosion Monitoring] P1 --> P13[Preventive Maintenance] P1 --> P14[Operator Training] P2[/Mitigative Controls/] -.->|Reduces| C1 P2 --> P21[Water Curtain] P2 --> P22[Fire Deluge] P2 --> P23[Emergency Shutdown] P2 --> P24[Evacuation Plan] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Bow-Tie:** The **left wing** shows threats with **preventive controls** (stop the event). The **central knot** is the *top event* (loss of containment). The **right wing** shows consequences with **mitigative controls** (limit the damage). ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Risk Management Decision Tree (ALARP) ```mermaid flowchart TD R0[Initial Risk Identified] --> R1{Is Risk
As Low As
Reasonably Practicable?} R1 -->|Yes, ALARP Demonstrated| R2[Risk Accepted] R1 -->|No, Further Reduction Possible| R3[Cost-Benefit Analysis] R3 --> R4{Cost Grossly
Disproportionate
to Benefit?} R4 -->|Yes| R2 R4 -->|No| R5[Implement Additional Control] R5 --> R6[Recompute Residual Risk] R6 --> R1 R2 --> R7[Document Decision] R7 --> R8[Monitor and Review] R8 --> R9{New Hazard
Identified?} R9 -->|Yes| R0 R9 -->|No| R10[Operation Continues] ``` ## 4.4 HAZOP Node-Worksheet Structure (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Node[HAZOP Node: Ammonia Storage Bullet Tank] --> T1[Design Intention:
Store 5000 MT NH3 at 16 bar, -33 deg C] T1 --> P1[Process Variable: PRESSURE] T1 --> P2[Process Variable: TEMPERATURE] T1 --> P3[Process Variable: LEVEL] T1 --> P4[Process Variable: FLOW] P1 --> P1a[Deviation: MORE Pressure] P1 --> P1b[Deviation: LESS Pressure] P1a --> P1a1[Causes: Refrigeration failure] P1a1 --> P1a2[Consequence: Pressure relief, vapor release] P1a2 --> P1a3[Safeguards: PSV, Rupture Disc, ESD] P1a3 --> P1a4[Action: Install additional high-high alarm] ``` ## 4.5 FTA Fault Tree Schematic: "Loss of Cooling in Urea Reactor" ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP((TOP EVENT
Loss of Cooling in Urea Reactor)) --> OR1{OR} OR1 --> E1[Cooling Pump A Fails] OR1 --> E2[Cooling Pump B Fails] OR1 --> E3[Cooling Water Supply Failure] E1 --> AND1{AND} AND1 --> E1a[Motor Failure] AND1 --> E1b[Power Supply Loss] E3 --> OR2{OR} OR2 --> E3a[Header Rupture] OR2 --> E3b[Valve Closure Error] OR2 --> E3c[Make-up Water Pump Trip] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between a **hazard** and a **risk** in a fertilizer process plant. Provide one example for each from an ammonia storage facility.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **hazard** is an intrinsic physical, chemical, or operational property that has the potential to cause harm, while a **risk** is the quantified combination of the *likelihood* of the hazardous event occurring and the *severity* of its consequence. > > **Example (Ammonia Storage):** > - **Hazard:** The presence of 5000 MT of anhydrous ammonia under 16 bar pressure (the property is *toxicity and pressure energy*). > - **Risk:** The estimated probability of 1 in 10,000 years of a major release causing fatality, weighted by the consequence severity (e.g., ₹50 Crore loss or 10 fatalities). > > *(1 mark for definition of hazard, 1 mark for definition of risk, 1 mark for example distinction)* --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *List any **six HAZOP guide words** with one-line meaning each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | S.No. | Guide Word | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intention | | 2 | **MORE** | Quantitative increase in the process variable | | 3 | **LESS** | Quantitative decrease in the process variable | | 4 | **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of the design intent | | 5 | **AS WELL AS** | An additional activity occurs along with the intended one | | 6 | **PART OF** | Only part of the design intent is achieved | | 7 | **OTHER THAN** | A completely different outcome occurs | *(0.5 mark per correct guide word; any 6 = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — HAZOP Questions:** > - Do NOT write a *theoretical essay*. Examiners want a **node-based deviation table** with causes, consequences, and safeguards clearly tabulated. > - Forgetting to **recommend at least one action** per major deviation is a common 2-mark loss. > - Citing **guide words incorrectly** (e.g., confusing "REVERSE" with "NO") will lose 1 mark per error. ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **7-step methodology of Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** in the fertilizer industry with a flowchart. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A nitric acid plant has a storage tank of 60% HNO₃. Construct a **HAZOP study for the deviation "MORE Temperature"** at the suction line of the HNO₃ transfer pump. List at least 3 causes, 3 consequences, and 3 safeguards. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **(a) Seven-Step PHA Methodology:** 1. **Define Scope:** Identify process boundaries and study objectives (e.g., urea reactor, NH₃ storage). 2. **Gather PSI:** Collect Process Safety Information — P&IDs, PFDs, MSDS, operating procedures, equipment specs. 3. **Hazard Identification:** Use HAZOP, What-If, Checklist, or FMEA to identify "what can go wrong." 4. **Consequence Analysis:** Model the physical, chemical, and toxicological outcomes (using ALOHA, PHAST software). 5. **Frequency Assessment:** Estimate probability of occurrence using historical failure data or FTA. 6. **Risk Evaluation:** Compute $R = f \times C$ and plot on a risk matrix; compare to tolerable risk. 7. **Risk Treatment:** Implement IPLs (alarms, interlocks, PSVs, procedures); document and schedule revalidation. *(1 mark per step explained; 7 marks for full 7 steps)* **(b) HAZOP Node: HNO₃ Transfer Pump Suction Line — Deviation "MORE Temperature"** | Element | Description | |---|---| | **Design Intention** | Maintain HNO₃ suction line at 25–40 °C to prevent decomposition of 60% acid | | **Deviation** | MORE Temperature (i.e., temperature exceeds 40 °C) | | **Cause 1** | Cooling water failure in line cooler | | **Cause 2** | Pump cavitation due to low NPSH (hot acid from upstream) | | **Cause 3** | Sun exposure on uninsulated outdoor piping | | **Consequence 1** | Thermal decomposition of HNO₃ → release of NO₂ (brown toxic gas) | | **Consequence 2** | Increased corrosion rate of carbon steel pipe | | **Consequence 3** | Pump seal damage leading to acid leak | | **Existing Safeguard 1** | Temperature indicator with high alarm at 45 °C | | **Existing Safeguard 2** | Insulated jacketed piping with cooling coil | | **Existing Safeguard 3** | Emergency shutdown valve on discharge | | **Recommended Action** | Install high-high temperature interlock at 50 °C linked to pump trip; add redundant temperature transmitter (SIL 2). | *(1 mark for design intention; 1 mark for causes; 1 mark for consequences; 1 mark for safeguards; 1 mark for action; 2 marks for clarity and engineering depth)* --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** What is **Risk Management**? Explain the **risk matrix concept** with a 5×5 matrix and describe the **ALARP principle** in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A urea reactor cooling system has two pumps in parallel (P and Q). Pump P failure rate = $2 \times 10^{-3}$/year, Pump Q failure rate = $3 \times 10^{-3}$/year. Compute: (i) The total system failure frequency *(3 marks)* (ii) The risk in ₹/year if each failure results in ₹2 Crore loss *(2 marks)* (iii) After installing a SIL 2 SIF with PFD = $10^{-2}$, compute the residual risk *(2 marks)* **Model Answer:** **(a) Risk Management & ALARP:** **Risk Management** is the systematic application of policies, procedures, and practices to the tasks of identifying, analyzing, evaluating, treating, and monitoring risk. *(2 marks)* **5×5 Risk Matrix** (Likelihood × Severity): | Sev → / Lik ↓ | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | L5 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | | L4 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | | L3 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | | L2 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | | L1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | - Scores 1–4 = **Acceptable (Green)** - Scores 5–12 = **ALARP region (Yellow)** — reduce if practicable - Scores 15–25 = **Intolerable (Red)** — must reduce *(2 marks for matrix)* **ALARP Principle:** - **Definition:** Risk must be reduced to *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* — the "grossly disproportionate" test. - **Test:** Apply further control UNLESS the cost (in money, time, effort) is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained. - **Decision Rule:** $\frac{\text{Cost of additional safeguard}}{\text{Risk reduction benefit}} \gg 1$ → stop reduction; else, implement. - **Documentation:** A formal "ALARP Demonstration" must justify any decision not to reduce further. *(3 marks for ALARP discussion)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Total System Failure Frequency (OR-gate, parallel pumps):** $$f_{\text{total}} = f_P + f_Q = 2 \times 10^{-3} + 3 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ *(1 mark for OR formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer = 3 marks)* **(ii) Risk in ₹/year:** $$R = f \times C = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (\text{₹}2 \times 10^7)$$ $$R = \text{₹}1 \times 10^5 \text{ per year} = \text{₹}1 \text{ Lakh/year}$$ *(2 marks)* **(iii) Residual Risk after SIL 2 SIF:** $$R_{\text{residual}} = R \times \text{PFD} = (\text{₹}1 \times 10^5) \times (10^{-2})$$ $$R_{\text{residual}} = \text{₹}1000 \text{ per year}$$ *(1 mark formula, 1 mark answer = 2 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — PHA & Risk Management** ### Quick-Recall Bullet Points - ✅ **PHA** = systematic identification, evaluation, and control of process hazards - ✅ **Hazard** is intrinsic; **Risk** = $f \times C$ (frequency × consequence) - ✅ **7-step PHA workflow:** Scope → PSI → Hazard ID → Consequence → Frequency → Risk Evaluation → Treatment - ✅ **HAZOP** uses *guide words* (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) + process variables - ✅ **FMEA** = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis — equipment-focused bottom-up technique - ✅ **FTA** uses *AND/OR gates*; AND-gate frequency = product, OR-gate frequency = sum - ✅ **ETA** maps *post-initiator* outcome branches; sum of branch probabilities = 1 - ✅ **Bow-Tie** = FTA (left) + ETA (right) visualization - ✅ **LOPA** = Layer of Protection Analysis; semi-quantitative SIL verification - ✅ **Risk Matrix:** Risk = Likelihood × Severity; standard 5×5 grid, color-coded - ✅ **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable; "grossly disproportionate" cost test - ✅ **SIL equation:** $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}})$ - ✅ **RRF** = $1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$; risk reduction factor after safeguard installation - ✅ **Fertilizer-specific hazards:** NH₃, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HF, H₂; threshold quantities under PSM/RMP - ✅ **Mandatory PHA revalidation:** at least every 5 years (OSHA PSM) and after any Major Change - ✅ **Regulatory frameworks:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, EPA RMP, Indian Factories Act 7A & 41B - ✅ **Document deliverables:** HAZOP worksheet, action closeout register, PHA report, PSI files ### High-Yield Mnemonics - **"No More Less, Reverse As Part Of Other"** — sequence to recall HAZOP guide words - **"OR Adds, AND Multiplies"** — Fault Tree gate frequency logic - **"PHA = Predict, Prevent, Protect"** — three pillars of process safety ### Numerical Stalwarts to Memorize - Standard 5×5 matrix boundaries: **1–4 green, 5–12 yellow, 15–25 red** - SIL bands: **1→10⁻², 2→10⁻³, 3→10⁻⁴, 4→10⁻⁵** - OSHA PSM threshold for NH₃ = **2500 lb (1134 kg)** - OSHA PSM threshold for H₂SO₄ (>94.7%) = **1000 lb (454 kg)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Maxim:** In any PHA question, always close your answer with: *"Recommendations are tracked through action closeout, and the PHA is revalidated at least every 5 years or after any major process change, in accordance with OSHA PSM 1910.119(e)(6)."*

topics.title: Work Controls Through Health Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Work Controls Through Health Protocols # Work Controls Through Health Protocols – Core Technical Foundation ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Work Controls Through Health Protocols** refers to the integrated system of **administrative, engineering, personal protective, and medical surveillance measures** implemented to eliminate, reduce, or control occupational health hazards arising from routine, non-routine, and emergency work activities in fertilizer manufacturing plants. These controls form the operational layer of the *Hierarchy of Controls* and translate the written Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) policy into actionable, task-level safeguards for workers handling hazardous chemicals such as ammonia, sulphuric acid, urea, nitric acid, and phosphoric acid. According to **KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2 – Occupational Health in Fertilizer Industry)**, work controls are the *practical implementation instruments* that bridge the gap between hazard identification (Module 1) and emergency response (Module 3). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Think of a fertilizer plant like a hospital intensive care unit (ICU).** > Just as an ICU uses multiple overlapping safeguards — monitors, restricted access, sterile gloves, regular vitals check, and visitor protocols — to protect a critically ill patient, a fertilizer plant layers work controls to protect workers from chemical, mechanical, and biological hazards. Each control is one "safety net"; when stacked together, no single failure causes a catastrophe. For a layman: imagine cooking in a kitchen with a hot stove. You turn the knob (*engineering control*), keep a timer to remind you (*administrative control*), wear an oven mitt (*PPE*), and visit a doctor if you get a burn (*medical surveillance*). All four are "work controls" for the same hazard — heat. ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Fertilizer Industry Fertilizer plants handle substances classified under **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** as: - **Acutely Toxic** (Anhydrous Ammonia – NH₃, CAS 7664-41-7) - **Corrosive** (Sulphuric Acid – H₂SO₄, Phosphoric Acid – H₃PO₄) - **Oxidizing** (Ammonium Nitrate – NH₄NO₃) - **Carcinogenic/Mutagenic** (certain process dusts containing heavy metals) The threshold metrics for evaluating exposure are: - **TLV-TWA** = **Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average** (typically **8 hours**, e.g., NH₃ = **25 ppm**) - **TLV-STEL** = **Short Term Exposure Limit** (**15 minutes**, e.g., NH₃ = **35 ppm**) - **PEL** = **Permissible Exposure Limit** (OSHA regulatory value) - **IDLH** = **Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health** (e.g., NH₃ = **300 ppm**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In the **Dec 2023** and **May 2024** KTU ESE papers, examiners explicitly asked students to define **TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and IDLH** with fertilizer-industry examples.
