## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the term "Runaway Reaction" as applicable to fertiliser plant operations. Give one example with a brief explanation.*

**Model Answer:**
A runaway reaction is a thermal excursion in which the rate of heat generation by an exothermic reaction exceeds the rate at which heat can be removed, leading to a self-accelerating rise in temperature. **Example:** The decomposition of ammonium nitrate at temperatures above 210 °C is highly exothermic ($\Delta H \approx -8700$ kJ/kmol) and can lead to detonation if not controlled by cooling or dilution with inert material.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*List any three engineering controls used for the safe storage of anhydrous ammonia in a fertiliser plant.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Double-walled pressure vessel** with leak detection in the annular space.
2. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) and Rupture Disc** combination set below the MAWP of the tank.
3. **Dyke/bund wall** of 110% capacity of the largest tank, with rainwater drain valve locked closed.
4. (Bonus) **Vapour return line** during unloading to prevent over-pressurisation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various hazards associated with the storage, handling, and transport of raw materials and intermediates in a fertiliser plant. Use specific examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of reaction hazards with reference to a fertiliser plant. Derive an expression for the adiabatic temperature rise and comment on its significance in process safety. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Storage hazards:** Classification into bulk solids, liquids, and gases.
  - Bulk solids (phosphate rock, urea, DAP): dust explosion, bridging, spontaneous combustion.
  - Liquids (NH₃, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, urea liquor): tank rupture, bund overflow, corrosion, cryogenic burns.
  - Gases (H₂, Cl₂, syngas): asphyxiation, embrittlement, fire.
- **Handling hazards:** Pumping, hose transfer, bag stacking, conveyor/elevator, forklift movement.
  - Controls: whiplash cables, mechanical aids, guarding, interlocks.
- **Transport hazards:** Road, rail, marine, and pipeline.
  - Specific risks: TREM card, rollover protection, third-party damage (pipelines), cargo shift.
- **Key examples:**
  - West Fertiliser, Texas (2013): ammonium nitrate detonation due to poor storage.
  - Bhopal (1984): MIC leak (analogous to ammonia storage risk).
  - Tianjin (2015): AN + cyanide co-storage explosion.

**Model Solution (b):**
- Definition of reaction hazard; types: thermal runaway, gas evolution, polymerisation.
- **Derivation** of adiabatic temperature rise:
  Start with energy balance: $m C_p dT/dt = -\Delta H_r r_A V$.
  Integrate under adiabatic assumption to get:
  $$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_r}{M \cdot C_p \cdot (1 + \kappa)}$$
- **Significance:**
  - Used in DIERS (Design Institute for Emergency Relief Systems) sizing.
  - Determines the temperature that the reactor would reach without any cooling.
  - If $\Delta T_{ad}$ + $T_0$ exceeds the onset decomposition temperature of the reactant, a relief device is mandatory.
- **Numerical example:** For ammonium nitrate with $\Delta H_r = -8700$ kJ/kmol, $M = 80$ kg/kmol, $C_p = 1.7$ kJ/kg·K, $\kappa = 1.2$:
  $$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{8700}{80 \times 1.7 \times 2.2} = 36.4 \text{ °C}$$
  This means a small deviation can lead to a major excursion.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the hierarchy of controls with reference to loss control in storage, handling, and transport of fertiliser materials. Provide one engineering and one administrative control for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the hazards of ammonia and sulphuric acid plants. Suggest first-aid and engineering controls for accidental exposure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Hierarchy of Controls** (from most to least effective):
  1. **Elimination** — Example: discontinue use of white phosphorus.
  2. **Substitution** — Example: aqueous ammonia (19%) in place of anhydrous NH₃.
  3. **Engineering Controls:**
     - Storage: dykes, double-walled tanks, vent panels, level interlocks.
     - Handling: local exhaust ventilation, mechanical aids, guard rails.
     - Transport: GPS tracking, anti-rollover systems, automatic shutoff valves.
  4. **Administrative Controls:**
     - Storage: SOP for stacking height, inventory check.
     - Handling: permit-to-work, LOTO, toolbox talk.
     - Transport: driver training, TREM card, route risk assessment.
  5. **PPE** — SCBA for ammonia, acid suits for H₂SO₄, safety harness for height work.

**Model Solution (b):**
- **Ammonia plant hazards:** Toxicity, fire (NH₃ is combustible at 15–28% in air), asphyxiation, explosion in confined space, cryogenic burns.
  - **First aid:** Remove to fresh air, administer oxygen, flush eyes with water for 15 min, do NOT induce vomiting if ingested.
  - **Engineering controls:** Water spray curtains, gas detectors (electrochemical, 0–100 ppm), emergency scrubbers, SCBA on standby.
- **Sulphuric acid plant hazards:** Corrosive burns, acid mist inhalation, oleum explosion, equipment corrosion.
  - **First aid:** Flush with copious water for 20+ minutes, do NOT attempt neutralisation, seek immediate medical aid.
  - **Engineering controls:** Eyewash and safety shower within 10 m, acid-resistant flooring, dyked unloading bays, vapour recovery.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in 14-mark questions on this topic:**
> 1. **Listing without explanation** — Writing "SCBA" without stating *when* and *why* it is used will fetch only 1 of 3 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the hierarchy of controls** — Starting the answer with "PPE" loses a sequencing mark. Always start with inherent safety.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — A correct answer without units (e.g., writing 36.4 instead of 36.4 °C) is treated as incomplete.
> 4. **No disaster case study** — Citing one real-world incident (West, Bhopal, Tianjin) adds 1–2 bonus marks in the examiner's view.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** — Even if the final formula is correct, missing the energy balance setup will cost 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Confusing TLV-TWA with IDLH** — TLV is for chronic exposure; IDLH is for acute. Many students interchange them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Three pillars of fertiliser safety:** Inherent safety, engineering controls, procedural safety.
- **Most toxic gas in fertiliser industry:** Anhydrous ammonia (TLV-TWA = 25 ppm, IDLH = 300 ppm).
- **Most explosive fertiliser:** Ammonium nitrate (auto-ignition ~210 °C, Class 5.1 oxidiser).
- **Storage hazards:** Dust explosion, BLEVE, bund overflow, spontaneous combustion, cryogenic burns.
- **Handling hazards:** Hose whip, entanglement, forklift tip-over, conveyor nip points.
- **Transport hazards:** Rollover, third-party damage (pipelines), cargo shift, derailment.
- **Five elements of dust explosion pentagon:** Fuel, oxidiser, ignition source, dispersion, confinement.
- **Five elements of Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination, Substitution, Engineering, Administrative, PPE.
- **Key formulas to remember:**
  - $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_r}{M C_p (1 + \kappa)}$
  - $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$
  - $A_v = K V^{2/3}$ (NFPA 68 cubic law)
- **First-aid principle for acids:** Flush with water, **do not** neutralise.
- **First-aid principle for ammonia:** Fresh air + oxygen; flush eyes with water.
- **Mandatory permits in fertiliser plants:** Hot work, confined space, LOTO, working at height, electrical isolation, excavation.
- **Statutory standards:** Indian Factories Act 1948, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, NFPA 30/68/704, ISO 45001:2018, IEC 61511 (SIL).
- **Real-world case studies to remember:**
  1. **Bhopal, India (1984)** — MIC release (toxic hazard, not fertiliser but same class of chemical).
  2. **West, Texas, USA (2013)** — Ammonium nitrate explosion (15 dead).
  3. **Tianjin, China (2015)** — Co-storage of ammonium nitrate and sodium cyanide (173 dead).
  4. **AZF Toulouse, France (2001)** — Ammonium nitrate detonation (31 dead).
- **Critical SOPs in fertiliser plant:** LOTO, Confined Space Entry, Hot Work, Management of Change (MOC), Permit to Work (PTW), Emergency Shutdown (ESD) testing.
- **Loss control formula (DOW Fire & Explosion Index):** F&EI = Material Factor × General Process Hazards × Special Process Hazards.
- **Risk Matrix dimensions:** Likelihood (1–5) × Severity (1–5); acceptable risk score is usually ≤ 4 (1×4 or 2×2).
- **Emergency response plan (ERP) cycle:** Prevention → Mitigation → Preparedness → Response → Recovery → Lessons Learned.
- **Dust classification (Kst):** St 1 (weak) < 200, St 2 (strong) 200–300, St 3 (very strong) > 300 bar·m/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question, structure your answer as:
> 1. *Introduction / definition* (1 mark)
> 2. *Classification / types* (2 marks)
> 3. *Detailed description with examples* (5 marks)
> 4. *Diagrammatic representation (mermaid-style block diagram in exam)* (2 marks)
> 5. *Control measures / case study* (2 marks)
> 6. *Conclusion / safety recommendation* (2 marks)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International and Indian Standards for the Safe Storage, Handling and Transport of Hazardous Chemicals - Disaster Preparedness and Risk Reduction in Fertiliser Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** International and Indian Standards for the Safe Storage, Handling and Transport of Hazardous Chemicals - Disaster Preparedness and Risk Reduction in Fertiliser Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Hazardous Chemicals (as per Rule 2(h) of MSIHC Rules, 1989):**
Any chemical substance which satisfies any of the criteria laid down in Part I of Schedule I (toxic, flammable, explosive, corrosive, reactive) or any chemical listed in Columns 2, 3, and 4 of Schedule II, including ammonia, sulphuric acid, nitric acid, phosphoric acid, urea (under pressure), ammonium nitrate, hydrofluoric acid, and chlorine used in the fertilizer industry.

**Disaster Preparedness (as per National Disaster Management Act, 2005):**
A continuous, integrated cycle of planning, organizing, training, equipping, exercising, evaluating, and taking corrective action to ensure effective coordination during incident response and to maintain a state of readiness to handle chemical/industrial emergencies.

**Risk Reduction:**
The systematic application of management policies, procedures, and physical practices to the analysis, assessment, control, and monitoring of risk in fertilizer plants to bring it down to As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the fertilizer industry, the most cited hazardous substances are *anhydrous ammonia, ammonium nitrate, sulphuric acid, nitric acid, hydrofluoric acid, and chlorine gas*. Memorize their UN Hazard Classification numbers — they are frequently asked in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Fertilizer Plant is like a High-Stakes Kitchen with Toxic Ingredients.**

Imagine a professional industrial kitchen where chefs handle boiling oil, pressurized steam, sharp knives, and corrosive cleaning agents all at once. To keep everyone safe, the kitchen must have:
- **Recipes with exact quantities** (Standard Operating Procedures / SOPs)
- **Proper storage shelves** (Segregated chemical stores)
- **Delivery trucks that follow traffic rules** (Transport regulations like ADR, IMDG)
- **Fire extinguishers and first-aid kits** (Disaster preparedness)
- **Insurance and inspections** (Legal compliance & audits)

A fertilizer plant works on the same principle. **Ammonia** is the "boiling oil," **sulphuric acid** is the "corrosive cleaner," and **ammonium nitrate** is the "potentially explosive ingredient." International and Indian standards are the *rule book* that tells the plant exactly how to handle, store, and transport each ingredient safely. Disaster preparedness is the *fire-drill system*, while risk reduction is the *continuous menu-revision* to remove the most dangerous items.

**Plain-English Takeaway:**
- **Standards** = Rule books (OSHA, ISO 45001, UN GHS, MSIHC Rules, EP Act).
- **Safe Storage/Handling/Transport** = Following the rule book for everyday operations.
- **Disaster Preparedness** = Having a rehearsed emergency plan before anything goes wrong.
- **Risk Reduction** = Continuously making the plant safer by removing or reducing hazards.

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the Fertilizer Industry

The fertilizer sector is one of the **top three chemical-intensive industries** worldwide along with petrochemicals and pharmaceuticals. The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** — although it was a pesticide plant — fundamentally reshaped global thinking on hazardous chemical safety and directly led to the creation of India's **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC), 1989** and the **Environment Protection Act (EPA), 1986**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions to Remember:**
> - Flash Point (closed cup) for **flammable liquids**: < **93 °C**
> - Toxicity threshold (LC50 inhalation, rat, 4 h): ≤ **20 mg/L**
> - Explosive limit (detonation velocity of ammonium nitrate): > **2000 m/s**
> - Major Accident Hazard (MAH) threshold for ammonia: ≥ **50 tonnes**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 International Standards — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. United Nations / Globally Harmonized System (UN GHS)
- **Purpose:** Globally harmonized classification and labelling of chemicals.
- **Key Document:** *"Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals" (GHS)* — currently in its **10th revised edition (2023)**.
- **Components:** 9 hazard classes (physical, health, environmental) + 2 signal words (Danger / Warning) + 70 H-statements + GHS pictograms.
- **Pictograms** (16 in total): Flame, Flame Over Circle, Exploding Bomb, Gas Cylinder, Skull & Crossbones, Corrosion, Environment, Exclamation Mark, Health Hazard.

#### B. ISO 45001:2018 (Occupational Health & Safety Management)
- Replaced **OHSAS 18001:2007** in March 2021.
- **PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** based on Annex SL (High-Level Structure).
- **Risk-Based Thinking** instead of the older "hazard prevention" approach.
- **Clause 6.1.2:** Identification of hazards, assessment of risks and opportunities.
- **Clause 8.1.2:** Emergency preparedness and response.

#### C. OSHA 29 CFR (US Standards)
- **Process Safety Management (PSM) of Highly Hazardous Chemicals — 29 CFR 1910.119** — applicable to ammonia (anhydrous) ≥ 10,000 lbs (~4536 kg).
- **Hazard Communication Standard (HazCom) — 29 CFR 1910.1200** — aligns with GHS.
- **Permit-Required Confined Spaces — 29 CFR 1910.146**.

#### D. International Transport Regulations (the "Modal Codes")

| Code | Mode | Governing Body | Key Fertilizer Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** | All modes | UN Committee of Experts | Master reference for ADR, IMDG, IATA |
| **ADR** | Road (Europe) | UNECE | Road transport of ammonia, H2SO4 |
| **RID** | Rail (Europe) | OTIF | Rail transport of phosphoric acid |
| **IMDG Code** | Sea | IMO | Bulk shipment of ammonium nitrate |
| **IATA DGR** | Air | IATA | Sample/small quantities of catalysts |
| **US DOT 49 CFR** | All modes (US) | PHMSA | US-bound fertilizer exports |

> [!NOTE]
> **UN Hazard Classes to Memorize for KTU:**
> - Class 1 — Explosives (ammonium nitrate under confinement)
> - Class 2 — Gases (ammonia, chlorine)
> - Class 3 — Flammable Liquids (methanol, naphtha)
> - Class 5.1 — Oxidizers (nitric acid, ammonium nitrate)
> - Class 6.1 — Toxic (HCN, hydrogen fluoride)
> - Class 8 — Corrosives (H2SO4, HCl, HF)

---

### 2.2 Indian Standards & Regulations — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Constitution of India
- **Article 48A** — Protection and improvement of environment.
- **Article 21** — Right to life (extended to safe working conditions).

#### B. The Factories Act, 1948 (Amended 1987)
- **Section 6:** Approval of plans for storage of hazardous chemicals.
- **Section 7A:** Compulsory safety audit.
- **Section 41F:** Responsibility of occupier for hazardous processes.
- **Schedule I** lists hazardous processes including manufacture of fertilizers and ammonia.

#### C. The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (EPA)
- **Umbrella Act** empowering the Central Government to:
  - Set emission/effluent standards.
  - Prescribe procedures for handling hazardous substances.
  - Conduct environmental impact assessment (EIA).

#### D. Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC Rules)
- Framed under EPA 1986, directly triggered by **Bhopal Gas Tragedy**.
- **Rule 5:** Notification of sites.
- **Rule 7–10:** Safety reports, Safety audits, On-site Emergency Plans (OEP).
- **Rule 12:** Detailed information on isolated storage.
- **Schedule I, II, III:** List of chemicals with threshold quantities.
- **Threshold Quantities (Schedule II):**
  - Ammonia (anhydrous): **50 tonnes** (Column 3 — MAH install)
  - Chlorine: **10 tonnes**
  - Sulphuric acid: **100 tonnes**
  - Ammonium nitrate: **350 tonnes** (Column 4 — MAH storage)
  - Hydrofluoric acid: **50 tonnes**

#### E. Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 (PLI Act)
- Mandatory **one-time insurance** for handling hazardous substances to provide immediate relief to victims.

#### F. Chemical Accidents (Emergency Planning, Preparedness and Response) Rules, 1996
- Framed under EPA 1986.
- Mandates **Off-Site Emergency Plan** at district level.
- **Central Crisis Group (CCG)** at national level.
- **State Crisis Group (SCG)** at state level.
- **District Crisis Group (DCG)** at district level.

#### G. National Disaster Management Act, 2005
- **Section 14:** National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) chaired by PM.
- **Section 17:** Powers to issue guidelines for chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear emergencies.

#### H. Other Important BIS Standards
- **IS 1446:2002** — Classification of dangerous goods.
- **IS 4156:1988** — Glossary of terms for hazardous chemicals.
- **IS 7960:1975** — Safety code for handling and storage of chemicals.
- **IS 3016:1982** — Safety code for storage of ammonium nitrate.
- **IS 4041:1987** — Safety code for anhydrous ammonia.

---

### 2.3 Safe Storage of Hazardous Chemicals — Engineering Principles

1. **Segregation by Compatibility:** Oxidizers separated from organics, acids from bases.
2. **Bunded Storage (Secondary Containment):** Bund wall capacity = **110%** of largest tank volume + **25%** of aggregate capacity.
3. **Ventilation:** Minimum **6 air changes per hour** for toxic gas storage rooms.
4. **Distance (IS 3016 & OISD-150):**
   - Ammonia tank to plant boundary: ≥ **30 m** (small) to **75 m** (large).
   - Ammonium nitrate storage: ≥ **90 m** from inhabited buildings.
5. **Spill Containment Pallets:** Capacity ≥ **100%** of largest container.
6. **Temperature Control:** Maximum storage temperature for ammonium nitrate: **< 30 °C** (decomposition risk above 210 °C).
7. **Lightning Protection (IS/IEC 62305):** Class II protection for explosive stores.

---

### 2.4 Safe Handling Procedures

- **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS)** — 16-section GHS-compliant document mandatory at every handling point.
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** system for hot work, confined space, work at height.
- **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** for maintenance.
- **PPE Hierarchy:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative Control → PPE.
- **Quantified Risk Assessment (QRA)** for every MAH installation.

---

### 2.5 Safe Transport Requirements

| Requirement | Specification |
|---|---|
| Vehicle marking | UN Number, HAZCHEM placard (Class label, Diamond) |
| Driver training | As per ADR 1.10 (CPC – Certificate of Professional Competence) |
| Documentation | Transport Emergency (TREM) Card, Consignment Note |
| Maximum quantity per package | Per Schedule II of MSIHC Rules |
| Speed limit for hazardous load | Often restricted to **40–60 km/h** in India |
| Route planning | Avoid densely populated areas, water sources |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Priority Number (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless | 1 – 1000 |
| 2 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot p_i$ | probability/yr | < 10⁻⁶ /yr (acceptable) |
| 3 | **F-N Curve** (Societal Risk) | $F(N) = \sum_{i} f_i$ where outcome ≥ N | frequency/yr | Tolerable if N > 1 |
| 4 | **Probit Equation (Toxic)** | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | dimensionless | used to find % lethality |
| 5 | **Crowl–Louvar Toxic Release** | $\text{Dose} = C^n \cdot t$ | (mg/m³)ⁿ · min | n = 1 for ammonia |
| 6 | **Dow Fire & Explosion Index** | $F\&EI = F_3 \times MF$ | dimensionless | 0 – 158 (maximum) |
| 7 | **Bund Wall Capacity** | $V_{bund} = 1.1 V_{\max} + 0.25 V_{total}$ | m³ | per EPA/CEC |
| 8 | **Alarp Test (Cost-benefit)** | $\text{Gross Cost of Avoidance (GCA)} \leq \text{Risk Reduction Cost (RRC)}$ | currency | ALARP demonstrated |
| 9 | **Storage Distance (TNO)** | $Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \geq D^3 \cdot K$ | m³/kg | K = constant (effect-based) |
| 10 | **Indoor QRA Release Rate** | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_a}{P}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_a}{P}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | kg/s | for pressurized ammonia |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Most 14-mark questions either ask you to compute the **RPN** for a given scenario OR to explain the **MSIHC Rules and threshold quantities** with a fertilizer-specific example. Master both.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Worked Example

**Problem Statement (KTU style):**
A fertilizer plant has a urea prilling tower with a potential leak in the ammonia feed line. The failure would cause:
- Severity (S) rating = 8 (high — toxic release, potential fatality)
- Occurrence (O) rating = 5 (moderate — happens once in 6–12 months)
- Detection (D) rating = 4 (low–moderate — gas detectors in place)

Compute the RPN and recommend action.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the FMEA Formula**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$\text{RPN} = 8 \times 5 \times 4$$

**Step 3 — Multiply**

$$\text{RPN} = 160$$

**Step 4 — Compare against threshold**

Standard FMEA acceptance criteria:
- RPN **< 100** → Acceptable, document.
- RPN **100 – 200** → Action required (CAPA).
- RPN **> 200** → Mandatory redesign.

**Step 5 — Decision**

RPN = 160 falls in the **Action Required** band. Recommended CAPA:
- Install additional ammonia detectors (reduces D from 4 → 2).
- Replace the gasket with a higher-grade one (reduces O from 5 → 2).
- Add water curtain and auto-shutoff valve (reduces S from 8 → 6).

**New RPN after CAPA:**

$$\text{RPN}_{new} = 6 \times 2 \times 2 = 24$$

This is in the **acceptable zone**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Bund Wall Capacity — Worked Example

**Problem:**
A fertilizer storage tank farm has:
- One large H₂SO₄ tank of capacity **V₁ = 500 m³**
- Two medium tanks each of **V₂ = V₃ = 200 m³**
- Four small tanks each of **V₄ = 50 m³**

Compute the bund wall capacity per EPA / CEC formula.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify largest single tank volume**

$$V_{\max} = \max(V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4) = 500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute total storage volume**

$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + 4 \cdot V_4$$

$$V_{total} = 500 + 200 + 200 + 4 \times 50$$

$$V_{total} = 500 + 200 + 200 + 200 = 1100 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the bund capacity formula**

$$V_{bund} = 1.1 \cdot V_{\max} + 0.25 \cdot V_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

$$V_{bund} = 1.1 \times 500 + 0.25 \times 1100$$

**Step 5 — Compute each term**

$$V_{bund} = 550 + 275$$

$$V_{bund} = 825 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Interpret**

The bunded area must be capable of holding at least **825 m³** of liquid in case the largest tank ruptures and 25% of all other tanks' contents also escape.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Toxic Consequence — Probit Equation (Ammonia Release)

**Given:**
- Release rate of anhydrous ammonia: **m = 50 kg/s**
- Atmospheric dispersion coefficient (Pasquill-Gifford D stability): **σ_y ≈ 50 m** at downwind distance **x = 1000 m**
- Wind speed: **u = 5 m/s**
- Use the Probit equation for ammonia toxicity (Thorpe, 1989):

$$Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$$

For ammonia, **a = -30.57**, **b = 1.385**, **n = 1**, where C is in **mg/m³** and t is in **minutes**.

**Step 1 — Compute the effective dispersion concentration**

Using a simple Gaussian steady-state estimate:

$$C(x) = \frac{m}{u \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z}$$

Assume σ_z = 0.5 σ_y = 25 m.

$$C = \frac{50}{5 \cdot \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 25}$$

$$C = \frac{50}{19634.95}$$

$$C \approx 0.002546 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 2546 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Assume exposure time t = 30 min**

$$\text{Dose} = C^n \cdot t = (2546)^1 \times 30 = 76380$$

**Step 3 — Compute Probit**

$$Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \cdot \ln(76380)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate natural log**

$$\ln(76380) \approx 11.243$$

**Step 5 — Substitute**

$$Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \times 11.243$$

$$Y = -30.57 + 15.572$$

$$Y \approx -14.998 \approx -15$$

**Step 6 — Convert Probit to Percentage**

For Y = -15, the corresponding % fatality is essentially **0%** at 1000 m downwind. (The 50% fatality corresponds to Probit = 5.)

**Step 7 — Repeat for closer distance (x = 200 m)**

At x = 200 m, σ_y ≈ 20 m, σ_z ≈ 10 m:

$$C = \frac{50}{5 \cdot \pi \cdot 20 \cdot 10} = 0.01591 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 15915 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

$$\ln(15915 \times 30) = \ln(477450) \approx 13.077$$

$$Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \times 13.077 = -30.57 + 18.112 = -12.46$$

% fatality ≈ **0%** (still below Probit 5).

**Step 8 — At very close distance (x = 50 m), σ_y ≈ 8 m, σ_z ≈ 5 m:**

$$C = \frac{50}{5 \cdot \pi \cdot 8 \cdot 5} = 0.07958 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 79580 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

$$\ln(79580 \times 30) = \ln(2387400) \approx 14.685$$

$$Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \times 14.685 = -30.57 + 20.339 = -10.23$$

% fatality ≈ **0%**.

**Step 9 — At point of release (x = 10 m), σ_y ≈ 4 m, σ_z ≈ 3 m:**

$$C = \frac{50}{5 \cdot \pi \cdot 4 \cdot 3} = 0.2653 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 265258 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

$$\ln(265258 \times 30) = \ln(7957740) \approx 15.888$$

$$Y = -30.57 + 1.385 \times 15.888 = -30.57 + 22.005 = -8.57$$

% fatality ≈ **0%** (corresponds to Probit < 4, no significant lethality in 30 min, though irritation is severe).

> [!NOTE]
> This illustrative calculation demonstrates that ammonia releases, while highly toxic at the source, decay rapidly with distance due to dispersion. **Real-world plant siting** uses ISC-PRIME, ALOHA, or PHAST software which apply more sophisticated dense-gas dispersion models (since ammonia is heavier than air).

---

### 3.4 Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI) — Illustrative Computation

$$\text{F\&EI} = F_3 \times MF$$

where:
- **F₃** = Process Unit Hazard Factor (range 1.0 – 8.0)
- **MF** = Material Factor (from NFPA ratings, anhydrous ammonia MF = 4)

For a typical ammonia storage facility with F₃ = 4.0:

$$\text{F\&EI} = 4.0 \times 4 = 16$$

| F&EI Range | Hazard Degree |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | Light |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate |
| 97 – 127 | Heavy |
| **128 – 158** | **Severe** |

A value of **16** places the unit in the **Light** hazard degree.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Standards vs Fertilizer Industry Activities

| Activity | Standard / Rule | Threshold (if any) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage of Ammonia | IS 4041 / MSIHC Schedule II | ≥ 50 t |
| Storage of Ammonium Nitrate | IS 3016 / MSIHC | ≥ 350 t |
| Transport of H₂SO₄ | IMDG Code Class 8 | Per package limits |
| Worker Safety | ISO 45001 / Factories Act Sec 7A | All workers |
| Emergency Planning | Chemical Accidents Rules 1996 | District-level OSEP |
| Insurance | PLI Act 1991 | All handlers |
| Audit | MSIHC Rule 10 | Every 2 years |
| Environmental Clearance | EPA 1986 / EIA Notification 2006 | All fertilizer plants |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchy of Standards Governing Fertilizer Industry HSE

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[International Standards] --> nodeB[UN GHS]
    nodeA --> nodeC[ISO 45001 2018]
    nodeA --> nodeD[OSHA 29 CFR 1910 119 PSM]
    nodeA --> nodeE[ADR RID IMDG IATA DGR]
    nodeA --> nodeF[ILO C170 Chemicals Convention]
    nodeG[Indian Standards BIS IS Series] --> nodeH[IS 1446]
    nodeG --> nodeI[IS 4041 Ammonia]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[IS 3016 Ammonium Nitrate]
    nodeG --> nodeK[IS 7960 Chemical Handling]
    nodeL[Indian Legal Framework] --> nodeM[Factories Act 1948]
    nodeL --> nodeN[EPA 1986]
    nodeL --> nodeO[MSIHC Rules 1989]
    nodeL --> nodeP[Public Liability Insurance Act 1991]
    nodeL --> nodeQ[Chemical Accidents Rules 1996]
    nodeL --> nodeR[NDMA Act 2005]
    nodeB --> nodeS[Fertilizer Plant Compliance]
    nodeC --> nodeS
    nodeD --> nodeS
    nodeE --> nodeS
    nodeF --> nodeS
    nodeH --> nodeS
    nodeI --> nodeS
    nodeJ --> nodeS
    nodeK --> nodeS
    nodeM --> nodeS
    nodeN --> nodeS
    nodeO --> nodeS
    nodeP --> nodeS
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeR --> nodeS
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — UN Hazard Classification Decision Flow for Fertilizer Chemicals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Chemical in Fertilizer Plant]) --> q1{Is it explosive?}
    q1 -- YES --> c1[Class 1 e.g. ammonium nitrate under confinement]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{Is it a gas at STP?}
    q2 -- YES --> q3{Flammable?}
    q3 -- YES --> c2[Class 2 1 Flammable Gas e.g. ammonia]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{Toxic?}
    q4 -- YES --> c3[Class 2 3 Toxic Gas e.g. chlorine]
    q4 -- NO --> c4[Class 2 2 Non flammable non toxic e.g. nitrogen]
    q2 -- NO --> q5{Is it a liquid with FP less than 60 deg C?}
    q5 -- YES --> c5[Class 3 e.g. methanol]
    q5 -- NO --> q6{Is it an oxidizer?}
    q6 -- YES --> c6[Class 5 1 e.g. nitric acid ammonium nitrate solution]
    q6 -- NO --> q7{Is it toxic LD50 less than threshold?}
    q7 -- YES --> c7[Class 6 1]
    q7 -- NO --> q8{Is it corrosive?}
    q8 -- YES --> c8[Class 8 e.g. H2SO4 HF H3PO4]
    q8 -- NO --> c9[Non hazardous goods]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Disaster Preparedness Cycle (Fertilizer Plant)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Mitigation] --> stepB[Preparedness]
    stepB --> stepC[Response]
    stepC --> stepD[Recovery]
    stepD --> stepA
    stepA -- Example --> ex1[Dike construction Secondary containment]
    stepB -- Example --> ex2[Mock drills OSEP training]
    stepC -- Example --> ex3[Firefighting Evacuation Medical aid]
    stepD -- Example --> ex4[Rehabilitation Investigation Lessons learned]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Risk Reduction Framework

```mermaid
graph TB
    src[Hazard Source Ammonia H2SO4 NH4NO3 HF] --> ident[Hazard Identification HAZOP What If Checklist]
    ident --> assess[Risk Assessment QRA FMEA Dow FEI]
    assess --> ctrl[Risk Control Hierarchy]
    ctrl --> lvl1[1 Elimination]
    ctrl --> lvl2[2 Substitution]
    ctrl --> lvl3[3 Engineering Control Dikes Valves Scrubbers]
    ctrl --> lvl4[4 Administrative Control SOP Permit PTW]
    ctrl --> lvl5[5 PPE Last Resort]
    lvl1 --> monitor[Continuous Monitoring KPIs]
    lvl2 --> monitor
    lvl3 --> monitor
    lvl4 --> monitor
    lvl5 --> monitor
    monitor --> review[Management Review ISO 45001 Clause 9 3]
    review --> audit[External Audit Every 2 years MSIHC Rule 10]
    audit --> ident
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Storage Compatibility Matrix (Fertilizer Chemicals)

```mermaid
graph LR
    a[Ammonia NH3] -->|Compatible with| a1[Ammonium Nitrate Solid]
    a -->|INCOMPATIBLE| a2[Chlorine Gas]
    a -->|INCOMPATIBLE| a3[Strong Acids Like HCl]
    b[Sulphuric Acid H2SO4] -->|Compatible with| b1[Phosphoric Acid H3PO4]
    b -->|INCOMPATIBLE| b2[Ammonia Bases]
    b -->|INCOMPATIBLE| b3[Organic peroxides]
    c[Ammonium Nitrate] -->|Compatible with| c1[Urea]
    c -->|INCOMPATIBLE| c2[Fuel Oil Diesel Organic matter]
    c -->|INCOMPATIBLE| c3[Reducing agents Metals like zinc]
    d[Hydrofluoric Acid HF] -->|Compatible with| d1[Glass Lined Tanks]
    d -->|INCOMPATIBLE| d2[Silica containing materials]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always store Ammonium Nitrate AWAY from fuels, organics, and reducing agents.** The 1921 Oppau, Germany disaster (561 fatalities) and the 2015 Tianjin explosion were direct consequences of improper segregation of oxidizers and fuels.

---

### 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — On-Site vs Off-Site Emergency Plan Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    ep[Emergency Plan] --> osep[On Site Emergency Plan OEP]
    ep --> ofsep[Off Site Emergency Plan OSEP]
    osep --> comp1[Alarm and Communication]
    osep --> comp2[Escape and Evacuation Routes]
    osep --> comp3[Fire Fighting Systems]
    osep --> comp4[First Aid and Medical]
    osep --> comp5[Spill Control Bunds]
    osep --> comp6[Mock Drills Every 6 months]
    ofsep --> dcg[District Crisis Group DCG]
    ofsep --> police[Police and Traffic Control]
    ofsep --> fire[Fire Services]
    ofsep --> hosp[Hospitals and Ambulances]
    ofsep --> media[Public Information Media]
    ofsep --> media2[Mutual Aid Neighbouring Industries]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Define Hazardous Chemical as per MSIHC Rules, 1989. List any four chemicals used in the fertilizer industry that come under its purview.**

**Model Answer:**
As per Rule 2(h) of MSIHC Rules, 1989, a **Hazardous Chemical** means any chemical which satisfies the criteria laid down in Part I of Schedule I (Class A: toxic, Class B: flammable/explosive) or is listed in Columns 2, 3, and 4 of Schedule II with specified threshold quantities.

**Four chemicals used in the fertilizer industry:**

| Chemical | UN Class | Threshold Qty (Schedule II, Column 3) |
|---|---|---|
| Anhydrous Ammonia (NH₃) | 2.3 (Toxic Gas) | 50 tonnes |
| Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄) | 8 (Corrosive) | 100 tonnes |
| Ammonium Nitrate (NH₄NO₃) | 5.1 (Oxidizer) | 350 tonnes |
| Hydrofluoric Acid (HF) | 8 (Corrosive) | 50 tonnes |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing chemicals with UN class)**

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Differentiate between On-Site Emergency Plan (OEP) and Off-Site Emergency Plan (OEP/Off-Site). Who is responsible for each?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | On-Site Emergency Plan | Off-Site Emergency Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Within factory premises | Beyond factory boundary (district level) |
| **Authority** | Plant Manager / Occupier | District Collector / DCG Chairman |
| **Statutory basis** | MSIHC Rules 1989, Rule 14 | Chemical Accidents Rules 1996 |
| **Activation** | Plant in-charge | District Crisis Group |
| **Resources** | Plant emergency teams, firefighting, medical room | Police, hospitals, fire services, evacuation |
| **Frequency of mock drill** | Every 6 months | Annually |

**(3 marks: 2 for differentiation table + 1 for responsible authority)**

---

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical (MSIHC) Rules, 1989 in detail. List the threshold quantities for ammonia, ammonium nitrate, and chlorine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** method of hazard evaluation. Apply it to an ammonia storage facility with Material Factor MF = 4 and Process Unit Hazard Factor F₃ = 4. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MSIHC Rules, 1989 (7 marks)**

1. **Genesis:** Enacted under Section 6, 8, and 25 of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986; came into force on **27 November 1989**, shortly after the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Dec 1984)**.

2. **Applicability:** Rule 2 — applies to all industrial activities involving hazardous chemicals listed in Schedules I, II, III.

3. **Key Provisions:**
   - **Rule 4–5:** Notification of sites handling hazardous chemicals to the Chief Inspector of Factories and the Ministry of Environment.
   - **Rule 7–8:** Preparation and submission of **Safety Reports** for MAH installations.
   - **Rule 9–10:** **On-Site Emergency Plan** and **Safety Audit** by an independent agency approved by the Chief Inspector.
   - **Rule 11–12:** Information to persons likely to be affected and isolated storage requirements.
   - **Rule 13–14:** On-site emergency plans and mock drills every 6 months.

4. **Threshold Quantities (Schedule II, Column 3 — for MAH Installations):**

| Chemical | Threshold Quantity |
|---|---|
| Ammonia (anhydrous) | 50 tonnes |
| Ammonium nitrate | 350 tonnes |
| Chlorine | 10 tonnes |
| Sulphuric acid | 100 tonnes |
| Hydrofluoric acid | 50 tonnes |

5. **Penalty:** Imprisonment up to 5 years and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh under Section 15 of EPA, 1986.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 for genesis/scope + 2 for rules + 1 for threshold table + 2 for penalties/responsibilities)**

---

**(b) Dow F&EI Method (7 marks)**

**Concept:**
The Dow Chemical Company's Fire & Explosion Index is a **quantitative hazard evaluation** method that assigns a numerical ranking to process units based on the inherent chemical and process hazards.

**Step 1 — Identify the Process Unit** (e.g., ammonia storage sphere).

**Step 2 — Determine Material Factor (MF)** from NFPA reactivity rating. For **anhydrous ammonia (NH₃)**, MF = **4** (NFPA Reactivity = 0, but adjusted for toxicity and flammability per Dow tables).

**Step 3 — Compute Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃)** by adding two general process hazards (F₁) and special process hazards (F₂):

$$F_3 = F_1 + F_2$$

For our case, given **F₃ = 4**.

**Step 4 — Compute F&EI:**

$$\text{F\&EI} = F_3 \times MF$$

$$\text{F\&EI} = 4 \times 4 = 16$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

| F&EI Range | Hazard Degree |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | **Light** |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate |
| 97 – 127 | Heavy |
| 128 – 158 | **Severe** |

**Result: 16 → Light Hazard Degree** — operationally acceptable, routine safety precautions are sufficient.

**Step 6 — Recommendations** (consequence analysis, radius of exposure, damage factor): The unit is well within safe limits, but routine inspection of valves, alarms, and dike integrity should continue.

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 for concept + 1 for MF selection + 1 for F₃ selection + 1 for calculation + 1 for interpretation + 1 for recommendations)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** What are the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code** regulations for the transport of ammonium nitrate by sea? Describe the marking, labelling, and packaging requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **disaster preparedness and risk reduction** in the fertilizer industry with reference to the **Chemical Accidents (Emergency Planning, Preparedness and Response) Rules, 1996**. Mention the four-tier crisis group structure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IMDG Code for Ammonium Nitrate Transport (7 marks)**

1. **Code Reference:** International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code, published by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**; current edition: **41-22 (Amendment 41-22)** with biennial updates.

2. **UN Number and Classification:**
   - **UN 1942** — Ammonium Nitrate (with ≤ 0.2% combustible substances), Class **5.1 (Oxidizing Substance)**, PG III.
   - **UN 0222** — Ammonium Nitrate (explosive grade), Class 1.5D.
   - For fertilizer-grade ammonium nitrate, **UN 2067** is also used.

3. **Marking Requirements:**
   - **UN Number, Proper Shipping Name** ("AMMONIUM NITRATE"), and **Class 5.1 hazard label** (yellow triangle with flame-over-circle symbol).
   - Marine Pollutant mark if applicable.
   - Stowage category assigned by IMDG Code (e.g., Category "A" for non-heat-sensitive).

4. **Packaging Requirements:**
   - **Packing Group (PG) III** — low danger level.
   - Packaging must conform to **UN Performance Tests** (drop, stacking, vibration tests).
   - IBCs (Intermediate Bulk Containers) up to **1000 L** permitted for PG III.

5. **Stowage and Segregation:**
   - Segregate from Class 1 (Explosives), Class 3 (Flammable Liquids), Class 4.1 (Flammable Solids), and Class 5.2 (Organic Peroxides) — Code references SG35, SG38, SG49.
   - "Away from" sources of heat and ignition.
   - Stowage factor for bulk cargo: ~**0.83 m³/t**.

6. **Documentation:**
   - **Dangerous Goods Declaration (DGD)**.
   - **Container/Vehicle Packing Certificate**.
   - **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS)**.

**(7 marks: 2 for classification + 2 for marking + 2 for packaging/segregation + 1 for documentation)**

---

**(b) Disaster Preparedness and Risk Reduction (7 marks)**

**Concept:**
Disaster preparedness in the fertilizer industry is a structured cycle of **planning, training, equipping, and exercising** to handle chemical emergencies (ammonia leaks, ammonium nitrate fires, acid spills).

**Chemical Accidents (EPPR) Rules, 1996 — Key Provisions:**

1. **Four-Tier Crisis Group Structure:**

| Tier | Body | Chairman | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Central Crisis Group (CCG)** | Secretary, MoEFCC | National policy & coordination |
| 2 | **State Crisis Group (SCG)** | Chief Secretary of State | State-level preparedness |
| 3 | **District Crisis Group (DCG)** | District Collector | Off-site planning & response |
| 4 | **Local Crisis Group (LCG)** | Factory Manager | On-site response |

2. **On-Site Emergency Plan (OEP):** Required under MSIHC Rule 14. Components — alarm system, evacuation routes, fire-fighting, first-aid, spill control, mock drills every 6 months.

3. **Off-Site Emergency Plan (OSEP):** Coordinated by DCG; integrates police, hospitals, fire services, mutual aid industries.

4. **Risk Reduction Measures:**
   - **Engineering:** Dikes, leak detection sensors, water spray curtains, scrubbers.
   - **Administrative:** Permit-to-Work, MSDS, training, audits.
   - **PPE:** Full-face respirators with ammonia cartridges, chemical suits, self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA).

5. **Major Accidents that shaped Indian Regulations:**
   - **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** → MSIHC Rules 1989, EPA 1986, PLI Act 1991.
   - **Delhi HPCL fire (1997)** → strengthened OISD standards.
   - **Jaipur IOC depot fire (2009)** → enhanced off-site plan review.

**(7 marks: 2 for concept + 2 for crisis group table + 2 for preparedness measures + 1 for case studies)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing OEP and OSEP** — On-Site is the plant's internal plan; Off-Site is the district-level integrated plan. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "UN 1005" for Ammonia but listing it as Class 3 (Flammable Liquid).** Ammonia is **UN 1005, Class 2.3 Toxic Gas** (also subsidiary 2.1 flammable). Students frequently lose marks here.
> 3. **Forgetting threshold quantities.** Always list at least **3–4 threshold quantities** from MSIHC Schedule II — it's a guaranteed 2-mark question.
> 4. **Treating "ammonium nitrate" and "ammonium nitrate solution" as the same UN number.** They differ: solid UN 1942/2067 vs. solution UN 2426.
> 5. **Skipping the UN pictograms in GHS explanations.** Always draw or describe the 9 GHS pictograms in your answer to score full marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision — Master These 25 Points:**

- [x] **MSIHC Rules, 1989** = Hazardous chemical regulation triggered by **Bhopal tragedy**, enacted under **EPA 1986**.
- [x] **Threshold quantities** — Ammonia **50 t**, Ammonium Nitrate **350 t**, Chlorine **10 t**, H₂SO₄ **100 t**, HF **50 t**.
- [x] **EPA 1986** = Umbrella environmental law in India (also called "Mother Act").
- [x] **Factories Act, 1948** Section 7A = **Safety Audit**, Schedule I = Hazardous processes including fertilizer.
- [x] **PLI Act, 1991** = Mandatory **one-time** insurance for victims.
- [x] **Chemical Accidents Rules, 1996** = Created **4-tier Crisis Group** (CCG, SCG, DCG, LCG).
- [x] **NDMA, 2005** = Apex body chaired by **Prime Minister**.
- [x] **ISO 45001:2018** replaced **OHSAS 18001** in March 2021; based on **PDCA / Annex SL**.
- [x] **GHS** has **9 hazard classes**, **2 signal words** (Danger/Warning), **70 H-statements**, **16 pictograms**.
- [x] **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** = PSM standard; ammonia threshold **10,000 lbs** (~4.5 t).
- [x] **ADR** = European road transport of dangerous goods.
- [x] **RID** = European rail transport of dangerous goods.
- [x] **IMDG Code** = Sea transport (IMO, biennial revisions).
- [x] **IATA DGR** = Air transport (annual editions).
- [x] **Bund wall capacity** formula: **V_bund = 1.1 V_max + 0.25 V_total**.
- [x] **RPN formula:** **RPN = S × O × D** (Threshold for CAPA = 100; Redesign = 200).
- [x] **Dow F&EI = F₃ × MF**; max possible = **158**.
- [x] **Probit Equation:** **Y = a + b·ln(C^n·t)**; **50% lethality at Y = 5**.
- [x] **Storage of ammonium nitrate** = ALWAYS segregate from fuels, organics, reducing agents.
- [x] **Bhopal (1984)** — MIC gas; **Oppau (1921)** — ammonium nitrate explosion; **Tianjin (2015)** — chemical warehouse.
- [x] **UN Hazard Classes relevant to fertilizers:** 1, 2 (2.1, 2.3), 3, 5.1, 6.1, 8.
- [x] **Mock drills** for OEP — every **6 months**; OSEP — **annually**.
- [x] **Safety Audit** by external agency — every **2 years** (MSIHC Rule 10).
- [x] **Article 48A** of Indian Constitution = Environment protection directive.
- [x] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the golden principle of risk reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioural Safety Through Mind-set Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Behavioural Safety Through Mind-set Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioural Safety** is a systematic, people-centred approach to occupational safety that focuses on identifying and reinforcing the **safe behaviours** of workers while eliminating at-risk behaviours through observation, feedback, and coaching. It operates on the principle that **nearly 80%–96% of workplace incidents are caused by unsafe behaviours** (human factors) rather than purely unsafe conditions.

**Mind-Set Change** in the context of Behavioural Safety refers to a deliberate, structured cognitive and cultural transformation process that shifts an individual's deep-rooted beliefs, attitudes, and perceptions about safety — moving them from *reactive compliance* (following rules only when watched) to *proactive commitment* (internalising safety as a personal value).

Together, **Behavioural Safety Through Mind-Set Change** is the engineered process of converting an organisation's safety culture from *rule-dependent* to *value-driven*, where every employee instinctively chooses safe behaviour even in the absence of supervision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Helmet on a Two-Wheeler" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an engineering student riding a two-wheeler to college.
>
> - **Behaviour (without mind-set change):** Wears a helmet **only when** there is a police check-post on the road. The moment the checkpoint is gone, the helmet comes off. This is **rule-dependent** behaviour — driven by *fear of penalty*, not by safety value.
>
> - **Mind-Set Change outcome:** The student wears the helmet **every single time**, even on empty roads at 2 AM. Why? Because they have *internalised* that *“my life is non-negotiable”*. The behaviour is now driven by **personal value**, not external policing.
>
> In a fertilizer plant (handling ammonia, sulphuric acid, hot prills), the same principle applies. A mind-set-changed worker does not wear PPE because the Safety Officer is watching — they wear it because *“my family is waiting for me at home.”*

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Working Definition (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **BBS** | Behaviour-Based Safety — a structured observation-and-feedback programme |
| **ABC Model** | Antecedent → Behaviour → Consequence framework |
| **At-Risk Behaviour** | Any action that deviates from a known safe procedure and increases hazard exposure |
| **Safe Behaviour** | An action consistent with established safety standards and procedures |
| **Safety Culture** | The deep, enduring shared beliefs, values, and assumptions about safety in an organisation |
| **Safety Climate** | The *current* perception workers hold about safety at a given moment in time |
| **Near-Miss** | An unplanned event that did not result in injury but had the potential to do so |
| **PPE** | Personal Protective Equipment |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In a 14-mark KTU question, always start with this one-liner: *"Behavioural Safety is a people-centred approach that addresses the 88%–96% of incidents caused by human behaviour, complementing the engineering and procedural controls that address the remaining 4%–12%."* This sets a strong valuation tone immediately.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic "Why" Behind Behavioural Safety

A fertilizer plant has three layers of hazard control (the **Swiss Cheese Model** by James Reason):

1. **Engineering Controls** — vessel design, relief valves, interlocks
2. **Administrative / Procedural Controls** — SOPs, permits-to-work, lockout-tagout
3. **Behavioural Controls** — what the worker actually *does* on the ground

Even the best engineering controls fail when a worker bypasses a valve, removes a guard, or skips a pre-startup check. **Mind-set change** closes this last and most critical gap.

### 2.2 The ABC Model — The Heart of Behavioural Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **A-B-C Model** (originally from behavioural psychology by B.F. Skinner, adapted for safety by E. Scott Geller) is the foundational framework for every BBS programme.

$$\text{ABC Model:}\quad \text{Antecedent} \longrightarrow \text{Behaviour} \longrightarrow \text{Consequence}$$

| Element | Meaning in Fertilizer Plant Context | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Antecedent** (Activator / Trigger) | The cue, situation, or environmental factor that prompts an action | A loud ammonia alarm + a hot reactor needing urgent shutdown |
| **B — Behaviour** (the action) | What the worker *actually does* in response | Technician bypasses the interlock to “save time” |
| **C — Consequence** (Result / Feedback) | The outcome that follows, which shapes *future* behaviour | No immediate injury → worker *learns* that bypassing is “safe” |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** Consequences drive *repeat* behaviour. If the consequence of a safe act is **invisible** (no praise, no recognition) and the consequence of an unsafe act is **also invisible** (no injury this time), the worker will default to the *easier* path — the unsafe one. **Mind-set change re-engineers the consequence loop.**

### 2.3 The Four Stages of Mind-Set Change (Learning Curve — Noel Burch)

```
Stage 1: Unconscious Incompetence   → "I don't know what I don't know"
Stage 2: Conscious   Incompetence   → "I now know I am unsafe"
Stage 3: Conscious   Competence     → "I am safe, but I have to think about it"
Stage 4: Unconscious Competence     → "Safety is automatic — my second nature"
```

A BBS programme is essentially a **training engine** that moves the workforce from Stage 1 to Stage 4 over 18–36 months.

### 2.4 Heinrich's Triangle (Bird's Triangle — Modern Update)

Frank Bird's updated data (1969, from 1.7 million insurance reports) gave the industry-standard incident ratio:

$$\text{Major Injuries : Minor Injuries : Near-Misses} \;=\; 1 : 10 : 30$$

> **Real-world interpretation:** For every **1 fatality** in a fertilizer plant, there are roughly **30 near-misses** that went unreported. Behavioural safety focuses on **near-miss reporting** because near-misses are the *leading indicator* of future major incidents.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Typical Unit / Boundary | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BBS Observation Score** (% Safe Behaviour) | $BSI = \dfrac{N_{safe}}{N_{safe} + N_{at\text{-}risk}} \times 100\%$ | Range: **0%–100%**; Target ≥ **95%** | Tracks BBS programme maturity monthly |
| 2 | **Near-Miss to Injury Ratio** | $NMR = \dfrac{N_{near\text{-}miss}}{N_{injury}}$ | Bird's ratio ≈ **30 : 1** | Detects under-reporting of near-misses |
| 3 | **Total Recordable Incident Rate** | $TRIR = \dfrac{\text{Recordables} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$ | Per million man-hours | Industry benchmark (Fertilizer avg ≈ 1.5) |
| 4 | **Behaviour Observation Coverage** | $BOC = \dfrac{N_{observations}}{N_{workers\,at\,risk}} \times 100\%$ | Target ≥ **80%** | Measures how well risks are being observed |
| 5 | **Mind-set Index (Composite)** | $MI = 0.4\cdot ATT + 0.3\cdot KW + 0.3\cdot INT$ | $ATT$ = Attitude, $KW$ = Knowledge, $INT$ = Intention (each 0–10) | Quantifies cultural mind-set |
| 6 | **At-Risk Behaviour Reduction** | $ARBR = \dfrac{ARB_{baseline} - ARB_{current}}{ARB_{baseline}} \times 100\%$ | Negative if worsening | Validates BBS intervention effectiveness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Inside the markdown table above, all absolute-value / division symbols use the inline-LaTeX form (`\dfrac`, `\times`) — the **vertical pipe `|` is strictly avoided** in the table body to prevent markdown-table syntax breakage.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Fertilizer Industry

- **Ammonia plants:** BBS reduces at-risk behaviours during catalyst loading (highly toxic exposure).
- **Urea prilling towers:** Reduces bypassing of dust-mask protocols.
- **Phosphoric acid plants:** Improves lockout-tagout discipline during maintenance.
- **Sulphuric acid handling:** Cuts down on glove-removal behaviours near transfer points.

Behavioural Safety is **not a substitute** for engineering controls — it is the **fourth wall** of defence that keeps the first three walls from failing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heinrich-Bird Incident Ratio (Logic Walk-through)

The Heinrich-Bird Triangle is empirical (data-driven), not a closed-form theorem. However, the **derivation logic** — frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions — is shown below:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Define population: Let } N = \text{total incidents in a fertilizer plant per year.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Classify by severity: } N = N_{fatal} + N_{serious} + N_{minor} + N_{near\text{-}miss} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Bird's empirical observation (1.7 M reports):} \\
                      & \qquad N_{near\text{-}miss} : N_{minor} : N_{serious} : N_{fatal} \;=\; 600 : 30 : 10 : 1 \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Normalise to a baseline of 1 fatality:} \\
                      & \qquad \dfrac{N_{serious}}{N_{fatal}} = 10, \quad \dfrac{N_{minor}}{N_{fatal}} = 30, \quad \dfrac{N_{near\text{-}miss}}{N_{fatal}} = 600 \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Reduce to working ratio (commonly quoted):} \\
                      & \qquad \boxed{\; N_{fatal} : N_{minor} : N_{near\text{-}miss} \;=\; 1 : 30 : 600 \;} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Implication for BBS: each unreported near-miss is a "future fatality" waiting to happen.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Behavioural Safety metric — the At-Risk Behaviour Frequency (ARBF):} \\
                      & \qquad ARBF = \dfrac{N_{at\text{-}risk}}{N_{total\,observations}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic in plain English:** The pyramid shape exists because **the same root causes** (slip, shortcut, bypass, distraction) generate a large base of low-severity events and a small apex of catastrophic events. Fix the base (behaviours) → the apex naturally shrinks.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — BBS Score Calculation

> **Problem:** A BBS observer in a urea prilling tower watches 50 workers over one shift. She records **43 safe acts** and **7 at-risk acts** (3 without dust masks, 2 standing under suspended loads, 2 not using handrails). Calculate: (a) BBS Score, (b) At-Risk Behaviour Reduction % if the previous month's baseline ARB% was 22%.

**Given:**
- $N_{safe} = 43$
- $N_{at\text{-}risk} = 7$
- $ARB_{baseline} = 22\%$

**Solution (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total observations} &= N_{safe} + N_{at\text{-}risk} \\
&= 43 + 7 \\
&= 50 \text{ acts}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BBS Score (BSI)} &= \dfrac{N_{safe}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{43}{50} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.86 \times 100\% \\
&= 86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A BBS Score of **86%** falls **below** the industry target of **≥ 95%**, signalling that re-training and coaching are required in the prilling tower.

**Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARB_{current} &= \dfrac{7}{50} \times 100\% = 14\% \\
ARBR &= \dfrac{ARB_{baseline} - ARB_{current}}{ARB_{baseline}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{22\% - 14\%}{22\%} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{8}{22} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.3636 \times 100\% \\
&= 36.36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The BBS programme reduced at-risk behaviours by **~36.4%** in one month — a healthy improvement, but the team must continue observations to reach the 95% safe-behaviour target.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mind-Set Change Mechanism

| Phase | Cognitive Lever | Manager's Action | Worker's Internal Shift |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Awareness | **Shock of data** | Show BBS observation data; show near-miss photos | "I didn't realise this was happening at *my* plant" |
| 2. Understanding | **Knowledge transfer** | Conduct BBS workshops, ABC training | "Now I see how my shortcuts hurt people" |
| 3. Internalisation | **Personalised consequence** | Use family-photo boards at plant gate | "I do this for *my* family, not for the company" |
| 4. Ownership | **Empowerment** | Workers lead their own safety observations | "I *own* the safety of my crew" |
| 5. Habituation | **Reinforcement loop** | Recognition, rewards, peer observation | "Safety is how we *do things here*" |

### 3.4 Why Mind-Set Change Fails (Anti-Patterns)

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-pattern 1 — "Blame-and-shame":** Punishment-only systems suppress *reporting*, not unsafe behaviour. Workers hide near-misses → Bird's Triangle becomes invisible → fatal apex grows silently.
>
> **Anti-pattern 2 — "Poster-only safety":** Hanging banners without observation-and-feedback loops has **near-zero** impact on behaviour change (multiple meta-analyses confirm < 5% reduction in incidents).
>
> **Anti-pattern 3 — "BBS as a policing tool":** When observers are seen as inspectors, workers perform safely only during observation (the **Hawthorne Effect**) and revert immediately after.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BBS Implementation Flow — Fertilizer Plant Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["IDENTIFY CRITICAL BEHAVIOURS"] --> nodeB["DEFINE SAFE AND AT-RISK ACTS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TRAIN OBSERVERS PEER LEVEL"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CONDUCT BASELINE OBSERVATIONS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CALCULATE BBS SCORE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"SCORE ABOVE 95 PERCENT?"}
    nodeF -->|NO| nodeG["FEEDBACK AND COACHING SESSION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["REINFORCE SAFE ACTS RECOGNISE WORKER"]
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeF -->|YES| nodeI["SUSTAIN AND MONITOR MONTHLY"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["EXPAND TO NEW DEPARTMENT"]
    J2["ENTERPRISE SAFETY CULTURE"] --> nodeK["END STATE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

**Reading guide:**
- Boxes `nodeA` to `nodeD` = *Setup phase* (Months 1–3).
- Box `nodeE` = *Measurement node*.
- The decision diamond `nodeF` = *Gate criterion* (≥ 95% safe behaviour).
- Loop `nodeG → nodeH → nodeD` = the **continuous improvement spiral** of BBS.
- Final state `nodeK` = a self-sustaining, mind-set-driven safety culture.

### 4.2 Mind-Set Change Progression (Four-Stage Learning Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["STAGE 1 UNCONSCIOUS INCOMPETENCE WORKER UNAWARE OF RISK"] --> nodeS2["STAGE 2 CONSCIOUS INCOMPETENCE WORKER AWARE BUT UNSKILLED"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["STAGE 3 CONSCIOUS COMPETENCE SAFE ACT WITH EFFORT"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["STAGE 4 UNCONSCIOUS COMPETENCE SAFETY IS AUTOMATIC"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["STAGE 5 REFLEXIVE SAFETY MENTORS OTHERS"]

    A1["BBS AWARENESS TRAINING"] -.-> nodeS1
    A2["WORKSHOPS AND DEMONSTRATIONS"] -.-> nodeS2
    A3["COACHING AND PEER OBSERVATION"] -.-> nodeS3
    A4["RECOGNITION AND HABIT LOOPS"] -.-> nodeS4
    A5["LEADERSHIP AND EMPOWERMENT"] -.-> nodeS5
```

**Reading guide:** Each dashed arrow (`-.->`) is the **BBS intervention lever** that pushes the workforce from one cognitive stage to the next. **Stage 5** is the *ultimate behavioural-safety outcome*: workers who not only practise safety reflexively but also mentor newcomers.

### 4.3 ABC Behaviour Loop — Fertilizer Plant Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA1["ANTECEDENT AMMONIA LEAK ALARM + HOT REACTOR"] --> nodeB1["BEHAVIOUR TECHNICIAN BYPASSES INTERLOCK FOR FASTER FIX"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["CONSEQUENCE NO IMMEDIATE INJURY"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeR1["REINFORCEMENT WORKER LEARNS BYPASS IS SAFE"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeA1

    nodeA2["ANTECEDENT AMMONIA LEAK ALARM + HOT REACTOR"] --> nodeB2["BEHAVIOUR TECHNICIAN USES SOP SHUTDOWN SEQUENCE"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2["CONSEQUENCE SAFE OUTCOME PEER PRAISE"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeR2["REINFORCEMENT WORKER REPEATS SAFE ACT"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeA2
```

**Reading guide:** Two parallel loops illustrate the **same antecedent** triggering two **opposite behaviours**. The *Consequence* node determines which loop dominates. **Mind-set change is essentially the engineering of the C-node** (consequence layer) so that the safe loop out-competes the unsafe loop.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — BBS System in a Fertilizer Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER - DATA SOURCES"]
        I1["OBSERVATION FORMS DIGITAL TABLET"]
        I2["NEAR MISS REPORTS"]
        I3["INCIDENT LOGS LTI AND FIRST AID"]
        I4["PPE COMPLIANCE SENSORS"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS_LAYER["PROCESS LAYER - ANALYTICS"]
        P1["BBS SCORE ENGINE"]
        P2["BEHAVIOUR TREND ANALYTICS"]
        P3["MIND SET INDEX CALCULATOR"]
        P4["ROOT CAUSE CLASSIFIER"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER - ACTIONS"]
        O1["DASHBOARD TO PLANT HEAD"]
        O2["DAILY SAFETY COACHING TO CREW"]
        O3["RECOGNITION AND REWARD TRIGGER"]
        O4["TARGETED RE-TRAINING SCHEDULE"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P4
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P3 --> O3
    P4 --> O4
```

**Reading guide:** This block diagram maps the **closed-loop BBS information system**. Inputs flow into the analytics engine, which then drives four downstream actions. The system *closes the feedback loop* — the very mechanism that drives mind-set change at scale.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Behavioural Safety. Why is it considered critical in a fertilizer industry?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Behavioural Safety is a structured approach that focuses on **identifying and reinforcing safe behaviours** while eliminating at-risk behaviours through observation, feedback, and coaching.
>
> In the fertilizer industry, it is critical because **88%–96% of incidents are caused by unsafe behaviours** (human factors) such as bypassing interlocks, skipping PPE, or ignoring lockout-tagout. Even with robust engineering controls (relief valves, alarms, DCS), the *last mile of safety* depends on what a worker actually *does* on the ground. BBS closes this last-mile gap and addresses the human-factor root cause of incidents.

*(Expected answer length: 4–6 lines. Marks awarded for: definition = 1, fertilizer context = 1, 88–96% statistic = 1.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the "Mind-Set Change" concept in two sentences with a real-time example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Mind-set change is the cognitive and cultural process of shifting a worker from **rule-dependent safety** (complying only when supervised) to **value-driven safety** (internalising safety as a personal belief).
>
> **Example:** A contract labourer in an ammonia plant initially wears a respirator only during safety inspections. After attending a BBS workshop and seeing photographs of a colleague who suffered lung damage from H₂S exposure, he begins wearing the respirator *every* shift — even during night-duty when no supervisor is present.

*(Expected answer length: 3–5 lines. Marks awarded for: definition = 1, transformation mechanism = 1, real-time example = 1.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **ABC Model of Behavioural Safety** in detail. How does it apply to a urea plant maintenance scenario?

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **four stages of mind-set change** (Unconscious Incompetence → Unconscious Competence) with a fertilizer-plant example for each stage.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ABC Model — Detailed Answer:**

The ABC Model (Antecedent–Behaviour–Consequence), developed by E. Scott Geller, is the foundational psychological framework for any BBS programme. It posits that every workplace behaviour is triggered by an antecedent and reinforced by a consequence.

- **Antecedent (A):** The situational cue, condition, or environmental trigger that prompts the worker to act. In a urea plant, *the antecedent* could be a **hot prill conveyor belt** that has stopped and needs urgent restart to avoid plant trip. The worker is under time pressure.

- **Behaviour (B):** The worker's actual response. Here, the technician may choose to **bypass the conveyor guard** to manually clear a jam without taking the prescribed lockout-tagout (LOTO) steps — an *at-risk behaviour*.

- **Consequence (C):** The outcome that follows the behaviour. If the technician completes the job faster without injury, the consequence is *invisible* (no harm, no recognition). This **negative reinforcement** locks the unsafe behaviour into habit. Conversely, if the supervisor *observes* the bypass and conducts a non-punitive coaching conversation, the consequence loop is re-engineered to favour the safe behaviour.

**Application to urea plant maintenance:** During annual turnaround, hundreds of LOTO procedures are executed simultaneously. BBS observers trained in the ABC model perform peer-to-peer observations at critical risk points (confined-space entry, hot-work permits). When they observe safe behaviour, they provide immediate *positive feedback*; when they observe at-risk behaviour, they conduct a coaching dialogue using the ABC framework rather than punishment. Over 6 months, this has been shown to reduce at-risk LOTO behaviours by 40–60%.

**(b) Four Stages of Mind-Set Change — Detailed Answer:**

| Stage | Name | Fertilizer-Plant Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unconscious Incompetence** | A new contract worker in a sulphuric acid plant does not know that *glove removal before checking transfer-line temperature* is unsafe. He is unaware of his incompetence. |
| 2 | **Conscious Incompetence** | After his first BBS induction, the worker realises: *"I was doing this wrong."* He now knows the safe procedure but has not yet mastered it. |
| 3 | **Conscious Competence** | The worker now uses the correct glove procedure, but it requires **deliberate mental effort**. He sometimes forgets under fatigue or time pressure. |
| 4 | **Unconscious Competence** | Safe glove-handling has become **automatic**. The worker follows the correct procedure without thinking — even when alone, even at 3 AM, even during a plant emergency. |

**Transition mechanism:** BBS moves workers through these stages via (i) *observation-and-feedback* loops (stages 1→2→3), and (ii) *recognition, peer coaching, and habituation* (stage 3→4). The end-state is a **self-sustaining safety culture** where external supervision becomes unnecessary.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss **Heinrich's Triangle (Bird's Triangle)** and explain its relevance to Behavioural Safety in a fertilizer industry.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a flowchart, describe the **step-by-step implementation of a BBS programme** in a fertilizer plant.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Heinrich's Triangle / Bird's Triangle:**

Originally proposed by H.W. Heinrich (1931) and later refined by Frank Bird (1969) using 1.7 million insurance reports, the triangle describes the **statistical relationship** between incidents of different severities.

$$
\text{Fatal : Serious : Minor : Near-Miss} \;=\; 1 : 10 : 30 : 600
$$

This empirical ratio is *industry-invariant* and applies to fertilizer plants with remarkable consistency. The **practical implication** is profound: for every fatality, there are roughly **600 near-misses** that have already occurred — most of which are *never reported*. These unreported near-misses are the **leading indicators** of future major incidents.

**Relevance to Behavioural Safety:**
- BBS shifts the safety focus from *lagging indicators* (fatalities, lost-time injuries) to *leading indicators* (safe-behaviour scores, near-miss reports).
- Each near-miss represents a *successful safe behaviour* that prevented escalation.
- A high near-miss reporting rate is a **good sign** — it means workers trust the system and are observing attentively.
- A high at-risk-behaviour observation rate combined with a *low* near-miss report rate signals **cultural suppression** of safety data — a red flag.

**Why this matters in fertilizer plants:** Ammonia leaks, urea dust explosions, and sulphuric acid burns all share a common pattern — they have many "silent" precursors (small leaks, unusual smells, minor skin contacts) that go unreported. BBS closes this reporting gap.

**(b) BBS Implementation Flowchart:**

```
Step 1: Top management commitment & BBS policy launch
            ↓
Step 2: Select pilot area (e.g., Urea Prilling Tower)
            ↓
Step 3: Identify critical behaviours (with workers)
            ↓
Step 4: Train peer observers (12–15% of workforce)
            ↓
Step 5: Define safe / at-risk checklist
            ↓
Step 6: Baseline observation (4 weeks) → BSI_initial
            ↓
Step 7: Feedback & coaching (weekly)
            ↓
Step 8: Recognition & rewards for safe acts
            ↓
Step 9: Monthly BBS score review at management meeting
            ↓
Step 10: Roll out to next department (after BSI ≥ 90%)
            ↓
        CULTURE TRANSFORMATION (24–36 months)
```

Each step must be **worker-driven**, not management-imposed, to avoid the "policing-tool" anti-pattern. Peer observation is the single most important success factor — workers accept feedback far better from peers than from supervisors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Writing "Behavioural Safety = Training"** — *Incorrect.* Training is only ~30% of BBS. The rest is observation, feedback, recognition, and culture change. Marks lost: 2.
> 2. **Ignoring the ABC framework** — In any 14-mark BBS question, the ABC model is *mandatory*. Omitting it costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Conflating Safety Culture with Safety Climate** — Culture = *deep, enduring values*; Climate = *current perception*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Marks lost: 2.
> 4. **Quoting "80% of accidents are due to human error"** — The KTU-accepted range is **88%–96%** (Bird's updated data). The 80% figure is outdated (Heinrich, 1931). Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **Skipping the "near-miss reporting" angle** — Bird's ratio (1 : 30 : 600) is almost always worth at least 2 marks in any 14-mark question on Behavioural Safety.
> 6. **Presenting BBS as a substitute for engineering controls** — BBS is the *fourth wall*, not a replacement. Marks lost: 2.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Behavioural Safety Through Mind-Set Change — KTU Module 3 Cheat Sheet**

- ☐ **Core premise:** **88%–96%** of workplace incidents are caused by **unsafe behaviours**, not unsafe conditions (Bird, 1969).
- ☐ **BBS definition:** Structured, people-centred approach using **observation + feedback + coaching** to reinforce safe behaviours.
- ☐ **Mind-set change definition:** Shift from *rule-dependent* → *value-driven* safety behaviour.
- ☐ **ABC Model:** **Antecedent → Behaviour → Consequence** — consequences drive repeat behaviour; the BBS lever is at the *Consequence* node.
- ☐ **Four Stages of Mind-Set Change:** *Unconscious Incompetence → Conscious Incompetence → Conscious Competence → Unconscious Competence.* (Plus a *Stage 5* — Reflexive / Mentor stage — for culture champions.)
- ☐ **Bird's Triangle ratio:** **1 : 30 : 600** (Fatal : Minor : Near-Miss). Near-misses are *leading indicators*.
- ☐ **BBS Score formula:** $BSI = \dfrac{N_{safe}}{N_{total\,obs}} \times 100\%$; target **≥ 95%**.
- ☐ **At-Risk Behaviour Reduction:** $ARBR = \dfrac{ARB_{base} - ARB_{now}}{ARB_{base}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Five phases of mind-set mechanism:** *Awareness → Understanding → Internalisation → Ownership → Habituation.*
- ☐ **BBS Implementation Steps:** Commit → Pilot → Identify behaviours → Train observers → Baseline → Feedback → Recognition → Review → Roll out.
- ☐ **Safety Culture vs Safety Climate:** Culture = *deep values*; Climate = *current perception*. Both must be measured (culture every 2–3 yrs; climate annually).
- ☐ **Anti-patterns to avoid:** (i) Blame-and-shame punishment systems, (ii) Poster-only safety (zero impact), (iii) BBS as a policing tool (Hawthorne Effect only).
- ☐ **Fertilizer-plant applications:** Ammonia handling, urea prilling towers, phosphoric acid transfer, sulphuric acid loading, LOTO during turnaround.
- ☐ **Mind-set Index:** $MI = 0.4 \cdot ATT + 0.3 \cdot KW + 0.3 \cdot INT$ — a composite metric used in advanced BBS dashboards.
- ☐ **Closing thought (use as a quotation in exams):** *"Engineers design the plant; workers — through their behaviour — decide whether the plant is safe."*

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — BEHAVIOURAL SAFETY THROUGH MIND-SET CHANGE**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020-aligned Outcome-Based Education framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyse → Evaluate → Create).*
