# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **Q1.** Define the term **Highly Hazardous Chemical (HHC)** as per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119. List any **four** examples of HHCs commonly found in a fertilizer manufacturing complex.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A Highly Hazardous Chemical (HHC), as defined by OSHA in 29 CFR 1910.119 Appendix A, is any chemical that has the potential to cause catastrophic harm (fatality, serious injury, or significant environmental damage) when present in a process in quantities equal to or exceeding its **Threshold Quantity (TQ)**.

**Four Examples in a Fertilizer Plant:**
1. **Anhydrous Ammonia (NH₃)** — TQ = 10,000 lbs (4,535 kg) — toxic and flammable.
2. **Chlorine (Cl₂)** — TQ = 1,500 lbs (680 kg) — highly toxic gas.
3. **Nitric Acid (HNO₃, >94.5%)** — corrosive and strong oxidizer.
4. **Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — strong corrosive, dehydration hazard.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Award full 3 marks only if definition is precise and examples are plant-specific. Generic examples (like gasoline) fetch only partial credit.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** What is **Process Safety Management (PSM)**? Enumerate the **three pillars** of the Comprehensive Management Program (CMP).

**Model Answer:**

**PSM Definition:**
Process Safety Management (PSM) is a comprehensive regulatory framework (originally codified by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 in 1992, post-Bhopal disaster) that integrates engineering, procedural, and managerial controls to prevent or minimize catastrophic releases of HHCs in industrial processes. PSM consists of **14 mandatory elements** including Process Hazard Analysis, Mechanical Integrity, Management of Change, and Emergency Response.

**Three Pillars of CMP:**
1. **Technology** — Engineering hardware controls (relief valves, safety instrumented systems, containment dikes, ventilation, alarms, interlocks).
2. **Procedures** — Documented work instructions (SOPs, operating procedures, maintenance procedures, emergency response plans, work permits).
3. **Management Practices** — Organizational and human systems (training programs, competency assessment, leadership commitment, safety culture, KPIs, audits).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** PSM definition + correctly naming the three pillars = 2 marks; brief explanation of each pillar with one example = 1 mark.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

> **Q. (a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **14 elements of Process Safety Management (PSM)** as per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A urea reactor has an initiating cause (tube rupture) with frequency $f_{IC} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ per year. The system has the following Independent Protection Layers (IPLs):
> - IPL-1 (BPCS alarm + operator action): PFD = $5 \times 10^{-1}$
> - IPL-2 (SIS trip): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - IPL-3 (Pressure Relief Valve): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - IPL-4 (Containment Dyke): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - IPL-5 (Emergency Response): PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
>
> If the **tolerable frequency target** is $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year, calculate the **mitigated frequency**, the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**, and state whether the system meets the risk target. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 14 PSM Elements (7 marks):

*(Student should draw the PSM Wheel diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes and list the 14 elements)*

**The 14 Elements:**
1. Employee Participation
2. Process Safety Information (PSI)
3. Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
4. Operating Procedures
5. Training
6. Contractors
7. Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)
8. Mechanical Integrity (Asset Integrity)
9. Hot Work Permit
10. Management of Change (MOC)
11. Emergency Planning and Response
12. Incident Investigation
13. Compliance Audits
14. Trade Secrets

**Brief explanation of any 5 elements (each ~1 mark):**
- **Process Safety Information:** Compilation of hazard data (toxicity, flammability, reactivity), technology data (process flow, P&IDs), and equipment data (design specs, materials).
- **Process Hazard Analysis:** Systematic study to identify and evaluate hazards using techniques like HAZOP, FMEA, What-If.
- **Mechanical Integrity:** Program to maintain integrity of pressure vessels, piping, relief valves, instruments through inspection, testing, and maintenance.
- **Management of Change:** Formal review and approval of all changes to process, equipment, procedures, or personnel.
- **Compliance Audits:** Evaluation every 3 years to verify PSM program effectiveness.

#### Part (b) — LOPA Calculation (7 marks):

**Step 1: State the LOPA Equation** *(1 mark)*
$$
f_{mitigated} = f_{IC} \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \times PFD_4 \times PFD_5
$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values** *(2 marks)*
$$
f_{mitigated} = (2 \times 10^{-2}) \times (5 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})^3 \times (1 \times 10^{-1})
$$

**Step 3: Group the Coefficients** *(1 mark)*
$$
f_{mitigated} = 2 \times 5 \times 10^{(-2 - 1 - 2 - 2 - 2 - 1)}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Exponent** *(1 mark)*
$$
f_{mitigated} = 10 \times 10^{-10} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate RRF** *(1 mark)*
$$
RRF = \frac{f_{IC}}{f_{mitigated}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion & Comparison** *(1 mark)*
Since $f_{mitigated} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \ll f_{tolerable} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year, the system **comfortably meets** the risk target. The risk reduction factor of **20 million** indicates the IPLs collectively provide excellent protection — the system is **over-designed** and any single IPL failure is still tolerable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **take the cube of $10^{-2}$** for the three identical PFDs (PSL-2, PSV, Dyke). Common error: writing $1 \times 10^{-2}$ once instead of three times. Lose 1 mark here.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

> **Q. (a)** Explain the **Comprehensive Management Program (CMP)** with the help of its **three pillars**. Describe how **technology, procedures, and management practices** integrate to manage hazards from HHCs in a fertilizer plant. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain the **Management of Change (MOC)** process in detail. Describe the **eight mandatory steps** with a suitable example from a fertilizer plant. What is the difference between a **permanent change** and a **temporary change** in the context of MOC? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Comprehensive Management Program (7 marks):

**Definition:** *(1 mark)*
The Comprehensive Management Program (CMP) is a holistic, integrated safety framework that combines **Technology**, **Procedures**, and **Management Practices** to control the hazards of Highly Hazardous Chemicals (HHCs) in industrial processes. It is founded on the principle of **Defence-in-Depth (Swiss Cheese Model)**.

**The Three Pillars:** *(4 marks — distribute equally)*

**1. Technology Pillar (Engineering Controls):**
- Hardware and software systems that physically or logically prevent, detect, or mitigate hazardous releases.
- Examples in a fertilizer plant:
  - **Pressure Relief Valves (PSVs)** on ammonia storage bullets.
  - **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** with SIL 2/3 trips.
  - **Distributed Control System (DCS)** with interlocks.
  - **Containment Dykes** around acid storage tanks.
  - **Scrubbers and flares** for toxic gas neutralization.

**2. Procedures Pillar (Administrative Controls):**
- Documented work instructions that standardize human actions.
- Examples:
  - **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** for reactor startup/shutdown.
  - **Work Permit Systems** (Confined Space, Hot Work, LOTO, Working at Height).
  - **Emergency Response Plans** for ammonia leaks.
  - **HAZOP/LOPA reports** as living documents.

**3. Management Practices Pillar (Organizational Controls):**
- Human and organizational systems that ensure technology and procedures function as intended.
- Examples:
  - **Competency-based Training** programs with annual revalidation.
  - **Behavior-Based Safety (BBS)** observations.
  - **Leading & Lagging KPIs** (e.g., TRIR, near-miss reporting rate).
  - **Audits & Inspections** (3-yearly compliance audit).
  - **Visible Leadership Commitment** (management walk-throughs).

**Integration & Synergy:** *(2 marks)*
- The three pillars are **interdependent** — technology fails without procedures; procedures fail without trained people; management practices fail without audit.
- A change in technology (e.g., new compressor) triggers a procedural update (new SOP), which triggers a management practice update (retraining).
- The pillars operate as **parallel defences** — failure of one does not collapse the system.

#### Part (b) — Management of Change (7 marks):

**Definition of MOC:** *(1 mark)*
MOC is a formal, documented process to ensure that **any change** to process chemistry, technology, equipment, procedures, or facilities is reviewed for safety implications, approved by competent authority, documented, and communicated before implementation. It is a mandatory PSM element (OSHA 1910.119(l)).

**Eight Mandatory Steps:** *(4 marks)*

| Step | Activity | Fertilizer Plant Example |
|------|----------|--------------------------|
| 1. **Trigger Identification** | A change is proposed | Increase urea reactor operating temperature from 185 °C to 195 °C |
| 2. **MOC Request Initiation** | Document in MOC register | Engineer files MOC-2024-047 with justification |
| 3. **Initial Screening** | Like-for-like or substantive? | Substantive — proceed to full review |
| 4. **Multi-Disciplinary Team Review** | Process + Safety + Ops + Maint + Env | Re-run HAZOP on changed node, LOPA check |
| 5. **Risk Assessment & Control Identification** | Update PSI, P&IDs, PFDs | New relief valve set point required; operator training needed |
| 6. **Approval by Authorized Person** | Plant Manager / HSE Head | Sign-off after all controls verified |
| 7. **Implementation & Communication** | Execute change + update docs | Update SOP, P&ID, instrument data sheet; toolbox talk to operators |
| 8. **Closure & Documentation** | Lessons learned | As-built red-line mark-up; archive in MOC register |

**Permanent vs Temporary Change:** *(2 marks)*

- **Permanent Change:** A long-term or irreversible modification to the process (e.g., new reactor installed, new catalyst, revised SOP). Full MOC cycle including all eight steps and proper documentation.
- **Temporary Change:** A short-term modification with a defined end date (e.g., bypassing an interlock for 2 weeks during maintenance, using a temporary pump). MOC is still required, but:
  - Must have a **predefined duration** (typically ≤ 90 days).
  - Must specify **restoration steps** at end of period.
  - Must undergo **full review** if extension requested.
  - Emergency changes (e.g., to prevent imminent hazard) may be implemented immediately but MUST undergo MOC review within 24–48 hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often **omit MOC for temporary changes** (e.g., interlock bypass), losing 2 marks. MOC applies to **ALL** changes — temporary, permanent, emergency.
> 2. **Like-for-like replacement** (e.g., exact same model valve) is NOT an MOC — but **change of vendor with different specifications** IS. Many students wrongly classify the latter as like-for-like, losing 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize This Block Before Walking Into the Exam Hall:**

### Quick-Recall Summary Points

- ✅ **HHC Threshold Quantity (TQ):** NH₃ = 10,000 lb; Cl₂ = 1,500 lb; HNO₃ (94.5%) = 500 lb (OSHA Appendix A).
- ✅ **PSM = 14 Elements** — Memorize all in sequence: Employee Participation → PSI → PHA → SOPs → Training → Contractors → PSSR → Mechanical Integrity → Hot Work → MOC → Emergency Response → Incident Investigation → Compliance Audit → Trade Secrets.
- ✅ **Three Pillars of CMP** — Technology + Procedures + Management Practices = **Defence-in-Depth**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** — Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (most to least effective).
- ✅ **LOPA Formula** — $f_{mitigated} = f_{IC} \times \prod PFD_i$. Each IPL PFD typically ≤ 10⁻².
- ✅ **Asset Integrity = Lifecycle Management** — Design → Fabricate → Install → Operate → Inspect → Maintain → Decommission.
- ✅ **Reliability Function** — $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; MTBF = $1/\lambda$; Availability = $MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ✅ **MOC Trigger** — Anything that changes process chemistry, technology, equipment, procedures, personnel, or facilities.
- ✅ **MOC for Temporary Changes** — Required, must have defined end date and restoration plan.
- ✅ **Contractor Safety 7-Stage Cycle** — Scope → Prequalification → Contract → Pre-Job Briefing → Execution → Audit → Closure.
- ✅ **Three Qualities of an IPL** — Effective, Independent, Auditable.
- ✅ **Wind Dispersion (Pasquill-Gifford)** — $C(x) = Q / (\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u)$.
- ✅ **Bhopal Disaster (1984)** — Catalyzed OSHA PSM Rule (1992) and global Seveso Directives.
- ✅ **PSM Wheel diagram, MOC flowchart, Asset Integrity cycle, Contractor lifecycle** — all diagram types are likely 7-mark sketch questions.

### Common KTU Viva Questions

- **Q: Is OSHA PSM applicable to Indian fertilizer plants?** — Not directly by name, but the Indian OSH Code 2020 and OISD standards adopt equivalent requirements; additionally, **Responsible Care** (ICCA) and corporate HSE policies align with PSM.
- **Q: What is the difference between PSM and Occupational Safety?** — PSM focuses on **catastrophic process-related** events (fires, explosions, toxic releases) — broader than worker personal safety (slips, trips, falls) covered under OSHA 1910 Subpart J.
- **Q: Can a contractor perform a PHA?** — A contractor may **facilitate** a PHA, but the **owner has ultimate accountability** and the team must include personnel knowledgeable about the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — PESFT633.** These notes cover the complete KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for the topic "Management of Hazards Associated with Highly Hazardous Chemicals" including CMP, PSM Asset Integrity, MOC, and Contractor Safety Management. For maximum retention, redraw **all five Mermaid diagrams by hand** and solve **at least one LOPA problem** before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Pollutants Generated in Fertiliser Manufacturing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Major Pollutants Generated in Fertiliser Manufacturing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pollutant (KTU 2024 Definition):** A pollutant generated in fertiliser manufacturing is any gaseous, liquid, or solid substance released into the environment (air, water, or soil) as a direct or indirect by-product of the chemical conversion of raw materials (such as natural gas, naphtha, sulphur, phosphate rock, and potash) into nitrogenous, phosphatic, or potassic nutrient products, in concentrations that exceed the permissible limits prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, major pollutants from the fertiliser industry are classified into three principal environmental compartments:

| Pollutant Stream | Common Name in Industry | Typical Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Pollutants** | Stack emissions & fugitives | Reformer stacks, acid plants, prilling towers |
| **Water Pollutants** | Liquid effluents | Process condensate, cooling blowdown, gypsum pond water |
| **Solid Pollutants** | Hazardous & non-hazardous waste | Phosphogypsum, spent catalyst, slag, sludge |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fertiliser Plant as a "Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Think of a Fertiliser Plant Like a Pressure Cooker Kitchen:**
> Imagine you are cooking a five-dish meal in a single busy kitchen. To prepare the main dish (urea or DAP), you must first pre-cook the ingredients (steam reforming, acidulation, neutralisation). During this pre-cooking:
> - **Smoke** rises from the burner (analogous to **NOx, SO2, CO2** from reformer and acid stacks).
> - **Steam** leaks from the lid (analogous to **ammonia slip** and **acid mist**).
> - **Spilled water** collects on the floor (analogous to **process condensate with ammonia and urea**).
> - **Peelings and bones** are thrown away (analogous to **phosphogypsum** and **spent catalyst**).
> Just as a chef must manage smoke, steam, spillage, and waste, an environmental engineer must identify, quantify, and treat every pollutant stream — otherwise the kitchen (environment) becomes polluted.

### 1.3 Categorical Hierarchy of Pollutants

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to master **seven** primary pollutant categories emitted across the fertiliser value chain:

1. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NO, NO2, N2O)** — collectively termed **NOx**.
2. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO2)** and **Sulphur Trioxide (SO3)**.
3. **Ammonia (NH3)** — both gaseous slip and dissolved.
4. **Fluorides (HF, SiF4)** — specific to phosphatic fertilisers.
5. **Particulate Matter (PM10, PM2.5)** — dust from rock grinding, prilling, granulation.
6. **Greenhouse Gases — CO2 and N2O.**
7. **Process Liquid Effluents** — containing ammonia, urea, nitrates, phosphates, fluorides, and **Heavy Metals (Cd, Pb, Hg, As, Cr)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners in Dec 2024 and June 2025 consistently tested **process-wise identification of pollutants**. Memorise at least **two specific pollutants per major process** (ammonia, urea, nitric acid, sulphuric acid, phosphoric acid, DAP/NPK).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Process-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Ammonia Plant (Steam Reforming of Natural Gas / Naphtha)

**Core Reaction Sequence:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, } 850^\circ\text{C}} \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Primary Reformer})$$

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Shift converter}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Shift Conversion})$$

$$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{High pressure}} 2\text{NH}_3 \quad (\text{Ammonia Synthesis Loop})$$

**Pollutants Generated:**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Why It Forms |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** (high volume) | Shift converter & methanator outlet | Inherent to the process for H2 purification |
| **CO** (trace) | Reformer furnace, partial oxidation | Incomplete combustion of fuel |
| **NOx** | Reformer furnace burners | Thermal NOx at **>1300 °C** flame temperature |
| **H2S** | Feed gas desulphurisation | Sulphur impurity in natural gas |
| **NH3 slip** | Synthesis loop purges | Incomplete conversion (typical 15–20% per pass) |
| **Cyanide (CN⁻)** | Wastewater from CO2 scrubber | Forms in alkaline scrubbers |
| **Particulates** | Catalyst dust, fly ash | Attrition in reformer/secondary reformer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **CO2 released from an ammonia plant** is the **single largest greenhouse gas emission** in nitrogen fertiliser manufacture. A 1000 MTPD ammonia plant emits approximately **1700–2000 MT/day of CO2** — this is the foundation of **Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)** discussions in the industry.

---

#### 2.1.2 Nitric Acid Plant (Ostwald Process)

**Core Reactions:**

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Rh gauze, } 900^\circ\text{C}} 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2$$

$$3\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **Nitric Oxide (NO)** and **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2)** — collectively **NOx**, the dominant stack emission.
- **Nitrous Oxide (N2O)** — a potent greenhouse gas with **GWP ≈ 298** (over 100 years).
- **Ammonia slip** — unconverted NH3 from the converter (typically < 10 ppm).
- **Nitrate-laden wastewater** from absorber tail gas scrubbing.

---

#### 2.1.3 Sulphuric Acid Plant (Contact Process)

**Core Reactions:**

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

$$2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{V}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ catalyst}} 2\text{SO}_3$$

$$\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_7 \text{ (oleum)}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **SO2** — primary stack emission (must be reduced via **Double Contact Double Absorption — DCDA** to < **400 mg/Nm3**).
- **SO3 / Acid Mist** — forms sub-micron sulphuric acid droplets.
- **Spent V2O5 Catalyst** — solid hazardous waste.

---

#### 2.1.4 Urea Plant

**Core Reaction:**

$$2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **NH3 in vent gases** from carbamate decomposer.
- **CO2 vent losses** from purge.
- **Urea dust** from prilling tower.
- **Process condensate** containing **NH3 (0.5–2%)** and **urea (0.1–1%)** — must be hydrolysed before discharge.

---

#### 2.1.5 Phosphoric Acid Plant (Wet Process)

**Core Reaction:**

$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 10\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 10\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{HF}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

| Pollutant | Quantity per MT of P2O5 | Toxicity |
|---|---|---|
| **Phosphogypsum** | **4.5–5.5 MT** | Solid waste (radioactive due to Ra-226) |
| **Fluorides (HF, SiF4)** | **20–60 kg** | Severe respiratory irritant |
| **Phosphate dust** | Variable | Particulate matter |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | 5–100 g | Heavy metal, carcinogen |
| **Process water with P2O5** | 5–15 m³ | Eutrophication agent |

---

#### 2.1.6 DAP / NPK / Complex Fertiliser Plant

**Reactions:**

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \text{ (MAP)}$$

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 \text{ (DAP)}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **NH3 slip** from granulator and dryer.
- **Fluorides** evolved during acidulation.
- **Particulate dust** from granulation.
- **HCl fumes** when MOP is added.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Board Examinations — All Major Pollutant Equations**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_i = Q \times C_i \times 10^{-6}$ | Emission rate of pollutant *i* from stack gas | kg/hr |
| 2 | $C_i = \dfrac{E_i}{Q}$ | Pollutant concentration in stack (back-calculated) | mg/Nm³ |
| 3 | $E_{\text{NOx}} = 1.57 \times F_{\text{NH}_3} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{NOx}}}{M_{\text{NH}_3}}$ | NOx emission from nitric acid plant per tonne NH3 oxidised | kg NOx/MT HNO3 |
| 4 | $E_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.05 \times F_{\text{NH}_3}$ | N2O emission factor (modern plant) | kg N2O/MT HNO3 |
| 5 | $E_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \times S \times \dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{\text{S}}} \times (1-\eta_{\text{conv}})$ | SO2 emission from sulphur content in feed | kg SO2/hr |
| 6 | $E_{\text{CO}_2} = 1.73 \times \text{NH}_3 \text{ production (MTPD)}$ | CO2 emission factor for ammonia plant (average) | MTCO2/day |
| 7 | $M_{\text{gypsum}} = 5 \times \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ production (MTPD)}$ | Phosphogypsum generated per MT P2O5 | MT/day |
| 8 | $F_{\text{fluoride}} = 0.04 \times \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ production}$ | Fluoride emission factor (uncontrolled) | kg F/MT P2O5 |
| 9 | $L_{\text{eff}} = Q \times (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})$ | Mass loading removed by effluent treatment plant | kg/day |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{control}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency of control equipment | % |

Where:
- $E_i$ = emission rate of pollutant *i*
- $Q$ = flue gas volumetric flow rate (Nm³/hr)
- $C_i$ = concentration of pollutant (mg/Nm³)
- $F_{\text{NH}_3}$ = mass flow rate of NH3 oxidised (kg/hr)
- $M_{\text{NOx}} = 46$ g/mol; $M_{\text{NH}_3} = 17$ g/mol
- $M_{\text{SO}_2} = 64$ g/mol; $M_{\text{S}} = 32$ g/mol
- $\eta_{\text{conv}}$ = conversion efficiency of SO2 → SO3
- $\eta_{\text{control}}$ = removal efficiency

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These pollutant-generation equations are not academic abstractions — they are the **backbone of**:

1. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** reports submitted to **MoEFCC** before plant expansion.
2. **Consent to Operate** applications under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.
3. **Carbon credit calculations** under **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme for ammonia plants.
4. **Pollution control equipment sizing** — for example, **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** for NOx abatement requires the exact emission rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation: NOx Emission from a Nitric Acid Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 — KTU Board Frequency: HIGH**
> A nitric acid plant oxidises **10,000 kg/hr of ammonia** in the Ostwald converter. The conversion efficiency of NH3 to NO is **96%**, and 90% of the resulting NO is oxidised to NO2. The tail gas leaving the absorption tower contains unabsorbed NO and NO2 totalling **1200 ppm by volume** (dry basis, at STP). Calculate:
> (a) The mass of NO emitted per hour.
> (b) The volume of NOx (as NO2 equivalent) in Nm³/hr if the flue gas flow is **50,000 Nm³/hr**.
> (c) Whether the plant complies with the CPCB standard of **400 mg/Nm3** as NO2.

#### Step 1 — Determine NH3 actually converted to NO

The Ostwald reaction requires **17 g NH3 to produce 30 g NO**:

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

So the stoichiometric ratio is:

$$\dfrac{M_{\text{NO}}}{M_{\text{NH}_3}} = \dfrac{30}{17}$$

NH3 converted to NO:

$$m_{\text{NH}_3,\text{converted}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.96 = 9{,}600 \text{ kg/hr}$$

NO produced (if fully absorbed):

$$m_{\text{NO, theoretical}} = 9{,}600 \times \dfrac{30}{17} = 16{,}941.18 \text{ kg/hr}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Real-World NO to NO2 Oxidation

90% of NO becomes NO2:

$$m_{\text{NO}_2,\text{ formed}} = 16{,}941.18 \times 0.90 = 15{,}247.06 \text{ kg/hr}$$

The remaining 10% remains as NO:

$$m_{\text{NO, unoxidised}} = 16{,}941.18 \times 0.10 = 1{,}694.12 \text{ kg/hr}$$

#### Step 3 — Convert NO to NO2-equivalent for emission reporting

Using molecular weight ratio (46/30):

$$m_{\text{NO}, \text{as NO}_2} = 1{,}694.12 \times \dfrac{46}{30} = 2{,}597.65 \text{ kg/hr as NO}_2 \text{ equivalent}$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate Total NOx as NO2 Equivalent

$$\text{Total NOx (as NO}_2\text{)} = 15{,}247.06 + 2{,}597.65 = 17{,}844.71 \text{ kg/hr}$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate Concentration in mg/Nm³ (Using Given Flue Gas Flow)

Given flue gas flow $Q = 50{,}000$ Nm³/hr, and concentration in emission = **1200 ppm**:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{NOx (as NO}_2\text{)}} &= \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{NO}_2}}{22.4} \\
&= 1200 \times \dfrac{46}{22.4} \\
&= 1200 \times 2.0536 \\
&= 2{,}464.29 \text{ mg/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 6 — Compliance Check Against CPCB Standard

$$\text{CPCB Standard} = 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

$$\text{Actual} = 2{,}464.29 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 > 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

**Result:** The plant **DOES NOT comply** with CPCB norms. A **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** or **Extended Absorption** system must be installed to reduce NOx levels.

---

### 3.2 Stoichiometric Derivation: CO2 Emission from an Ammonia Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 — KTU Board Frequency: MODERATE**
> An ammonia plant produces **1500 MTPD (Metric Tonnes Per Day)** using steam reforming of natural gas. Calculate:
> (a) Daily CO2 emission in MT/day.
> (b) Annual CO2 emission in tonnes.
> (c) The number of carbon credits (1 credit = 1 tCO2) earned if the plant reduces emissions by **20%** via CCS.

#### Step 1 — Use the Standard CO2 Emission Factor for Ammonia

$$\text{Emission Factor (EF)} = 1.73 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{tNH}_3$$

#### Step 2 — Daily CO2 Emission

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{CO}_2, \text{daily}} &= \text{Production} \times \text{EF} \\
&= 1500 \text{ MT/day} \times 1.73 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{tNH}_3 \\
&= 2{,}595 \text{ MT/day}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3 — Annual CO2 Emission (assuming 330 operating days)

$$E_{\text{CO}_2, \text{annual}} = 2{,}595 \times 330 = 856{,}350 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

#### Step 4 — Carbon Credits from 20% Reduction

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ reduced} = 856{,}350 \times 0.20 = 171{,}270 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

$$\text{Carbon Credits} = 171{,}270 \text{ credits/year (since 1 credit} = 1 \text{ tCO}_2\text{)}$$

At a market price of approximately **₹800–1500 per credit**, this translates to ₹13.7–25.7 crores/year in revenue — a major economic incentive for CCS adoption.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant-to-Process Mapping

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Table — Memorise for 14-Mark Questions**

| Process | Primary Air Pollutant | Secondary Air Pollutant | Liquid Effluent | Solid Waste | Critical Control Technology |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ammonia (Reforming)** | CO2 (high volume) | NOx, CO, NH3 slip | Cyanide, NH3 wastewater | Spent catalyst (NiO) | MEA / Benfield CO2 removal |
| **Nitric Acid** | NOx (NO, NO2) | N2O (potent GHG), NH3 | Nitrate-rich water | Spent Pt-Rh gauze | SCR + Extended Absorption |
| **Sulphuric Acid** | SO2 | SO3 / acid mist | Dilute acid | Spent V2O5 catalyst | DCDA, wet scrubber |
| **Urea** | NH3, CO2 | Urea dust | Process condensate | Carbamate residue | Hydrolyser stripper |
| **Phosphoric Acid** | Fluorides (HF, SiF4) | Particulate dust | P2O5-laden water | **Phosphogypsum (4.5 MT/MT P2O5)** | Wet scrubber, gypsum pond |
| **DAP / NPK** | NH3, Fluorides | HCl fume, dust | Phosphate + ammonia | Granulation fines | Cyclone + wet scrubber |
| **Complex Fertilisers** | NH3 | Particulates | Mixed nutrient liquor | Various | Bag filter + scrubber |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Fertiliser Industry Pollutant Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA[Raw Material Natural Gas Naphtha] --> processA[Ammonia Plant Steam Reformer]
    rawB[Raw Material Sulphur] --> processB[Sulphuric Acid Plant Contact Process]
    rawC[Raw Material Phosphate Rock] --> processC[Phosphoric Acid Plant Wet Process]
    rawD[Raw Material Ammonia CO2] --> processD[Urea Plant]
    processA --> air1[Air Pollutants CO2 NOx CO NH3]
    processA --> water1[Water Pollutants NH3 Cyanide]
    processA --> solid1[Solid Waste Spent Catalyst Slag]
    processB --> air2[Air Pollutants SO2 SO3 Acid Mist]
    processB --> solid2[Solid Waste Spent V2O5]
    processC --> air3[Air Pollutants HF SiF4 Particulate]
    processC --> water3[Water Pollutants P2O5 Fluoride]
    processC --> solid3[Solid Waste Phosphogypsum 4.5 MT per MT P2O5]
    processD --> air4[Air Pollutants NH3 CO2 Urea Dust]
    processD --> water4[Water Pollutants Process Condensate NH3 Urea]
    air1 --> ctl1[Control Technology MEA Scrubber SCR]
    air2 --> ctl2[Control Technology DCDA Wet Scrubber]
    air3 --> ctl3[Control Technology Wet Scrubber Cyclone]
    air4 --> ctl4[Control Technology Hydrolyser Stripper]
    water1 --> ctl5[Effluent Treatment Biological Stripping]
    water3 --> ctl6[Effluent Treatment Gypsum Pond Neutralisation]
    water4 --> ctl7[Effluent Treatment Hydrolysis Stripper]
    solid1 --> ctl8[Solid Disposal Secured Landfill]
    solid3 --> ctl9[Solid Disposal Gypsum Stack or Reuse Cement Industry]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pollutant Generation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Fertiliser Industry Pollutants]
    root --> air[Air Pollutants]
    root --> water[Water Pollutants]
    root --> solid[Solid Pollutants]
    air --> airA[Gaseous NOx SO2 NH3 CO2 HF]
    air --> airB[Particulate PM10 PM2.5 Urea Dust Phosphate Dust]
    water --> waterA[Inorganic NH3 Urea Nitrate Phosphate Fluoride]
    water --> waterB[Heavy Metals Cd Pb Hg Cr As]
    water --> waterC[Organic COD BOD Process Condensate]
    solid --> solidA[Process Waste Phosphogypsum Spent Catalyst Slag]
    solid --> solidB[Hazardous Waste Spent V2O5 Spent Pt-Rh Carbon Sludge]
    airA --> impactA[Environmental Impact Acid Rain Eutrophication Smog Global Warming]
    waterA --> impactB[Environmental Impact Eutrophication Aquatic Toxicity Bioaccumulation]
    solidA --> impactC[Environmental Impact Land Use Radioactive Contamination Ra226]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **linear flow** from raw material → process → pollutant → control technology. This mirrors how a **Consent to Operate** application is structured under the Water and Air Acts.
> - The second diagram shows the **hierarchical classification** of pollutants, which is the format KTU expects in 7-mark answer sub-parts.
> - Notice how **phosphogypsum** is highlighted as the single largest solid waste stream — at **4.5–5.5 MT per MT of P2O5 produced**, a 1000 TPD phosphoric acid plant generates **~5000 MT/day of phosphogypsum**, equivalent to the daily waste output of a small city.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

> List **any six major air pollutants** generated in fertiliser manufacturing processes and name one process that generates each.

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| S.No | Pollutant | Generating Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | NO / NO2 (NOx) | Nitric acid plant (Ostwald process) |
| 2 | SO2 | Sulphuric acid plant (Contact process) |
| 3 | NH3 slip | Urea plant (carbamate decomposer) |
| 4 | Fluorides (HF, SiF4) | Phosphoric acid plant (acidulation of rock) |
| 5 | CO2 | Ammonia plant (CO2 removal section) |
| 6 | Particulate dust | DAP / NPK plant (granulator / dryer) |

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Supplementary]`

> What is **phosphogypsum**? State its environmental significance and two methods of safe disposal or utilisation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Phosphogypsum (1 mark):** Phosphogypsum is the calcium sulphate dihydrate (CaSO4·2H2O) by-product generated when phosphate rock is acidulated with sulphuric acid in the wet-process manufacture of phosphoric acid. Approximately **4.5–5.5 tonnes** of phosphogypsum are produced per tonne of P2O5 manufactured.

**Environmental Significance (1 mark):**
- Contains trace radioactivity (Radium-226) from the phosphate rock.
- Generates acidic leachate if improperly stored, contaminating groundwater.
- Requires large land area for stack disposal (about 1 hectare per 100 TPD P2O5).

**Disposal / Utilisation Methods (1 mark):**
1. **Cement Industry Use** — as a retarder in cement clinker manufacture (replacing natural gypsum).
2. **Agricultural Use** — after purification, as a soil conditioner / sulphur fertiliser.
3. **Backfilling of abandoned mines.**
4. **Construction material** — in plaster of Paris, gypsum boards, and bricks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by listing pollutants without naming the **specific process unit** that generates them. Always pair the pollutant with the source (e.g., "NH3 slip from urea carbamate decomposer" — NOT just "NH3").
> - Students forget to mention **N2O** as a greenhouse gas from nitric acid plants — this is a frequently-missed 1-mark point.
> - In numerical problems, students often **mix up the molecular weights** of NO (30) and NO2 (46). Memorise: NO = 30, NO2 = 46, NH3 = 17, SO2 = 64, CO2 = 44.
> - Students present **NOx concentration in ppm** without converting to **mg/Nm³** using the ideal gas equation at STP — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2025 - Expected Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat flow diagrams, explain the major **air pollutants generated** in **(i) Ammonia plant**, **(ii) Nitric acid plant**, and **(iii) Phosphoric acid plant**. For each plant, identify **at least three pollutants** with their **source points** and **control technologies**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sulphuric acid plant burns **50 tonnes/day of sulphur** to produce H2SO4. The conversion efficiency of SO2 to SO3 in the converter is **98%**. The stack gas flow rate is **20,000 Nm³/hr** measured at STP. Calculate:
   (i) The mass of SO2 emitted per day in kg/day.
   (ii) The concentration of SO2 in the stack gas in mg/Nm³ and ppm (by volume).
   (iii) Whether the plant meets the CPCB emission standard of **400 mg/Nm³**.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Ammonia Plant Air Pollutants (2.5 marks):**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|
| CO2 | Shift converter & CO2 removal system | MDEA / Benfield absorption (recovered for urea feedstock) |
| NOx | Primary reformer furnace burners | Low-NOx burners, SCR |
| NH3 slip | Synthesis loop purge | Recovery as ammonium nitrate / urea |
| CO | Reformer flue gas | Complete combustion with excess O2 |
| H2S | Feed gas desulphuriser | ZnO adsorber beds |

**(ii) Nitric Acid Plant Air Pollutants (2.5 marks):**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|
| NO / NO2 | Converter tail gas (main stack) | Extended absorption + SCR |
| N2O | Converter catalyst bed | High-temperature thermal decomposition (secondary catalyst) |
| NH3 slip | Converter inlet (unreacted) | Optimised Pt-Rh gauze operation |
| Acid mist | Absorber & bleaching section | Mist eliminators / demisters |

**(iii) Phosphoric Acid Plant Air Pollutants (2 marks):**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|
| HF, SiF4 | Reaction tank & filter | Wet scrubber with silica recirculation |
| Particulate dust | Rock grinding, conveying | Bag filters / cyclones |
| Phosphate mist | Evaporator section | Mist eliminators |

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Sulphur burnt = 50 tonnes/day = 50,000 kg/day
- Conversion efficiency η = 98% = 0.98
- Stack gas flow Q = 20,000 Nm³/hr
- CPCB standard = 400 mg/Nm³

**Step 1 — Mass of SO2 theoretically produced from 50 tonnes of S**

Reaction: S + O2 → SO2

Molecular weight ratio:

$$\dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{\text{S}}} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2$$

Theoretical SO2 production:

$$m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{theoretical}} = 50{,}000 \times 2 = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of SO2 actually emitted (unconverted)**

Emitted SO2 fraction = (1 − η) = 1 − 0.98 = 0.02

$$m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{emitted}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.02 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Per-hour emission rate**

$$E_{\text{SO}_2} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{24} = 83.33 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Concentration in mg/Nm³**

$$C_{\text{SO}_2} = \dfrac{E_{\text{SO}_2} \times 10^6}{Q} = \dfrac{83.33 \times 10^6}{20{,}000} = 4{,}166.5 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Concentration in ppm (using ideal gas: 1 ppm = M/22.4 mg/Nm³)**

$$\text{ppm} = \dfrac{C}{M_{\text{SO}_2}/22.4} = \dfrac{4{,}166.5}{64/22.4} = \dfrac{4{,}166.5}{2.857} = 1{,}458.4 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 6 — Compliance Check**

$$C_{\text{actual}} = 4{,}166.5 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 > 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \text{ (CPCB limit)}$$

**Result:** The plant **DOES NOT comply** with CPCB norms. It must install a **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)** system to improve conversion to >99.7% and reduce SO2 emissions below 400 mg/Nm³.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **major water pollutants** generated in fertiliser industries. Discuss the characteristics, treatment methods, and **CPCB effluent discharge standards** for the following:
   (i) Ammonia plant process condensate
   (ii) Phosphoric acid plant gypsum pond water
   (iii) Nitric acid plant weak acid effluent

**(b) [7 Marks]** A phosphoric acid plant produces **300 MTPD of P2O5** by the wet process. Calculate:
   (i) The quantity of **phosphogypsum** generated per day (assume 5 MT gypsum per MT P2O5).
   (ii) The **fluoride emissions** in kg/day, if the fluoride generation factor is **0.04 kg F per kg P2O5** and 90% is recovered by wet scrubbing.
   (iii) The land area required for stacking the gypsum for **365 days** at a stack height of **10 metres** (assume gypsum bulk density = 1.5 tonnes/m³).

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Ammonia Plant Process Condensate (2.5 marks):**

- **Sources:** Condensate from CO2 regeneration, process steam drains, urea synthesis purge (if integrated).
- **Key pollutants:** NH3 (50–2000 mg/L), methanol, suspended solids, low BOD/COD.
- **Treatment:** Steam stripping → recovered NH3 recycled; biological polishing; multi-effect evaporation.
- **CPCB Standard (for fertiliser industry):** NH3 ≤ 30 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5, BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, COD ≤ 250 mg/L, SS ≤ 100 mg/L.

**(ii) Phosphoric Acid Plant Gypsum Pond Water (2.5 marks):**

- **Sources:** Wash water from gypsum filters, pond overflow.
- **Key pollutants:** P2O5 (0.5–2% as H3PO4), fluorides (800–2000 mg/L as F), low pH (1.5–3.0), suspended solids.
- **Treatment:** Lime neutralisation (pH to 6–7), fluoride precipitation as CaF2 or adsorption on activated alumina, settling in clarifier.
- **CPCB Standard:** Total P (as P) ≤ 5 mg/L, fluoride ≤ 2 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5, SS ≤ 100 mg/L.

**(iii) Nitric Acid Plant Weak Acid Effluent (2 marks):**

- **Sources:** Absorber weak acid stream, bleaching section condensate.
- **Key pollutants:** HNO3 (1–5%), nitrates (5000–15000 mg/L), fluorides (trace).
- **Treatment:** Alkaline neutralisation with lime or caustic soda; biological denitrification (rare in industry); recycling back to absorption tower.
- **CPCB Standard:** Total N as NO3 ≤ 10 mg/L (drinking water), pH 6.5–8.5.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- P2O5 production = 300 MTPD
- Gypsum factor = 5 MT per MT P2O5
- Fluoride generation factor = 0.04 kg F per kg P2O5
- Scrubber recovery = 90%
- Stack height = 10 m
- Bulk density of gypsum = 1.5 tonnes/m³
- Period = 365 days

**(i) Phosphogypsum Generated per Day (2 marks):**

$$M_{\text{gypsum, daily}} = 300 \times 5 = 1{,}500 \text{ MT/day}$$

**(ii) Fluoride Emissions per Day (3 marks):**

Total fluoride generated:

$$F_{\text{generated}} = 0.04 \times 300{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg F/day}$$

Fluoride emitted (after 90% recovery = 10% emitted):

$$F_{\text{emitted}} = 12{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}200 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iii) Land Area Required for 365-Day Gypsum Stack (2 marks):**

Annual gypsum generated:

$$M_{\text{gypsum, annual}} = 1{,}500 \times 365 = 547{,}500 \text{ MT/year}$$

Volume of stack (at bulk density 1.5 t/m³):

$$V_{\text{stack}} = \dfrac{M}{\rho} = \dfrac{547{,}500}{1.5} = 365{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

At stack height h = 10 m:

$$\text{Land area} A = \dfrac{V}{h} = \dfrac{365{,}000}{10} = 36{,}500 \text{ m}^2 = 3.65 \text{ hectares}$$

This massive land requirement (~3.65 hectares/year per plant) is why **phosphogypsum utilisation** is a national priority under the **Department of Fertilizers** policy framework.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Major Pollutants Generated in Fertiliser Manufacturing**

- [ ] **Ammonia Plant** → CO2 (largest GHG), NOx, CO, NH3 slip, cyanide in effluent, spent Ni catalyst.
- [ ] **Nitric Acid Plant** → NOx (NO + NO2), N2O (GWP = 298), NH3 slip, weak acid effluent.
- [ ] **Sulphuric Acid Plant** → SO2 (dominant), SO3 / acid mist, spent V2O5 catalyst.
- [ ] **Urea Plant** → NH3 vent, CO2 vent, urea dust, process condensate (NH3 + urea).
- [ ] **Phosphoric Acid Plant** → HF / SiF4, particulate dust, **phosphogypsum (4.5–5.5 MT/MT P2O5)**, Cd / Pb / Hg traces, radioactive Ra-226.
- [ ] **DAP / NPK Plant** → NH3 slip, fluorides, dust, HCl fume.
- [ ] **Key Emission Factors to Memorise:** CO2 = 1.73 t/tNH3; Fluoride = 0.04 kg F/kg P2O5; NOx (modern plant) ≈ 0.2–0.5 kg NO2/tHNO3 after abatement.
- [ ] **CPCB Standards (must remember):** NOx (as NO2) ≤ 400 mg/Nm³; SO2 ≤ 400 mg/Nm³; NH3 (stack) ≤ 175 mg/Nm³; Fluoride (stack) ≤ 25 mg/Nm³; PM ≤ 150 mg/Nm³.
- [ ] **Conversion Formula to remember:** ppm (vol) → mg/Nm³ = ppm × M / 22.4 (at STP).
- [ ] **Control Technologies:** SCR for NOx; DCDA for SO2; Wet scrubber for HF; Hydrolyser-stripper for urea effluent; Gypsum stack + cement reuse for phosphogypsum.
- [ ] **Statutory Framework:** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Strategy:** In any KTU 2024 question on "major pollutants," always structure your answer in **four blocks**: (1) Pollutant list with source point, (2) Stoichiometric / numerical basis, (3) Health and environmental impact, (4) Control technology. Examiners reward structured answers with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution Control Measures in Fertiliser Industry
Pollution Prevention Strategies in Fertiliser Industry
Environmental Standards Applicable to Fertiliser Industry
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Standards Applicable to Fertiliser Industry

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Standards** in the context of the fertilizer industry are a structured set of legally enforceable quantitative limits, qualitative norms, procedural protocols, and permissible thresholds that govern the discharge of pollutants into the four environmental media — **air, water, land, and noise** — from fertilizer manufacturing, processing, storage, and transportation facilities. These standards are issued by competent regulatory bodies such as the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)**, the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC)**, and internationally by agencies like the **World Bank Group / IFC (International Finance Corporation)** and the **US Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Examination Ready):**
> *"Environmental standards for the fertiliser industry constitute a regulatory framework comprising emission limits for gaseous effluents (NH₃, SO₂, NOₓ, HF, PM, urea dust), discharge norms for liquid effluents (BOD, COD, TSS, total nitrogen, total phosphorus, fluorides, heavy metals), noise level benchmarks, and solid/hazardous waste handling protocols, designed to ensure that industrial activity does not exceed the assimilative capacity of the surrounding ecosystem."*

The principal Indian legislative instruments that empower these standards are:

| Statute | Year | Core Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act** | **1974** | Control of water pollution & consent to establish/operate |
| **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act** | **1981** | Control of air pollution & emission standards |
| **Environment (Protection) Act (EPA)** | **1986** | Umbrella Act for all environmental matters |
| **Hazardous Waste Management Rules** | **2016 (amended 2022)** | Handling of chemical/reaction waste |
| **Solid Waste Management Rules** | **2016** | Disposal of non-hazardous solid waste |
| **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC)** | **1989** | Safety in chemical industry |
| **Public Liability Insurance Act** | **1991** | Compensation for industrial accidents |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Layer Safety Net" Model

Imagine a tall apartment building (the **fertilizer plant**) located next to a river (**surrounding ecosystem**) and a residential colony (**neighboring community**).

- **Layer 1 — Air Standards** = Like ensuring the chimney smoke does not choke the neighbors. Specific gases (NH₃, SO₂, fluorides) are limited to safe concentrations.
- **Layer 2 — Water Standards** = Like ensuring the waste-water pipe never lets toxic water reach the river or the colony's wells. BOD, COD, nitrogen, and fluoride limits are checked at the point of discharge.
- **Layer 3 — Land & Noise Standards** = Like ensuring the building's garbage is segregated and its noisy generator does not disturb sleep. Solid waste is categorized, treated, and noise is capped at decibel limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Standards are Critical for Fertilizer Industry:**
> Fertilizer plants handle **highly reactive chemicals** — ammonia, sulfuric acid, nitric acid, urea, phosphoric acid, and HF. A single emission excursion can cause **acid rain, eutrophication, fluorosis, or smog formation**. Standards transform "responsible behaviour" into a **measurable, auditable, and legally binding** obligation.

## 1.3 Key Pollutants from Fertilizer Industry at a Glance

The fertilizer industry emits a characteristic pollutant profile:

- **Nitrogenous Fertilizer Plants (Urea, Ammonia, Ammonium Nitrate):** NH₃, NOₓ, urea dust, CO₂, particulate matter.
- **Phosphate Fertilizer Plants (SSP, TSP, DAP, MAP):** Fluorides (HF, SiF₄), phosphogypsum, particulate matter, SO₂.
- **Sulphuric Acid Plants (used in phosphate processing):** SO₂, SO₃, acid mist.
- **Mixed/Complex Fertilizer Plants:** A combination of all the above plus heavy metals (Cd, Pb, Hg, As) as trace contaminants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Standards Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Architecture of Indian Environmental Regulation

Environmental standards in India follow a **three-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Statutory Tier** — Acts of Parliament (Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986) define the legal powers of the boards.
2. **Regulatory Tier** — Rules, Schedules, Notifications issued by MoEF&CC and the Boards (e.g., EPA Notification G.S.R. 176(E) on fertilizer industry emissions).
3. **Operational Tier** — Consent conditions, EIA conditions, and ISO 14001 / OHSAS 45001 / ISO 45001 management system requirements imposed on individual units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Questions on environmental standards often ask *"Discuss the various statutory and regulatory mechanisms applicable to fertilizer plants."* A three-tier answer covering **Statute → Rules → Site-specific Consent** always scores full marks.

## 2.2 Air Quality Standards (CAA — Clean Air Act Analogue in India)

### 2.2.1 Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS) — India (CPCB, 2009)

These are **ground-level concentration limits** that the plant must maintain in its surrounding zone, monitored over 24-hour and annual averages. They are universal across industries, including fertilizer plants.

$$ C_{\text{measured}} \leq C_{\text{standard}} \quad \forall \, t \in \{24\text{-hr},\; \text{Annual}\} $$

where $C_{\text{measured}}$ is the observed ambient concentration and $C_{\text{standard}}$ is the CPCB-prescribed limit in $\mu g/m^3$ or $ng/m^3$.

### 2.2.2 Source (Stack / Emission) Standards for Fertilizer Industry

Unlike ambient standards, emission standards apply **at the discharge point (stack)** and use units like **mg/Nm³** (milligrams per normal cubic meter). They are **technology-forcing** — newer plants face stricter norms.

**The 3-M (Mass / Maximum / Monitoring) Rule** for KTU:
- **M**ass rate limit (kg/hr) for high-flow sources
- **M**aximum concentration limit (mg/Nm³) for the plume
- Continuous **M**onitoring (CEMS) for parameters like SO₂, NOₓ, PM

### 2.2.3 Stack Height Formula (A Critical KTU Formula)

For a fertilizer plant emitting gaseous pollutants, the **minimum stack height** is governed by the formula:

$$ H = 14 \, (Q_h)^{0.25} \quad \text{...(for Q}_h \leq 10^4 \text{ kg/hr)} $$

$$ H = 14 \, (Q_h)^{0.25} - 1.5 \, (Q_h)^{0.25}\cdot 10^{-3} \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{...(with gas temperature correction)} $$

where:
- $H$ = effective stack height in **metres**
- $Q_h$ = mass emission rate of SO₂ equivalent in **kg/hr**
- $\Delta T$ = difference between exit gas temperature and ambient temperature in **°C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world interpretation:** A taller stack ensures that pollutants are discharged into faster, more turbulent air layers, improving dispersion and reducing ground-level concentration. The exponent **0.25** is derived from atmospheric dispersion theory (Briggs plume rise) and reflects the **fourth-root dependence** on emission rate.

## 2.3 Water Quality Standards

### 2.3.1 Inland Surface Water Standards (IS: 2296)

Fertilizer plant effluents must meet the following before discharge into inland surface waters:

| Parameter | Symbol/Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand | $\text{BOD}_5$ | 5-day oxygen demand at 20°C | mg/L |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand | $\text{COD}$ | Oxidation strength by dichromate | mg/L |
| Total Suspended Solids | $\text{TSS}$ | Filterable residue after drying at 103–105°C | mg/L |
| Total Nitrogen | $\text{TN}$ | $\text{NH}_3 + \text{NO}_2^- + \text{NO}_3^- + \text{Org.N}$ | mg/L |
| Total Phosphorus | $\text{TP}$ | $\text{PO}_4^{3-} \text{ (as P)}$ | mg/L |
| Fluoride | $\text{F}^-$ | Ionic fluoride | mg/L |
| pH | $-\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Hydrogen ion activity | — |
| Free Ammonia | $\text{NH}_3$ | Un-ionized form | mg/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why BOD/COD matter in fertilizer context:** Fertilizer effluents contain urea, ammonia, and organic byproducts. If discharged untreated, they cause **oxygen depletion in receiving waters**, killing aquatic life (the classic **eutrophication** chain). High BOD → low dissolved oxygen → fish mortality.

### 2.3.2 The General Standards for Discharge of Effluents (EPA Schedule VI)

For fertilizer industry, key limits include:
- **pH:** 5.5 to 9.0
- **BOD₅:** ≤ 100 mg/L (for land-based discharge via irrigated fields)
- **COD:** ≤ 250 mg/L
- **TSS:** ≤ 100 mg/L
- **Ammoniacal Nitrogen (as N):** ≤ 50 mg/L
- **Total Nitrogen (as N):** ≤ 100 mg/L
- **Free Ammonia (as NH₃):** ≤ 5 mg/L
- **Total Phosphorus (as P):** ≤ 5 mg/L
- **Fluoride (as F⁻):** ≤ 10 mg/L
- **Sulphides (as S):** ≤ 2 mg/L

### 2.3.3 The Key Quantitative Relationship (KTU-Critical)

The **BOD/COD ratio** indicates biodegradability of an effluent:

$$ \eta_{\text{bio}} = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}} $$

- $\eta_{\text{bio}} > 0.5$ → **biologically treatable** by conventional activated sludge
- $0.3 < \eta_{\text{bio}} < 0.5$ → requires **advanced / modified biological treatment**
- $\eta_{\text{bio}} < 0.3$ → effluent is **toxic or refractory**; needs physico-chemical or advanced oxidation

## 2.4 Solid & Hazardous Waste Standards

### 2.4.1 Phosphogypsum — A Defining Fertilizer Waste

Phosphogypsum is produced as a byproduct of phosphoric acid manufacture by the wet process. Per the EPA notification, it is classified as a **non-hazardous waste** if it meets radiation norms. It must be stored in **lined stacks** to prevent leachate of fluorides, sulphates, and heavy metals into groundwater.

### 2.4.2 Spent Catalyst & Contaminated Oil

Spent catalyst from ammonia/urea plants and used oil from compressors are classified under **Schedule I / II of Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2022)** and must be sent to **registered re-processors / recyclers** only.

## 2.5 Noise Standards

Noise is regulated under the **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000** and ambient noise standards are:

| Zone Type | Day (6 AM – 10 PM) dB(A) | Night (10 PM – 6 AM) dB(A) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | 75 | 70 |
| Commercial | 65 | 55 |
| Residential | 55 | 45 |
| Silence Zone (hospitals, schools) | 50 | 40 |

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Standards Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Standard | Variable Definitions | Application / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stack height | $H = 14 (Q_h)^{0.25}$ m | $Q_h$ = SO₂-equivalent emission rate (kg/hr) | Minimum height of fertilizer plant stacks |
| 2 | BOD/COD ratio | $\eta_{\text{bio}} = \text{BOD}_5 / \text{COD}$ | Ratio of biological to chemical oxygen demand | Decides type of treatment plant |
| 3 | Noise limit (industrial) | $L_{\text{day}} = 75\,\text{dB(A)}$ | Daytime industrial zone limit | DG set / compressor house boundary |
| 4 | Effluent pH range | $5.5 \leq \text{pH} \leq 9.0$ | Hydrogen ion activity window | ETP outlet compliance |
| 5 | Fluoride discharge limit | $[\text{F}^-] \leq 10\,\text{mg/L}$ | Ionic fluoride concentration | Phosphate fertilizer effluent |
| 6 | Free ammonia limit | $[\text{NH}_3] \leq 5\,\text{mg/L}$ | Un-ionized ammonia | Urea/Ammonium nitrate plant |
| 7 | Mass-based emission | $E_{\text{mass}} = C \cdot Q \cdot 10^{-6}$ (kg/hr) | $C$ in mg/Nm³, $Q$ in Nm³/hr | Stack emission inventory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pattern reminder for tables:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been avoided inside cells; the absolute-value-style expressions have been rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation or in plain text. This is the KTU Board safe syntax.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Stack Height Calculation for a Sulphuric Acid Plant

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A sulphuric acid plant in a fertilizer complex emits SO₂ at the rate of **800 kg/hr** from its acid mist vent. The gas exit temperature is **180 °C** and the ambient temperature is **30 °C**. Determine the minimum effective stack height as per CPCB norms.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**

$$ Q_h = 800 \, \text{kg/hr}, \quad T_{\text{exit}} = 180^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_{\text{ambient}} = 30^\circ\text{C} $$

**Step 2 — Calculate the temperature difference**

$$ \Delta T = T_{\text{exit}} - T_{\text{ambient}} = 180 - 30 = 150^\circ\text{C} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the uncorrected stack height** using the basic CPCB equation:

$$ H_0 = 14 \, (Q_h)^{0.25} $$

$$ H_0 = 14 \times (800)^{0.25} $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $(800)^{0.25}$**

$$ (800)^{0.25} = (800)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{800}} = \sqrt{28.284} = 5.318 $$

**Step 5 — Substitute back**

$$ H_0 = 14 \times 5.318 = 74.45 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 6 — Apply the temperature correction term**

$$ \Delta H = 1.5 \times (Q_h)^{0.25} \times 10^{-3} \times \Delta T $$

$$ \Delta H = 1.5 \times 5.318 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 $$

$$ \Delta H = 1.196 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 7 — Calculate the final effective stack height**

$$ H = H_0 - \Delta H $$

$$ H = 74.45 - 1.196 \approx 73.25 \, \text{m} $$

**Step 8 — Statement of result**

> **Minimum effective stack height required ≈ 73.25 m (rounded up to 74 m in practice).**

**Interpretation:** The temperature correction reduces the height slightly because a hotter gas already has natural buoyancy (plume rise), requiring a slightly less tall structure to achieve the same ground-level dilution.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Effluent Compliance Check for a Urea Plant

**Problem Statement:**
A urea plant discharges a treated effluent stream with the following characteristics. The stream is to be discharged into an inland surface water body. Determine the parameters that fail EPA Schedule VI standards.

| Parameter | Measured Value | EPA Standard |
|---|---|---|
| pH | 8.2 | 5.5 – 9.0 |
| BOD₅ | 85 mg/L | 100 mg/L |
| COD | 280 mg/L | 250 mg/L |
| TSS | 90 mg/L | 100 mg/L |
| Free NH₃ (as N) | 6.5 mg/L | 5 mg/L |
| Fluoride | 8 mg/L | 10 mg/L |

**Step 1 — Compare each parameter against its standard**

**pH:** $5.5 \leq 8.2 \leq 9.0$ → **PASS** ✓
**BOD₅:** $85 < 100$ → **PASS** ✓
**COD:** $280 > 250$ → **FAIL** ✗
**TSS:** $90 < 100$ → **PASS** ✓
**Free NH₃:** $6.5 > 5$ → **FAIL** ✗
**Fluoride:** $8 < 10$ → **PASS** ✓

**Step 2 — Compute biodegradability index**

$$ \eta_{\text{bio}} = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{\text{COD}} = \frac{85}{280} = 0.304 $$

**Step 3 — Interpret the index**

$$ 0.3 < \eta_{\text{bio}} = 0.304 < 0.5 $$

This places the effluent in the **"modified biological treatment required"** category.

**Step 4 — Recommendation**

The plant must install an **advanced biological treatment** (e.g., Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor — MBBR, or Sequencing Batch Reactor — SBR) with **breakpoint chlorination** to bring free NH₃ below 5 mg/L, plus an **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)** such as Fenton's oxidation to reduce COD below 250 mg/L.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Compliance Matrix — Fertilizer Plant Unit-wise

| Plant Unit | Key Pollutants | Primary Standard | Limit Value | Measurement Point | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ammonia Plant Reformer** | NOₓ, CO | Stack emission | NOₓ ≤ 200 mg/Nm³ | Reformer stack | Online CEMS |
| **Urea Prilling Tower** | NH₃, urea dust | Stack + ambient | NH₃ ≤ 50 mg/Nm³, dust ≤ 50 mg/Nm³ | Prill tower top | Continuous |
| **Sulphuric Acid Plant** | SO₂, acid mist | EPA notification | SO₂ ≤ 4 kg/tonne acid | Tail gas stack | Online CEMS |
| **Phosphoric Acid Plant** | HF, SiF₄, fluorides | Stack + effluent | HF ≤ 5 mg/Nm³, F⁻ ≤ 10 mg/L in water | Digester vent + ETP | 24-hr composite |
| **DAP / NPK Plant** | Particulate matter, NH₃, F⁻ | EPA notification | PM ≤ 150 mg/Nm³ at bag filter outlet | Granulator stack | Online |
| **Cooling Tower Blowdown** | TDS, chromates, biocides | EPA Schedule VI | TDS ≤ 2100 mg/L, Cr⁶⁺ ≤ 0.1 mg/L | Cooling tower outlet | Daily |
| **Boilers (Coal/Gas)** | PM, SO₂, NOₓ | EPA Stack Regulations | PM ≤ 100 mg/Nm³, SO₂ ≤ 600 mg/Nm³ | Boiler stack | Online |

---

## 3.4 Conceptual Derivation — The Logic Behind "Why" Standards Exist

The **assimilative capacity** of a receiving environment is mathematically expressed as:

$$ C_{\text{assimilative}} = C_{\text{background}} + \frac{\dot{M}_{\text{emission}}}{Q_{\text{receiving}} + Q_{\text{dilution}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{decay constant term}} $$

where:
- $C_{\text{assimilative}}$ = maximum concentration the environment can absorb without harm
- $C_{\text{background}}$ = existing concentration in receiving media
- $\dot{M}_{\text{emission}}$ = pollutant mass emission rate
- $Q_{\text{receiving}}$ = volumetric flow of receiving water/air

> **Core Idea:** Standards are designed to keep $C_{\text{downstream}} \leq C_{\text{assimilative}}$. If discharge exceeds this, **ecological damage** results — fish kills (in water), smog (in air), and fluorosis (in soil/groundwater near phosphate plants).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
